# Chapter Eight: Low Tide, Again He came home from the hospital after four days, weaker than before, refusing to admit it, already talking about getting the boat's post fixed before the insurance adjuster could come look at it and lowball him. I let him talk. I'd learned that much, at least, these past weeks — that letting my father talk about the boat was sometimes the only way he had of talking about anything else. The structures are still there. I want to say that plainly, because if I don't say it plainly I'll be tempted to make it into something it isn't. They didn't leave. They didn't do anything further, not in any way you could point to on a chart or a seismograph. The Halifax broadcasts stopped calling it a crisis sometime in the third month and started calling it a "new baseline," which is the kind of phrase institutions reach for once they've run out of better ones. Del says the response office is being scaled back, that Priya's been reassigned to a coastline in Newfoundland where apparently something similar started up in the last few weeks, and he says it like it's gossip, because to Del, by now, it mostly is. I still work the shoreline stations some mornings, though there's less to log than there was. The die-off has thinned to something almost ordinary — a few fish some days, none on others, no worse, statistically, than a bad algae season, if you didn't know what a bad algae season used to look like before any of this started. Most of what washes up now decomposes the way it always did. Most. Not all. I keep one in a jar in my kitchen, which I've never told Priya about and never will, a small perch that came up on the tideline six weeks ago looking exactly the way that first pollock had — too firm, too fresh, eyes clear — and that has simply not changed since. Not decayed. Not decomposed. Not, as far as I can tell, alive in any sense a biologist would recognize, but also not properly dead. I look at it some mornings with my coffee and I don't have a word for what it is, and I've stopped trying to find one, which is its own kind of surrender, I suppose, though it doesn't feel like defeat exactly. It feels more like living next to weather you've stopped expecting to explain. I never got a clean version of what my father heard out there, or what I heard at the point that first night, or what any of it actually meant, and I've come to understand that I'm not going to. I've told this story now to enough people — Priya, once, off the record, over the phone; a counselor the province offered for free that I went to exactly twice; Del, badly, over beers, more than once — that I can feel it changing shape a little each time, smoothing at the edges the way a stone does in a tide, and I no longer entirely trust my own memory of that night against the memory of having told the story about that night. Maybe there were words. Maybe *we are sorry for the water* is something I built afterward out of the two truest words I actually caught — *sorry* and *water* — and stitched a sentence around them because a sentence was easier to carry than a feeling with no shape. My father and I don't talk about what happened on the boat. We came close, once, the week after he got home, both of us in his kitchen with dinner going cold between us, and he started to say something about her — my mother — and stopped himself, and I let him stop, because I understood by then that some doors, once you've seen what's behind them, don't need opening again to be real. What we do now is smaller than reconciliation and I think, honestly, more durable than it. He calls some Sundays. I go by with groceries some Wednesdays, and we argue about nothing — insurance forms, the boat, whether I'm getting enough sleep — and neither of us says the large things, but the large things sit in the room with us anyway, acknowledged, unspoken, the way you'd acknowledge a piece of furniture that's always been there. This morning I drove down to the point before work, the way I still do sometimes, though I've never again felt what I felt that first night, not once, in all the times I've stood there since. The tide was low, properly low, ordinary low, and there was a scatter of fish along the wet sand, most of them gulls-picked already, none of them arranged in any pattern I could make myself believe was deliberate. Except one. Near the waterline, small, silver, its eyes too clear. I crouched by it a long time, longer than I needed to, coffee going cold in my other hand the way it always seems to out here, and the structure sat where it always sat, dark and patient against a grey morning sky, having said, as far as any of us could tell, the only thing it had ever intended to say, and having nothing further to add. I didn't bag the fish. I didn't log it. I left it there on the sand, half in the water, half out, neither one thing nor the other, and walked back up to my truck, and drove to work, and let the tide decide, on its own time, what to do with it.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is a closing chapter that functions as a deliberate anti-resolution, and it largely succeeds on its own terms. The writing plan called for a retrospective, unreliable narrator mode, refusal of neat symmetry, and an ending that rhymes with Chapter 1 without resolving it. The chapter delivers all of this with considerable skill. The prose voice is controlled and distinctive. The narrator's scientific register surfaces naturally ("not decayed. Not decomposed. Not, as far as I can tell, alive in any sense a biologist would recognize") without feeling like a tic being performed for the reader. The self-awareness about memory and storytelling — "I can feel it changing shape a little each time, smoothing at the edges the way a stone does in a tide" — is handled with genuine sophistication. The stone-in-tide metaphor is earned rather than decorative; it connects to the coastal setting and to the narrator's professional relationship with physical processes. The jar of perch in the kitchen is an excellent detail — specific, strange, domestic, and genuinely uncanny without being overwrought. It does real work: it shows Mara's continued scientific engagement, her secrecy from Priya, and the way the extraordinary has been absorbed into the ordinary. The line "living next to weather you've stopped expecting to explain" is one of the chapter's best — it's precise and resonant without straining. The reconstruction of what was heard — "maybe *we are sorry for the water* is something I built afterward out of the two truest words I actually caught" — is the chapter's most ambitious moment and it mostly lands. It honors the plan's commitment to keeping the alien communication genuinely ambiguous while also giving the reader something emotionally coherent to hold. The italicized phrase is well-chosen: *sorry* and *water* are the two words that would matter most to a fishing community facing a die-off, and the suggestion that Mara stitched a sentence around them is psychologically true. The father-daughter relationship is handled with appropriate restraint. "Some doors, once you've seen what's behind them, don't need opening again to be real" is a strong line. The description of their current relationship — "smaller than reconciliation and I think, honestly, more durable than it" — is exactly the kind of honest, unsentimental observation the plan called for. It avoids the trap of false resolution while still conveying genuine emotional movement. The final scene at the point is well-constructed. The fish with clear eyes, the cold coffee, the structure "having said, as far as any of us could tell, the only thing it had ever intended to say, and having nothing further to add" — this is restrained and effective. The final action (leaving the fish for the tide to decide) is a good closing image that refuses both the scientific impulse to log and categorize and the emotional impulse to make meaning. Weaknesses: The chapter is almost entirely tell rather than show in its first half. The summary of what has happened — Tomas home from hospital, the Halifax broadcasts, Del's gossip about Priya — is competent but reads as narrated summary rather than dramatized scene. The plan called for a "low-key, unglamorous scene" in the kitchen, and while that scene is referenced, it's summarized rather than rendered. The chapter's emotional weight rests heavily on the final scene at the point, which is brief. The middle section, while well-written, is essentially a series of reflective paragraphs that tell us where things stand rather than showing us. The dialogue is minimal to nonexistent — this is a deliberate choice for a closing chapter, but it means the "Weak Dialogue" metric is essentially not tested. The characters are consistent with their profiles: Tomas talking about the boat as a way of talking about everything else, Mara's scientific register, Del as gossip clearinghouse. The chapter follows its plan closely: the retrospective unreliable narrator mode, the rhyme with Chapter 1 (dockside, dead fish), the refusal of resolution, the structures remaining on the horizon. The "something is unresolved, someone doesn't say the thing they meant to say" instruction is honored. The plan's instruction that "ordinary life closes back over the extraordinary without fully absorbing it" is the chapter's central achievement. One minor concern: the prose occasionally tips toward the slightly too-polished for a narrator who has just described her own unreliability. The self-awareness is sophisticated but sometimes feels more like a skilled author's voice than Mara's specifically. This is a small complaint against an otherwise well-sustained voice. Overall, this is a strong closing chapter that honors its plan's ambitions and delivers genuine emotional and thematic coherence without false resolution. The writing is controlled, the details are specific and earned, and the refusal of uplift feels principled rather than merely fashionable. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 10 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of speculative fiction that demonstrates genuine literary craft. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. The story follows Mara, a marine biologist/water sampler, through the aftermath of alien megastructures arriving offshore and killing the fish. The narrative is told in retrospect with a confessional, first-person voice that feels earned rather than affected. The central tension is not really about the aliens at all — it's about Mara's fractured relationship with her father, her grief over her mother, and her identity as someone who left and came back. CHARACTER: Mara is exceptionally well-drawn. Her habit of "naming things" as a coping mechanism, her clinical detachment as emotional armor, her inability to call her father, her shame at not writing down the experience — these are consistent, specific, and psychologically true. Tomas (the father) is rendered with impressive economy: the passive-aggressive voicemails, the beer cans on the rail as "witnesses," his going out to the water to hear his dead wife. Del functions as a foil and emotional pressure valve with appropriate restraint. The characters behave believably throughout, including the father's refusal to discuss the supernatural experience in clinical terms ("don't make it into a science thing"). DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the story's strongest elements. It's spare, indirect, and loaded with subtext. "Mine's terrible tonight. Burned the bottom of the pot" doing the work of reconciliation. The father's "Never do" in response to the text. These feel like real people talking around things rather than at them. The compensation meeting dialogue is particularly good — the fisheries official repeating "it's documented" without being able to say when is a sharp, realistic detail. PACING: The story moves well across eight chapters. The first contact moment in Chapter 3 is appropriately placed — not too early, not too late. The hospital sequence in Chapter 7 provides the emotional climax. Chapter 8's resolution is quiet and earned. The pacing occasionally slows in the middle chapters (4 and 5) where the radio-report exposition and the porch argument feel slightly more mechanical, but these are minor issues. WORLD BUILDING: The world-building is restrained and effective. The structures are never over-explained. The "eighty cents on the dollar" compensation, the Christmas banner still up, the co-op forms — these ground the extraordinary in the mundane with skill. The detail that different people receive different sensory experiences from the contact event is genuinely interesting and scientifically plausible-feeling. THE CONTACT MOMENT: Chapter 3 is the story's centerpiece and it largely succeeds. "We are sorry for the water" arriving as a pre-formed memory rather than heard language is a genuinely creative approach to first contact. The author wisely has Mara immediately question whether she's constructed the sentence afterward — this epistemic humility is both realistic and thematically resonant. The warmth of the sand, the bruise-colored water, the gulls stopping — these details accumulate without becoming overwrought. METAPHOR AND POETRY: The metaphors are generally earned. "A long itemized list of the dead, tallied out along the sand" is excellent. "Fear wearing a lab coat" is sharp. "A door left unlocked" for the father's text message works. Some metaphors are slightly overworked — "the specific guilt-anger combination" and similar constructions appear frequently enough to become a tic of the narrator's voice (which is arguably intentional, given her self-described tic of naming things). The "stone smoothing in a tide" metaphor for memory in Chapter 8 is perhaps the most conventional moment in an otherwise inventive piece. FORCED POETRY: There are moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard. "The specific shame of a scientist caught doing something entirely unscientific" is good, but the story has many "specific [noun] of [situation]" constructions that begin to feel formulaic by Chapter 5. This is a real stylistic tic that occasionally tips from voice into mannerism. UNEARNED TRANSFORMATIONS: The father-daughter reconciliation is handled with admirable restraint — it's explicitly not a reconciliation, just "a door left unlocked." The story resists the temptation to make the alien contact catalyze a neat emotional resolution. The ending, with Mara leaving the fish on the sand, is appropriately ambiguous. Nothing feels unearned because the story is careful not to claim too much. PURPLE PROSE: Largely avoided. The prose is controlled and precise. The closest it comes is in Chapter 3's contact sequence, but even there the author pulls back from full lyricism with Mara's analytical voice reasserting itself. TELL-DON'T-SHOW: Minimal. The story shows rather than tells with impressive consistency. The father's grief for his wife is never stated — it's shown through his night trips past the buoy line. Mara's guilt about leaving during her mother's illness is shown through the "you left when it mattered" line landing without explanation. COHERENCE: The story is highly coherent. The fish-in-a-jar in Chapter 8 callbacks to the pollock in Chapter 1. The "naming things" motif runs throughout. The structure's silence and patience is consistent. The ending returns to the beach, the low tide, the fish — completing the circle. CREATIVITY: The approach to first contact — through dying fish, through chemical/biological signaling, through a message that arrives as pre-formed memory and may be partially constructed by the receiver — is genuinely original. The story's refusal to explain the structures or their purpose is a confident artistic choice. WEAKNESSES: The radio-report chapter (Chapter 4) is the weakest, functioning more as exposition delivery than narrative. The Sheila Boudreau character is slightly too convenient as a foil. The "specific [noun] of [situation]" construction becomes noticeable. The story's emotional arc (estranged daughter, grieving father, dead mother) is not itself surprising, even if its execution is excellent. Overall, this is a sophisticated, emotionally intelligent piece of literary science fiction that uses the alien contact premise as a lens for examining grief, estrangement, and the limits of scientific rationalism. Its restraint is its greatest strength. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Pacing: 16 World Building: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 82.8/100
+ Darkly Comic British YA Fiction — Midnight Blood (Chapter Avg: 80.9 | Final: 82.4)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Darkly Comic British YA Fiction Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Darkly Comic British YA Fiction Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸 -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming Setting: Comprehensive school in a grim, damp English town. Protagonist: 15-year-old narrator, sardonic, self-deprecating, obsessed with true crime podcasts and convinced the world is ending in small, specific ways rather than big dramatic ones. Best friend: chaotic, overconfident, drags them into trouble. Possible characters: - Nell (narrator) – deadpan, catastrophizing, unreliable in a comic way (assumes murder before mundane explanation) - Priya or Dev (best friend) – the instigator, treats every crisis as content - The texter: could be a sibling, an ex-friend, a mysterious new number, or a group chat gone wrong - Mum – overworked, oblivious, obsessed with her own drama (affair? diet culture? amateur dramatics society) - A teacher, Mr. Okafor, who becomes accidentally embroiled - Rival mean-girl figure, Saskia, whose blood emojis might be a prank - A dog named Colin who may or may not be responsible for actual blood Possible plot directions for the blood emoji text: 1. It's literally about period products — a friend group chat about needing tampons, narrator misreads as murder threat, comic escalation to a "conspiracy" that unravels into absurdity, mundane truth is the twist. Dark comedy through inflated stakes over nothing sinister but dealing with body horror/embarrassment/awkward puberty themes with a genuinely dark edge (bullying, misogyny, shame). 2. It's about an actual crime — someone's older sibling in trouble, drug debt, a "someone got hurt" situation — genuinely darker but still handled with British YA absurdist tone (Skulduggery Pleasant/early Malorie Blackman mix). 3. Combination: starts as a joke/misunderstanding about period stuff, but spirals into uncovering something genuinely dark in the background (an adult in the community, a missing pet, a black-market thing, a local urban legend "The Butcher of Ling Road"). 4. Blood emoji is code within a friend group for a dare/initiation game gone very wrong — like blood pact / secret society at school, escalates into something properly sinister by the end (a mock ritual that goes wrong, someone actually gets hurt). Tone: darkly comic British YA — think mix of Louise Rennison irreverence with a Malorie Blackman-ish edge of real stakes, some Skulduggery Pleasant snark, occasional true dread. Should have gallows humor, class-conscious jokes, small-town claustrophobia, self-aware narrator commentary, footnote-y asides. Structure over 8 chapters (~1000 words each = 8000 words total): Ch1: Midnight text arrives — 🩸🩸🩸. Establish Nell's voice, paranoid true-crime brain, best friend Priya's group chat "The Coven" (all girls in year 10 who share period trackers/emotional support). Immediate false alarm suspicion: someone's dying, is it Priya, is it about the missing cat, etc. End chapter on Nell sneaking out to investigate. Ch2: Recap of context/backstory — establish social dynamics, the Coven, Saskia beef, the missing cat (Colin — wait use for dog name elsewhere, cat named Meatloaf), rumor of "The Ling Road Man." Nell and Priya meet at 1am, comic sneaking-out sequence, discover the text was sent to the wrong group chat (embarrassing reveal) — but also a REAL clue: someone else in town also got the same emoji from an unknown number, suggesting bigger mystery. Ch3: They start investigating properly — comic detective sequence, interviewing sleepy neighbors, dog-walking incident, red herrings. Introduce the darker undercurrent: a spate of local pet disappearances / a strange man seen near the woods / rumors of "The Reaping" - a local urban legend about a slaughterhouse that closed after "an incident." Ch4: Escalation — they find something (a genuinely creepy scene, blood on Meatloaf's collar, dead animal, etc.), decide to tell Mr. Okafor or an adult, but adults are useless/comic obstacle. Introduce sibling subplot — Nell's older brother Jamie acting suspicious, maybe HE sent the texts, or he's in trouble with local older lads. Ch5: Midpoint twist — the emoji trail leads to realization that the messages are being sent from multiple numbers, like a chain/pyramid scheme of dread, an actual local "prank"/dare game called "Bloodhound" that year 11s play, where you send blood emojis to mark next target for a hazing prank. Real fear: Priya realizes she's a target next. Panic increases, comedy dims slightly, stakes feel real. Ch6: Deep dig into why — reveal true crime backstory element: years ago an incident (a kid got badly hurt in "the game," town covered it up, or a teacher was implicated), Nell's own family connected somehow (mum knew the victim, or Jamie was involved back then). Tension between comic tone and real darkness — sibling reveal: Jamie sent the original texts as a warning to protect Priya/Nell, not a threat. Ch7: Climax — confrontation at the abandoned slaughterhouse/woods/whatever creepy locale where "Bloodhound" finale happens; comic chaos (Nell falls in mud, Priya's inhaler moment, Saskia turns out to be an ally not enemy) mixed with real danger (ringleader is more unhinged than expected, actual risk of harm). Should have a genuinely dark comic set-piece — maybe involves fake blood/pig's blood prank that goes wrong, someone actually injured but not fatally, twist that Meatloaf the cat is fine and was never part of it (comic anti-climax amid real climax). Ch8: Resolution — dust settles, adults finally react (too late/useless comically), the "society" is broken up, quiet coda returns to Nell's voice, dark humor about how everyone moves on, final gut-punch/warm note about friendship, Nell gets a new midnight text that could restart cycle (open dark comic ending) or a heartfelt look at what "found family"/sisterhood means amid absurdity. Possibly final line loops back to blood emoji but recontextualized as inside joke/found solidarity (Coven adopts it as their symbol now, reclaiming it). # Reflection The pure prank/misunderstanding-only route (idea 1) is very funny but risks being too slight for 8 chapters — needs stakes to sustain darkness promised by "darkly comic." The pure crime thriller route (idea 2) risks losing the comic YA voice and becoming too heavy/generic thriller, less "YA" in feel, more grim. The hybrid (ideas 3+4 combined) seems strongest: gives comedy engine (misfired group chat, Nell's overactive true-crime brain, sneaking around at night, Priya's chaos) AND real dark stakes (a hazing game called Bloodhound with real history/injury, and a sibling connection that adds emotional weight). This lets tone shift from farce to dread and back, satisfying "darkly comic" fully rather than picking one lane. Key strengths to lean into: - Nell's first-person voice: witty, digressive, self-aware, class-conscious, obsessed with podcasts — needs consistent comic register throughout even in dark scenes (gallows humor undercuts horror without erasing it). - The blood emoji should have a satisfying layered explanation: literal misfire (mundane, funny) as first red herring, THEN the game "Bloodhound" as real explanation, THEN historical incident as depth/stakes, THEN emotional core with Jamie as protector. - Should avoid being genuinely grisly/gratuitous — the darkness is more about menace, class/small-town claustrophobia, casual cruelty of teenage social hierarchies, and history repeating, rather than graphic violence. Good fit for YA. - Ending should land on that dark-comic sweet spot: real emotional resolution (sisterhood/friendship, brother's flawed love) plus a wink of humor and a slightly unsettling final beat so it isn't saccharine — reclaiming the emoji as their own is a nice thematic bow. Pacing across 8 chapters at 1000 words needs tight scene economy — plan is roughly: Ch1 hook/voice, Ch2 first false answer + real mystery seed, Ch3-4 investigation with rising creepy discoveries, Ch5 midpoint real twist (Bloodhound game, personal stakes), Ch6 history/backstory reveal + family connection, Ch7 climax set piece, Ch8 denouement. This maintains momentum and gives each chapter a clear job, avoiding sagging middle. Decision going forward: use the hybrid plan, Nell/Priya as core duo, "Bloodhound" hazing game with real history as the mystery engine, Jamie as emotional anchor, humor via voice and side characters (Mr. Okafor, Saskia reveal as ally, chaotic mum), climax at derelict slaughterhouse/woods, ending reclaiming the blood emoji as in-joke/symbol of solidarity with one last unsettling comic sting.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a darkly comic British YA short story in first person, narrated by Nell (15), a deadpan, true-crime-obsessed catastrophist whose voice carries the whole piece — digressive, witty, self-deprecating, class-aware, prone to footnote-y asides and inflated dramatic comparisons that undercut real dread with comedy (and vice versa). The humour comes from voice and character, not slapstick alone; the darkness comes from real small-town menace, a genuine historical incident, and the casual cruelty of teenage hierarchies — not graphic violence. Plot: the midnight blood-emoji text kicks off a mystery with layered explanations. First a mundane/comic misfire (wrong group chat, period-tracker admin), which seems to resolve the tension but plants a seed: other people got the same text from an unknown number. This leads Nell and best friend Priya into investigating a local phenomenon — "Bloodhound," a secret hazing/dare game among older students that uses the blood emoji to mark the next target, echoing a covered-up incident from years back where someone got seriously hurt. Nell's older brother Jamie is entangled — not as villain but as someone trying to warn/protect the girls because of his own history with the game. The climax is a chaotic, half-farcical, half-genuinely-frightening confrontation at a derelict slaughterhouse/woods location, mixing real danger with comic disaster (mud, inhalers, an unlikely ally in queen-bee rival Saskia). The ending is quietly warm but not saccharine — friendship/found-family bond among the girls, adults reacting uselessly and too late, and a final unsettling comic sting where the group reclaims the blood emoji as their own in-joke, with one last text left ambiguously open. Stylistic aims: - Consistent first-person comic voice even in tense scenes (gallows humour, bathos) - Escalating structure: mundane → weird → dark → resolved-but-not-neatly - Grounded, textured setting (grim English town, comprehensive school, chippy, bus stop, WhatsApp/group chat culture) - Avoid: gratuitous gore, tidy moralising, adults saving the day, tonal whiplash without control - Chapter endings on hooks/turns to maintain momentum at ~1000 words each # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — "🩸🩸🩸"** Midnight text lands. Establish Nell's voice, her true-crime brain immediately spiraling into worst-case murder scenarios. Texts Priya. Establish the Coven group chat, family background (chaotic mum, absent dad, older brother Jamie being weirdly cagey lately). Chapter ends with Nell deciding to sneak out to meet Priya, phone buzzing again with a second cryptic blood emoji from an unknown number. **Chapter 2 — The Wrong Group Chat** Comic sneaking-out sequence. Nell and Priya compare notes at 1am by the bus stop/chippy. Reveal: original text was a misfire — Priya's period-tracker group chat, embarrassing but funny. Tension seems to deflate — until they realize other girls at school also got an identical text from an unknown number that night, unconnected to any tracker chat. Seed planted: this isn't a one-off. End on them spotting something odd — a smear of something on Priya's garden gate, or Nell's cat Meatloaf missing. **Chapter 3 — Meatloaf's Collar** Morning after; school day comedy (Saskia beef, Mr. Okafor's hapless assembly on "digital citizenship"). Nell and Priya start quietly polling others who got the text — pattern emerges among specific year groups. Nell finds Meatloaf's collar with blood on it in the garden — mundane explanation possible (cat fight) but planted as dread. Local rumour surfaces: "the Ling Road lot" and an old story about the derelict slaughterhouse/abattoir on the edge of town. **Chapter 4 — The Abattoir Story** Deeper investigation — interview an eccentric neighbour or older sibling of a friend who half-remembers "Bloodhound," a dare/hazing tradition among older kids that went quiet years ago after "an incident." Nell notices Jamie behaving strangely — hiding his phone, sneaking out at night. Comic obstacle: adults are useless (mum too absorbed in her own drama; Okafor over-reacts to the wrong thing). Chapter ends with Nell finding a blood emoji text on Jamie's phone, sent TO someone else. **Chapter 5 — Bloodhound** Midpoint reveal: Bloodhound is real, an ongoing secret game/hazing ritual run by older students, using the emoji as a target-marker — receiving it means you're "it" for a dare that escalates in cruelty. Priya realizes she may have been marked as next target (not a misfire after all — the timing lines up). Fear becomes real and personal; comedy dims but voice persists in gallows-humour asides. Nell confronts Jamie, who admits he knows about it but won't explain fully — panic and betrayal beat. **Chapter 6 — What Happened to Callum** Jamie finally opens up: years ago a kid named Callum was badly hurt during a Bloodhound dare at the old abattoir, covered up by the school/families to protect reputations; Jamie was there and has carried guilt since. This is why he's been sending warning texts to try to intercept the next round before it spirals — the original midnight blood-emoji was actually Jamie's, sent to the wrong contact by mistake, warning Nell obliquely. The mystery reframes: it's not one villain, but inherited harm repeating. Saskia unexpectedly reveals she knows details too (her cousin was involved) — becomes an ally. **Chapter 7 — The Slaughterhouse** Climax: Nell, Priya, Saskia, and Jamie converge at the derelict abattoir where the current Bloodhound ringleader (an older student, chillingly casual, banal-evil rather than cartoonish) is orchestrating the next dare on the newly marked target. Chaotic dark-comic set piece: mud, dropped phones, Priya's asthma scare, a stand-off that's tense but laced with absurdity (fake vs real blood confusion, someone's mum's car alarm going off). Real danger peaks then breaks — the ringleader is stopped not through violence but exposure/humiliation, adults arrive too late and comically miss the point. **Chapter 8 — Reclaimed** Aftermath: school and parents handle it clumsily/inadequately (suspensions, non-apologies, gossip). Jamie and Nell's relationship repaired a little. The Coven processes what happened with dark humour intact. Quiet emotional beat about sisterhood/found family and inherited damage being interrupted rather than fully healed. Final comic-unsettling sting: the girls adopt 🩸🩸🩸 as their own private symbol of solidarity — and the story ends with Nell getting one more midnight text, blood emojis, from an unknown number, which she now just laughs at and doesn't investigate — except the very last line leaves a flicker of doubt that maybe she should.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Writer Might Approach This A human working from "the text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸" would probably start smaller and weirder than a plotted-out mystery machine. They might: - Sit with the image itself for a while before deciding what it means — let it be genuinely ambiguous (menstruation? violence? a joke? a warning?) and mine the *discomfort of not knowing* rather than rushing to architecture a plot that explains it satisfyingly by chapter 6. - Write from a specific, lived-in irritation or obsession — a human writer often smuggles in real texture: a real school they went to, a real petty social humiliation, a real sibling dynamic — rather than "chaotic mum," "hapless teacher," "eccentric neighbour," which are archetypes assembled from genre familiarity rather than observation. - Let scenes wander a bit and be inefficient. Human short stories, especially YA-voiced ones, often digress into a joke or a tangent that doesn't serve plot at all but serves character/voice — a rant about a teacher's shoes, a pointless argument about a podcast episode. LLM writing tends to make every scene efficiently serve plot advancement, which reads as tidy but airless. - Resist explaining everything. A human might leave Jamie's motivations partially opaque, might not fully unpack "Callum" and the abattoir history with clean exposition — might let some things be gestured at, misremembered, contradictory, the way real town legends actually work (three versions of the same story, none confirmed). - Undercut the "neat symbolic ending" instinct. Reclaiming the emoji as a bonding symbol is a very LLM-shaped bow — thematically pleasing, symmetrical, a little greeting-card. A human writer might end on something messier: unresolved awkwardness, a joke that doesn't quite land, silence, an ending that trails off mid-thought rather than gonging the theme. # Common LLM Tropes/Pitfalls (General) 1. **Symmetry addiction** — everything set up must pay off, every motif must return transformed at the end (the emoji reclaimed, the brother redeemed, the rival becoming ally). Real short stories often leave loose threads. 2. **Safety** — sanding off genuine cruelty, danger, or moral ugliness so nobody is really unlikeable, nothing is really unresolved, no one does something unforgivable. Darkly comic YA (e.g., actual British YA like Malorie Blackman or even Kevin Brooks) allows uglier, sadder truths — real class contempt, real fear, kids who are genuinely awful and stay that way. 3. **Over-explaining** — LLM prose often has characters (or narration) explain the theme, explain the joke, explain the emotional beat ("this made me realise that..."). Human comic writing trusts the reader more, lets jokes land without a lampshade, lets emotional beats be shown through mundane action or silence. 4. **Wrapping with a bow** — tidy justice (ringleader "exposed and stopped"), tidy family repair (Jamie and Nell reconcile), tidy institutional response even if "clumsy," which itself is a soft, safe version of "adults fail." Human writers might let the adult failure be more damning/absurd/uncomfortable, or let reconciliation be partial and awkward rather than a hug-shaped beat. 5. **Genre-blend smoothing** — using recognizable YA-mystery scaffolding (detective duo, red herrings, midpoint twist, climax showdown at spooky location) can feel like assembled Lego rather than lived story. A human might break structure — e.g., no big showdown at all, or the "showdown" is deliberately anticlimactic/embarrassing rather than tense-then-resolved. 6. **Impressive-at-expense-of-readable** — ornate metaphor stacking, over-clever voice that never breathes, jokes that are constructed rather than observed. YA voice needs looseness, real teenage cadence (fragments, self-interruption, bathos, brand names, actually funny specific details) rather than "witty adult ventriloquizing a teen." 7. **Even pacing/beat-per-chapter mechanicalness** — the plan currently has each chapter doing a clean "job" (reveal, twist, escalate) which can feel like a checklist rather than a story that breathes unevenly — some chapters short and punchy, some slower, digression allowed. # Deep Dive: Critiquing the Current Plan **Plot machinery is very "mystery-box."** The layered reveal structure (misfire → pattern → collar/blood → abattoir legend → Bloodhound → Callum backstory → climax → reclamation) is essentially a well-oiled genre plot. It's satisfying but it's also exactly the kind of shape an LLM defaults to: rising layered revelation, midpoint twist, tidy villain, redemption arc for the flawed-but-loving brother, ally-conversion for the rival. Every single character function is doing double-service to the theme (inherited harm, sisterhood) too neatly. **Jamie's arc is a classic "secretly good, misunderstood, redeemed" LLM move** — softens what could be messier. What if Jamie is NOT fully forgivable, or his warning texts don't actually help and even make things worse? What if his guilt doesn't resolve into a clean scene of understanding? **Saskia becoming an ally is a very predictable YA-plot pivot** (mean girl secretly has depth/becomes ally) — it's the "enemies to reluctant allies" beat that's extremely common and slightly safe. Could be more interesting if she doesn't become an ally, or becomes one in a way that's uncomfortable/self-interested rather than warm. **The climax at the abattoir risks being over-engineered "big scene" territory** — multiple characters converging, danger peaking then breaking via exposure/humiliation of ringleader — this is very neatly cathartic. A human writer might instead make the climax smaller, weirder, more anticlimactic, funnier and sadder simultaneously — maybe nothing "big" actually happens at the abattoir, maybe the real horror is something quiet they find there, maybe the ringleader isn't confronted properly at all and the story's climax is actually an emotional scene between Nell/Jamie/Priya instead. **The ending's "reclaiming the emoji" as solidarity symbol is the most bow-shaped part of the whole plan** — thematically pat, plus the "ambiguous final text" stinger is a fairly stock dark-comedy-YA closer (cyclical dread callback). It could be true to voice but risks feeling engineered rather than earned. Needs either subverting or roughening up — maybe the final text is something banal that Nell has now been trained to over-read, showing her paranoia hasn't gone anywhere and possibly IS the joke/tragedy, rather than a neat "solidarity" bow with a spooky wink tacked on. **Backstory (Callum, covered-up incident) is a stock device** — "town covers up dark secret from years ago" is very familiar; risks being generic unless grounded in specific, odd, human detail rather than functioning purely as plot-explaining lore-dump in chapter 6. Human writers usually reveal backstory through partial, contradictory, oblique means (overheard, misremembered, a photo, a joke that lands wrong) not through a sit-down exposition chapter. **Every chapter currently has a clean "job"** — this is efficient but risks feeling mechanical. A human writer might let a chapter be "wasted" on something that seems like a digression (a whole chapter about a disastrous family dinner, or an argument about nothing) that actually does more character work than plot work. **Voice risk**: the plan describes Nell as "witty, self-deprecating, class-aware, footnote-y" — this is good on paper but could easily tip into performed-quirky LLM-teen voice (constant clever one-liners, over-polished bathos) rather than actually sounding like a real, specific, sometimes inarticulate 15-year-old. Need to guard against every sentence being a punchline. # Brainstorm: Ways to Make It More Human - Let some chapters be shorter/longer unevenly rather than uniform ~1000 words each in feel (even if similar in actual length, pacing/density should vary). - Allow at least one major thread to NOT resolve — e.g., we never fully learn who the "unknown number" originally was, or the Callum story stays contradictory/half-known. - Make Jamie's redemption incomplete — he did something shameful back then and doesn't get absolved by the plot; Nell's feelings toward him at the end are complicated, not just repaired. - Cut or twist the Saskia-becomes-ally beat — maybe she helps for selfish reasons, or her "alliance" is temporary/transactional, or she remains prickly. - Make the ending resist neatness — instead of "reclaiming the emoji as solidarity," maybe the group just never talks about it again, the way teenagers actually bury trauma with jokes and silence; the horror lingers unspoken rather than being transformed into a cute symbol. - Add texture through specific, odd, non-thematic details — brand names, a weird food obsession, an unrelated running joke about a teacher's mug, something that doesn't serve the plot but makes the world feel lived-in. - Let the humor be uglier/riskier at points — actual class-contempt jokes, actual meanness between the girls, something that isn't clean "banter" but has real bite, maybe something a reader might wince at rather than just chuckle. - Consider making the climax smaller-scale and stranger rather than a full ensemble showdown — maybe just Nell and Priya, maybe the "ringleader" is disappointingly ordinary/pathetic rather than a satisfying villain to expose. - Avoid a wrap-up final chapter that states the theme; end on an image, an action, or a piece of dialogue instead of reflection. - Let institutional/adult failure be genuinely bleak/funny rather than "clumsy but ultimately fine" — maybe nothing happens to the people responsible at all, which is truer to how these things often go, and darker. - Vary sentence rhythm to sound like a real teenager thinking, not a comedian scripting — include actual inarticulacy, trailing off, repetition, non-sequiturs.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a darkly comic British YA short story, first person, narrated by Nell, 15, whose voice should feel like an actual teenager thinking on the page — digressive, sometimes inarticulate, prone to bathos and specific weird detail rather than constant polished one-liners. The comedy should come from character and observation, not performed cleverness; the darkness should come from real small-scale menace, class texture, and things not resolving cleanly, rather than a tidy mystery-box plot. Given the constraints (8 chapters, ~1000 words each — a genuinely short piece), I'm scaling the plot down from a layered whodunnit with a full ensemble climax to something tighter and more human: fewer characters, fewer twists, more room for texture, silence, and things left unexplained. The story stays centred on the literal prompt — a midnight text of three blood emojis — and resists over-explaining it. I want at least one major thread to stay ambiguous by the end. Key choices: - **Plot**: The text triggers Nell's true-crime-addled panic. It turns out to be from her older brother Jamie, drunk/upset, meant for someone else — a botched, half-explained warning about something he's tangled up in (a low-level, grubby, non-cinematic bit of local trouble — not a grand hazing-cult mythology, but something smaller and more plausible: a debt, a dare that went wrong once, a group of older lads he's trying to get out from under). I'm deliberately shrinking "Bloodhound" from an elaborate secret society into something closer to a stupid, sad, ordinary bit of teenage cruelty/tradition that got out of hand once and everyone half-remembers wrong. - **Structure**: Instead of a multi-character converging climax at a symbolic location, the climax is smaller — Nell alone, or with Priya, dealing with Jamie directly. The "investigation" plotline is real but modest; I'll let some chapters be digressive (a family dinner, a bad day at school) rather than every chapter doing a mechanical plot job. - **No full villain, no full exposure/justice scene.** The person responsible for the original incident (years back) is never confronted or punished on the page — that's left to town rumour, the way it would be in real life. - **Jamie is not redeemed cleanly.** Nell's feelings about him stay mixed at the end — grateful, embarrassed, still angry, still not fully sure what happened. - **Saskia** either cut down to a minor, non-redemptive presence, or removed entirely to keep the cast small and human-scale — favouring depth of a few relationships over breadth of plot. - **Ending resists the bow.** No reclaimed symbol, no neat solidarity moment stated aloud. Instead: an ordinary, slightly unresolved image — life going on, jokes not quite landing, something still faintly unsettling under the surface. The final beat returns to the prompt's texture (a text, a phone lighting up in the dark) without wrapping it in meaning. - **Voice**: fragments, digressions, deflating similes, real specificity (brand names, bad school food, family financial stress, a genuinely non-metaphorical mundane world). Avoid constant lampshading of jokes or emotions. Trust the reader. - **Avoid**: cleanly redemptive arcs, villain unmasking scenes, thematic statements in narration, symmetrical payoffs for every setup, uniform chapter "jobs." # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — Midnight** Nell gets the text: 🩸🩸🩸, from an unknown-ish number (turns out later to be an old contact she'd deleted — Jamie's mate's number, or Jamie from a phone she doesn't recognise). Establish voice through her spiralling true-crime brain, some real texture of home life (mum asleep in front of the telly, dad not around/or working nights, damp house, a very ordinary bedroom). She doesn't investigate immediately — she just lies there, can't sleep, texts Priya who is unhelpful/asleep. Chapter ends small: she hears the front door go at half one. Someone's come in, or gone out. **Chapter 2 — Jamie** Morning after. Ordinary, textured domestic chapter — breakfast, mum's stress about money/work, a digression about something unrelated and mundane (an argument about the immersion heater, or toast). Nell clocks Jamie's off — quiet, bruised knuckle or a cut lip he brushes off, cagey about his phone. She half-asks about the text, he deflects with something that's almost a joke, almost not. Plant unease without explaining it. End on Nell finding out at school that a couple of other kids also got a weird blood-emoji text last night — but from different numbers, and nobody's that bothered, it becomes a low-key meme/joke at school rather than a grand mystery. **Chapter 3 — School** A chapter that's mostly digressive and comic — school day texture, Priya, minor social hierarchy stuff, a teacher (Mr Okafor or similar) doing something faintly absurd. The blood emoji becomes a running joke among some kids (someone's made it a reaction meme) which Nell finds both funny and quietly wrong, because she can't shake that Jamie's involved somehow. Small clue: she overhears older lads (Year 11s, including someone called Deano or similar, unglamorous name) mention "sorting it before Friday," tone that doesn't match the joke version. End with Nell watching Jamie get into a car after school that she doesn't recognise. **Chapter 4 — Deano** Nell tries (badly, amateurishly, not like a competent detective) to find out more — asks Priya, gets half a rumour: something about money owed, or a dare/bet from way back that Jamie's tangled in again, or an older feud. Texture: this should feel low-level and grubby rather than mythic — a debt over a phone, a fight after a party, something plausible and unglamorous. Family dinner scene — mum oblivious/self-absorbed, Nell trying to bring it up and failing, comic-awkward, some real tenderness/exasperation. End with Nell finding Jamie's old phone (or seeing his current phone light up) with another blood emoji sent to a name she doesn't recognise. **Chapter 5 — What Nell Actually Knows** Slower chapter, more internal — Nell reflects (without over-explaining thematically) on a half-memory: something that happened when Jamie was younger, a friend of his called something ordinary (not a grand tragic name, keep it low-key — "Ryan hurt his arm that time," told through vague, half-remembered kid logic) that nobody talks about properly, that maybe wasn't as serious as she built it up to be in her head, or maybe was and got buried. This should feel uncertain — Nell herself doesn't know how much is real memory vs playground legend. Priya has her own family stress subplot glimpsed here too (small, human, not resolved) to widen the world slightly. End with Jamie not coming home for tea. **Chapter 6 — Half One Again** Nell can't sleep, goes looking for Jamie against better judgement (mundane version of "investigation" — walking to the bus stop, the chippy, somewhere ordinary and local, not a gothic slaughterhouse). Priya comes with her, comic-tense sneaking-out beat but smaller scale than before. They find Jamie — not in grand danger, but in a stupid, sad, human-scale bad situation (arguing with the older lads over money/a phone/an actual small thing that's been inflated by pride and fear on both sides). Some tension, some threat, but grounded — not a monster, just blokes being pathetic and a bit frightening. Nell's presence embarrasses/interrupts more than heroically solves it. **Chapter 7 — Going Home** The immediate situation defuses — not through triumphant exposure or justice, just through anticlimax: someone's mum shouting from a window, a car alarm, the absurd ordinary interruptions of real life breaking tension. Walk home, Jamie and Nell mostly silent or talking around it rather than a big cathartic conversation. He gives a partial, reluctant, incomplete explanation — enough to understand the shape of it (debt, old guilt, wanting to protect her by warning the wrong number that awful night) without a full tidy monologue. Some dry humour returns. Nothing is fixed. End on something small and specific — him giving her his last chip, or similar. **Chapter 8 — Later** Coda, shorter chapter. A few days/weeks later, textured normal life resuming — school, Priya, the blood-emoji joke fading into forgotten meme status among the wider school (this being darkly funny — how quickly things get absorbed and trivialised). Nell's relationship with Jamie is a bit different, not healed, not broken — some new wary tenderness, some things unsaid and staying unsaid. Final beat: Nell's phone lights up at midnight again. She looks at it. Ambiguous, small, deliberately anticlimactic ending — could be nothing, could be something — she decides not to open it right away, or opens it and it's utterly banal (a Snapchat streak reminder, wrong number, something bathetic) — landing the story on a note that's funny, unresolved, and quietly unsettling all at once, without stating what it all meant.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Nell Kowalczyk **Background & personality:** Fifteen, youngest of two, lives on the less-nice end of a not-nice-anywhere town — the kind of place with one good chippy and three closed ones. Her surname gets mangled by every substitute teacher and she's stopped correcting them past a certain point of exhaustion. She's been "into true crime" since she was about twelve, back when it was innocuous (a phase, like the horse phase, except it never stopped), and now has a genuinely encyclopaedic knowledge of forensic entomology and serial killer trivia that she deploys at wildly inappropriate moments. She catastrophises constantly but with total sincerity — she isn't performing panic for effect, she really does think the smoke alarm chirping at 3am is A Sign. She's smart in a scattered way — good at English, hopeless at maths, reads Wikipedia rabbit holes instead of doing homework. Flaws: she's a coward in the specific way of people who talk a big game — she'll happily theorise about a murder for forty minutes but freeze up in an actual confrontation. She's also a bit of a snob about people who don't find her funny, and she lies to herself constantly about how much she cares what people think of her. **Idiosyncrasies:** Keeps a "case file" notes app on her phone for things that are almost certainly nothing (the neighbour's bins, a missing traffic cone, now this). Eats toast with the crusts left for last like a reward system. Can't sleep without a podcast on but always picks the most disturbing one available, then complains she can't sleep. **Physicality:** Small, a bit hunched, chews the drawstring of her hoodie without noticing. Bites her nails down to nothing on her left hand only. Walks fast even when she's not going anywhere in particular, like she's trying to outpace her own thoughts. Face is very readable — she has no poker face at all, which she thinks is a personality trait ("resting alert face") and everyone else thinks is hilarious. **Motives & relationships:** Loves Jamie fiercely but resentfully — he got the "easy" personality in the family, everyone likes him without trying, and she's aware she's the weird one. Wants, more than anything, to be taken seriously, which is a very fifteen-year-old want and she'd die before admitting it in those words. Priya is her actual best friend, the person she is unfiltered with. Mum she loves but finds embarrassing and slightly exhausting to be around, though there's real tenderness under the eye-rolling. **Flavour lines:** "Statistically, most people are murdered by someone they know, which is either extremely comforting or the opposite, depending on how your week's going." "I didn't sleep. I lay there doing the maths on how long it'd take someone to fall out with a five-tonne meat hook and decided, on balance, not very long at all." "Jamie says I've got 'a main character complex.' I say at least I'd be an interesting corpse." --- # Priya Anand **Background & personality:** Nell's best friend since Year 7, when they bonded over both getting detention for the same pointless reason (talking, obviously, always talking). Priya is the calmer, more socially fluent of the two but has her own low hum of anxiety she manages by being aggressively organised — colour-coded revision timetables she never follows, three different diaries. Comes from a slightly better-off household than Nell's, which neither of them talks about directly but both know. Her parents are going through something — not divorcing exactly, but a coldness in the house Priya mentions in passing and then changes the subject from, every time. She's funnier than she gives herself credit for, delivers her best lines completely deadpan and then looks faintly surprised when people laugh. Flaws: conflict-avoidant to a fault, will agree to plans she hates rather than say no, and has a habit of going quiet and unreachable when things get too real rather than actually engaging. **Idiosyncrasies:** Terrified of wasps to an irrational, screaming degree. Always has gum but never offers it first, you have to ask. Has an inhaler she's slightly precious and embarrassed about, pretends she doesn't need it as often as she does. **Physicality:** Taller than Nell, good posture from years of Bharatanatyam lessons she quit at twelve and still misses. Talks with her hands. Has a specific unimpressed eyebrow that does more work than most people's whole sentences. **Motives & relationships:** Wants things at home to be normal again but has no idea how to make that happen so she pours her energy into Nell's dramas instead, which are more fixable. Genuinely loves Nell's chaos, finds it grounding somehow, a problem she can actually help with. Slightly intimidated by Jamie in the normal way you're intimidated by your best friend's older brother, finds him funnier than he probably deserves. **Flavour lines:** "You text me 'blood emoji times three' at half twelve at night and then go to sleep? That's not a cry for help, Nell, that's just rude." "I'm not scared. I'm cautious. There's a difference and the difference is wasps." "Can we establish, before we do anything stupid, that this is a stupid idea? I just want it on the record." --- # Jamie Kowalczyk **Background & personality:** Seventeen, in the year above where things start to actually matter (college applications, or the version of that which exists in a town where most people don't go), and visibly buckling under a pressure he won't name. Was the golden kid for a while — good at football, easy with people, teachers liked him — and has been quietly quietly coming apart for about a year in a way the family has collectively decided not to look at directly. Funny in a way that's a defence mechanism more than joy; his humour gets sharper and more deflecting exactly when things are worst. Owes money to, or is entangled with, a small orbit of slightly older lads from school who've left and gone nowhere, doing nothing, and who operate by a code of pride and grievance that's stupid and also genuinely a bit frightening because stupid pride is how people actually get hurt. Flaws: he protects people by lying to them, which he thinks of as kindness. Bottles things up until they come out sideways, at 1am, as three blood emojis to the wrong number. Not a bad person — good at love, bad at asking for help. **Idiosyncrasies:** Still does the voices from a cartoon they watched as kids when he's trying to make Nell laugh, even now, even when it's clearly not landing. Always gives away the last chip. Has a cut lip or bruised knuckle he waves off with a different flimsy excuse each time. **Physicality:** Bigger than Nell in the way older brothers are just structurally bigger, but has started to look thinner lately, tired around the eyes in a way that ages him oddly. Slouches on the sofa taking up the whole thing on purpose. Has a laugh that used to be loud and now comes out clipped, like he's rationing it. **Motives & relationships:** Loves Nell and Mum in a protective, slightly performative way — the man of the house complex, badly worn. Doesn't want Nell to know the specifics of what he's in because he can't stand the idea of her being scared, doesn't clock that she's more scared not knowing. Has a real, buried guilt about something from a few years back (Ryan's arm, half-referenced, never fully explained) that colours how he behaves now, though he'd never frame it that way himself. **Flavour lines:** "You weren't meant to get that. Forget it. It's nothing, it's — forget it, Nell, seriously." "I'm fine. Look at my face, this is a fine face." "Someone has to be the boring one in this family and it's clearly not going to be you, so." --- # Mum (Sandra Kowalczyk) **Background & personality:** Mid-forties, works two jobs stitched together into something that only barely counts as one income (care work shifts plus weekend bar work at the same dismal pub for a decade), permanently three conversations behind on what's actually going on with her kids because she's asleep by nine on the nights she's not working. Not a bad mother — genuinely loving, occasionally fierce in their defence — but stretched so thin that "noticing things" is a luxury she can't always afford, and she knows it, which makes her defensive about it. Copes with humour that's blunter and less clever than her kids', repeats the same three sayings ("not in this house you don't," "I haven't got the energy for this today, love, I mean it"). Flaws: avoidant about anything that would require actually confronting Jamie, prefers to believe things are fine because the alternative is unbearable on top of everything else. **Idiosyncrasies:** Falls asleep in front of the telly with the same mug of cold tea every night, wakes up furious that no one woke her. Has increasingly elaborate opinions about a property renovation show she watches religiously and disagrees with loudly, alone, at high volume. **Physicality:** Tired in the shoulders, moves like someone permanently doing mental arithmetic about money. Softens completely, briefly, when either kid is actually sick or upset — the mask drops for exactly as long as it takes to make them tea and toast, then goes back up. **Motives & relationships:** Wants, more than anything, for her kids to have it easier than she did, and is quietly heartbroken every day that this doesn't seem to be happening. Loves both kids without favouritism but relates to them differently — Jamie she treats like a small adult and co-conspirator, Nell she still babies slightly, which Nell resents and secretly likes. **Flavour line:** "I'm not being funny, Nell, but it is half eleven and I have got precisely one and a half brain cells left and you want to talk to me about *blood emojis*."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One: Midnight The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸. No words. No "hey." No "u up." Just three drops of blood, sitting there in the dark like they'd been waiting for me to open my eyes. I want it on record that I did not scream. I want it on record that I am, generally, a calm and rational person who does not immediately assume the worst about every piece of digital information that comes into her possession. That is a lie, obviously, but I want it *on record*, because if this turns out to be my last known communication before something terrible happens to me, I'd like whoever reads my phone afterwards to think I handled it with dignity. I did not handle it with dignity. I sat up so fast I got a head rush and had to lie back down again like a Victorian woman who's heard bad news about a war. Here is what you need to understand about me: I have watched forty-one hours of a podcast called *Dead Air* in the last two months alone. I know what a "burner phone" is. I know that most murders happen between people who already know each other, which everyone treats as reassuring and which I personally think is the single most alarming statistic in the world, because it means it's basically always someone you had round for tea once. So when three blood emojis land in my inbox at 12:03am from a number saved in my phone as "??? (maybe Chelsea's mum???)," my brain does not go, *huh, weird.* My brain goes: *this is it. This is the thing. This is the beginning of the true crime podcast episode about me,* and somebody in 2031 with a soothing voice is going to say *"on the night of the fourteenth, fifteen-year-old Nell Kowalczyk received a text that would change everything,"* over some ominous music, and my mum is going to cry on camera in a cardigan I picked out for her at Christmas. I read it four more times. It did not become less blood-emoji-shaped. I texted Priya. *did u get a weird text just now.* Nothing. Obviously nothing. It was gone midnight and Priya sleeps like she's being paid to, unlike me, who has been informally diagnosed by WebMD with eleven different sleep disorders, none of them real. I texted her again. *priya. blood emojis. three of them. from an unknown number. this is either a joke or I am going to be a documentary.* Still nothing. A little grey tick. Delivered. Not read. The worst kind of silence, the kind with a receipt. Downstairs the telly was still going — I could hear the tinny burble of it through the floor, which meant Mum had fallen asleep in front of the property show again, mug of tea gone cold on the arm of the sofa, and would wake up at some ungodly hour furious that nobody had woken her, as if any of us are contractually obliged to monitor her narcolepsy. My room is directly above the living room and the walls in our house are apparently made of wet cardboard, so I get the full audio experience of whatever she's watching whether I want it or not. That night it was a couple called Trace and Deb wondering aloud whether they could really justify knocking through into the kitchen. I lay there listening to Trace and Deb agonise about a stud wall while I quietly prepared myself to be murdered. I want to be clear that I know how that sounds. I tried texting the number back. *who is this.* Two little words. Devastatingly polite, considering. Nothing. I opened the number's profile, which had no photo, just the default grey silhouette, the digital equivalent of a blank stare. I scrolled up through the chat history to see if there was anything else there, some context, some clue, and there wasn't, because there was no chat history — this was the very first thing this number had ever said to me, ever, in the history of our acquaintance, and what it had chosen to say was three drops of blood and then silence, like a Victorian ghost that's forgotten how words work. I did the responsible thing, which was to open my Notes app and start what I call, in my head, a *case file*, though it is really just a bulleted list titled "BLOOD TEXT ???" with things like: - unknown number, no name saved, no photo - 12:03am is a Weirdly Specific time to send nothing but emojis - could be a prank (Chelsea? Deano? someone bored) - could NOT be a prank - check if anyone else got this - do NOT tell mum, she will simply say "not in this house you don't" and go back to sleep, which is not helpful I lay there for a long time listening to Trace and Deb decide, tentatively, to go ahead with the knock-through, and I thought about how funny it was that somewhere out there someone might genuinely be dying while a woman called Deb worried out loud about whether the exposed brick would "read as a feature or a mistake," and I didn't know yet how close to home that thought was going to turn out to be, standing there in the dark with my phone lighting up my face like something out of one of my own stupid podcasts. At half one, I heard the front door go. I lay very still and listened. Not the sound of someone coming in. The sound of someone going out.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates genuine command of the voice established in the character profiles. The narrator's personality comes through with remarkable consistency — the true-crime obsession, the catastrophising, the self-aware but sincere panic, the specific detail of the Notes app "case file." These aren't performed quirks bolted onto a generic narrator; they feel inhabited. The voice is the chapter's greatest strength. Lines like "like a Victorian woman who's heard bad news about a war" and "the digital equivalent of a blank stare" land because they're grounded in character logic rather than reaching for effect. The Victorian woman simile is particularly good — it's deflating and specific and slightly absurd in exactly the right register. The Trace and Deb subplot (the property show couple) is a genuinely excellent piece of texture: it's funny, it's class-specific, it's atmospheric, and the line "how funny it was that somewhere out there someone might genuinely be dying while a woman called Deb worried out loud about whether the exposed brick would 'read as a feature or a mistake'" earns its moment of near-profundity without announcing itself as profound. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the text arrives, Nell spirals, she texts Priya (who doesn't respond), she makes a case file, she hears the front door at half one. The plan's instruction to "end small" is executed well — the final beat is genuinely unsettling without being melodramatic, and the distinction between "someone coming in" and "someone going out" is a clean, effective piece of misdirection/tension. Where the chapter is slightly less successful: the meta-commentary on her own narration ("I want it on record") is a well-worn YA device, and while it's executed competently here, it's not particularly fresh. The podcast-narrator fantasy ("somebody in 2031 with a soothing voice") is good but the joke runs slightly long — the cardigan detail is the right place to end it, but it takes a beat too long to get there. There's also a minor tendency to over-explain the joke: "like a Victorian ghost that's forgotten how words work" is fine but the preceding setup already did the work, and the tag slightly deflates it. The chapter is notably light on dialogue (there essentially is none, which is appropriate for a midnight chapter), so the "Weak Dialogue" metric is almost inapplicable — what exists is text messages, which are handled naturally. The darkness is well-earned: the chapter doesn't manufacture menace, it lets the ordinary domestic texture (cold tea, wet cardboard walls, Trace and Deb) do the atmospheric work, and the final line lands with genuine quiet dread. The comedy and the unease coexist without either undermining the other, which is the central tonal challenge of the piece and is handled with real skill here. Minor quibbles: "standing there in the dark with my phone lighting up my face like something out of one of my own stupid podcasts" is a slightly clunky sentence — the "standing there" is odd since she's been lying in bed, and the simile is vague where the rest of the chapter is specific. This is the one moment where the prose feels slightly rushed. Overall: a confident, well-executed opening chapter that establishes voice, stakes, and atmosphere without over-explaining any of them. The plan is followed closely, the character is consistent with her profile, and the tonal balance is genuinely achieved rather than merely attempted. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Two: Jamie Nobody got murdered overnight, as far as I could tell, which I chose to interpret as a good sign rather than evidence that I'd wasted an entire REM cycle on nothing. Downstairs, the immersion heater situation had reached crisis point, which is to say Mum had left it on again and was now standing in the kitchen holding the electric bill like it had personally wronged her. "Forty quid," she said, to nobody, to the toaster. "Forty quid for hot water we didn't even use, because somebody" — she did not look at me, which meant it was aimed at Jamie, who was not present to receive it — "can't remember to switch a switch." "I'll switch it," I said, not moving. "I'm not being funny, Nell, but it is quarter to eight and I have got precisely one and a half brain cells left and I don't need the immersion heater lecture from you as well." I let that go. There's no version of that conversation that ends well for anyone, and I had bigger things going on than water temperature, namely the fact that I had potentially been marked for death at 12:03am and nobody in this house appeared to have registered it. Jamie came down at ten to, which was late even for him, and I clocked it before I clocked anything else about him — the lateness, the way he came in already halfway through pretending everything was normal, like he'd rehearsed the walk from the stairs to the fridge. Then I clocked the lip. Split, just at the corner, not dramatic, the kind of thing you could believably explain with a cupboard door if nobody looked too closely. "What happened to your face," I said, because I am constitutionally incapable of not saying the thing. "Cupboard door," he said, not looking up from the fridge. "Which cupboard." "The murder one. Full of knives. You'd know, you're obsessed." "Ha," I said, flatly, because it was actually a decent line and I wasn't going to reward it. "Seriously though." "Seriously, cupboard door." He got the milk out, sniffed it theatrically, put it back. "You look like you didn't sleep. Very serial killer under-eye situation you've got going on." "I got a weird text." That got half a look. Not enough of one. "What kind of weird." "Blood emoji. Three of them. Midnight, from a number I don't know." He went very still in a way that I would have missed if I hadn't spent the whole night practising the specific art of watching someone's face for information. It lasted maybe one second. Then he shrugged, put the milk carton down harder than it needed to be put down, and said, "Probably someone's mucking about. Chelsea's lot do stuff like that." And then, before I could say anything else: "You want toast or are you doing the tragic diet thing again." "I'm not doing a diet, I just don't like the bread you buy—" "Right, well, it's the only bread there is, so." And he was gone, upstairs, two at a time, phone already out of his pocket before he'd cleared the doorway, thumb going at it in a way that looked less like texting and more like damage control. I sat with my toast — crusts saved for last, obviously, the reward has to mean something — and thought: that was not a normal conversation. At school it had already become a Whole Thing, in the way things become a Whole Thing overnight without anyone quite agreeing to it. By first period, at least four other people I knew of had also got a blood-emoji text at some point after midnight — Chelsea, obviously, because Chelsea gets everything first; a boy called Ross who mostly exists to repeat things Chelsea says one beat later; and, bafflingly, Mr Okafor's form group had somehow turned it into a bit, so that by break there was a small but committed faction of Year Tens sending each other 🩸🩸🩸 as a reaction to literally anything — a bad mark on a test, someone dropping their yoghurt, the news that the Year 11 prom theme was "Enchanted Garden." "It's not funny," I told Priya, who was laughing at her own phone. "It's a bit funny." "Someone sent that to actual me at actual midnight from an actual unknown number and you're doing it as a joke about yoghurt." "Nell, half of Year Ten got it. It's not a message from beyond the grave, it's some bored Year Eleven with nothing better to do." She put her phone away, finally looked at me properly, did the eyebrow. "You didn't sleep, did you." "I heard the front door at half one." "People's front doors do that. It's called having a front door." "Jamie's got a cut lip and won't say how, and he changed the subject about the text so fast I nearly got whiplash." Priya considered this the way she considers everything, carefully, like she's checking it for wasps. "Okay, that bit's slightly weird," she admitted. "But 'my brother's cagey and has a cut lip' is basically just 'my brother is seventeen.' That's not a case file, Nell, that's Tuesday." She was right, probably. That was the annoying thing about Priya, she was almost always right, in the calm, infuriating way of someone who'd never once in her life stayed awake until two in the morning constructing a timeline of her own murder. Still. After school, walking to the gate, I saw Jamie get into a car I didn't recognise — not a school car, not anyone's mum, a grey hatchback with a dent in the door, engine already running like it hadn't planned on stopping for long — and something in me, the part that watches too many podcasts, went very quiet and very alert, the way you go quiet when you've heard a floorboard creak in a house you thought was empty.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Two delivers on most of what the plan promised: a textured domestic morning, Jamie's evasiveness planted with restraint, the blood-emoji joke spreading through school as a meme, and the chapter ending on Nell watching Jamie get into an unknown car.
