Model Output:
# Chapter Five: What Mercy Doesn't Say She didn't sleep. She sat on the edge of her bunk with the scrap of permit unfolded on her knee, reading the same three lines over and over as if repetition might fill in the redacted parts. *LIVE SPECIMEN — DESIGNATION [REDACTED] — HANDLE PER XENO-CONTAINMENT PROTOCOL 4.* Xeno-containment. Not vermin. Not cargo damage. Something somebody had paid to keep alive, and had failed to keep contained, and had let go out into the world as an as-is lot in a rusted grain silo on a moon nobody important lived on. She thought about the man in the heavy coat. *Company policy.* She thought about the way he hadn't looked at any of them directly the whole time, the particular flatness of a man who's decided the less he knows the safer he'll sleep. Somewhere under all of that, unbidden, came Adder's Fork — not the whole of it, she never let herself have the whole of it at once, just a fragment: the smell of the settlement after, ash and wet dust, and a woman's voice through a translator box that hadn't been working right, saying something none of them had bothered to get fixed in time to understand. She'd been given an order and a designation for the people in that settlement too — not livestock, not vermin, but not fully people either, not on the paperwork, not in the voice of the man who'd given the order. *Relocate. Contain. Handle per protocol.* Words did a lot of work when you needed them to. She folded the memory back up the way she'd folded the permit, small enough to carry without it showing, and went to find Dobbs before he could make good on his threat to space the crate at first light. "We're not spacing it," she told him, in the galley, before he'd even had coffee. Dobbs looked at her over the rim of his cup with an expression she didn't often see on him — something careful, something that wasn't quite argument yet but was gathering toward it. "That thing near killed Silas." "I know what it did." "Then explain to me why we're keeping a hold full of teeth on a three-day burn to Corvale." She didn't explain. That was the trouble, and she knew it even as she was doing it — she made the call the way she'd been trained to make calls, fast and from the front, without laying out the reasoning for the people who'd have to live with it. "We're not spacing it," she said again. "We're not opening it either. We hold steady till Corvale and figure out where it actually belongs." "And if it comes at somebody else first?" "Then we deal with that when it happens." Dobbs didn't like it. He said so, plainly, more plainly than he usually allowed himself with her — "You're asking me to trust you on faith, Mercy, same as always" — but he didn't push past that, because trust on faith was the only currency he'd ever really wanted from her in the first place, and he wasn't about to stop taking it now over one bad night. Silas took it worse. He came to breakfast moving stiff, arm bound tight, and when Mercy told him the decision he set his fork down with a precision that was somehow louder than if he'd thrown it. "You want to gamble the whole boat on a feeling, Captain," he said, "that's your right. Just don't ask me to feel good about it." "I'm not asking you to feel anything." "Good. Because I don't." He didn't argue further than that, which was almost worse — he simply withdrew into a cold, competent quiet that lasted the rest of the day, checking his own weapon rather than the crate, treating the whole subject as a decision that had been made over his head, which it had. Ellis, for his part, took the news with something closer to relief than anyone expected, and by that evening Mercy caught him — was meant to catch him, she suspected, given how little effort he'd put into hiding it — leaving an open ration tin near the loose grating by the aft vent, a good ten feet clear of the crate itself. "That's not smart," she told him. "No," he agreed, not moving to take it back. "You do know if that thing comes through that vent for the food, it's coming through a foot from your bunk." "I know." He looked at the grating a moment longer. "I just keep thinking — whatever it is, it's alone in the dark on a ship full of people who want it dead. I know what that's like. Not the — I mean, not exactly, but. Enough." Mercy didn't answer that right away. She thought about telling him to stop, ordering it even, the way she'd ordered Dobbs and steamrolled Silas without a word of explanation either time. Instead she found herself crouching, retying the loose grating panel so it wouldn't clatter and give him away to the whole ship, and said nothing else about it, which Ellis understood, correctly, as permission. She didn't tell either of them about the permit in her jacket. She told herself she was still deciding what it meant. That was true, as far as it went. It just wasn't the whole of the truth, and she'd gotten very good, a long time ago, at living inside that particular gap.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Five is a strong piece of character-driven prose that largely delivers on the ambitious intentions laid out in the writing plan. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. STRENGTHS: The chapter's central achievement is Mercy's interiority rendered through oblique, fragmented memory rather than confession. The Adder's Fork passage is handled with genuine restraint: "a woman's voice through a translator box that hadn't been working right, saying something none of them had bothered to get fixed in time to understand." This is excellent — the horror is in the bureaucratic negligence, the untranslated voice, the passive construction. The parallel drawn between the redacted species designation and the dehumanizing language of colonial "relocation" ("not livestock, not vermin, but not fully people either, not on the paperwork") is thematically coherent and earns its weight without being stated too baldly. The dialogue is consistently good. Silas's "You want to gamble the whole boat on a feeling, Captain, that's your right. Just don't ask me to feel good about it" lands exactly as the character profile intends — cold, precise, not melodramatic. Dobbs's "You're asking me to trust you on faith, Mercy, same as always" is quietly devastating in its accuracy. Ellis's speech about knowing what it's like to be "alone in the dark on a ship full of people who want it dead" is the chapter's most emotionally exposed moment, and it works because it's immediately qualified ("Not the — I mean, not exactly, but. Enough.") — the self-correction feels genuinely young and uncertain rather than scripted. The ending action — Mercy crouching to retie the grating rather than ordering Ellis to stop — is a fine piece of show-don't-tell. It communicates her decision without narrating it, and the final paragraph's acknowledgment that she's "living inside that particular gap" between partial truth and full truth is honest about her self-deception without being heavy-handed. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Mercy's interiority with fragmented memory (not a monologue), the unilateral decision creating friction rather than solidarity, Ellis leaving food near the vents, Silas's cold disagreement, Dobbs's loyalty-on-faith. The near-encounter with the creature is handled as planned — wordless, glimpsed only through Ellis's action rather than direct contact. WEAKNESSES: The Adder's Fork memory passage, while generally well-handled, contains one moment that tips slightly toward the over-written: "She folded the memory back up the way she'd folded the permit, small enough to carry without it showing." This is the chapter's most self-consciously literary line, and it's the kind of metaphor that calls attention to itself. It's not egregious, but it's the one place where the prose reaches for effect rather than earning it through plainness. The thematic parallel between the redacted designation and Adder's Fork is drawn a touch too explicitly in the narration: "Words did a lot of work when you needed them to." This is the chapter telling us what we've already understood from the imagery. The reader has already made the connection; the sentence is unnecessary and slightly condescending. Ellis's speech, while mostly good, contains "I know what that's like. Not the — I mean, not exactly, but. Enough." The self-correction is smart, but "Enough" as a standalone sentence is a slightly writerly flourish — it's the kind of thing that reads as crafted rather than spoken, though it's a minor complaint. The chapter is perhaps slightly too tidy in its emotional architecture — each crew member gets their beat (Dobbs: loyalty-on-faith, Silas: cold withdrawal, Ellis: guilty compassion), and while each beat is well-executed, the chapter moves through them with a certain efficiency that occasionally feels more like a plan being executed than a scene unfolding. The plan called for friction rather than solidarity, and the chapter delivers that, but the friction is perhaps slightly too neatly distributed. The creature remains appropriately absent and ambiguous, consistent with the plan. The corporate permit detail is well-deployed as a plot mechanism without becoming an exposition dump. Overall, this is accomplished, disciplined prose that respects its own intentions. The weaknesses are minor — one over-literary metaphor, one unnecessary thematic statement — against a chapter that consistently trusts its reader, maintains character voice, and handles difficult emotional material with genuine restraint. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Six: Company The hail came in eleven hours out from Corvale, a polite chime on the comm that Mercy almost let ring through to voicemail out of pure instinct before something made her answer it live. The face that resolved on the screen was unremarkable in a way that took effort to achieve — a man in his fifties with a collar pressed too sharp for a ship this far out, tired eyes, the particular blandness of someone who'd learned long ago that looking dangerous only made people dig in. "Captain," he said, like they were already acquainted. "Name's Voss. I represent Halbrecht Exogenic. I understand you picked up some freight at Kessup's Reach a few days back." "We pick up a lot of freight." "This particular lot," Voss said, unbothered, "came off a transport that had an... administrative accident. We'd like it back. Company's prepared to pay a recovery fee — call it four thousand, credited on delivery, no questions, no report filed against your ship for unlicensed transport of a restricted specimen." Mercy kept her face still. Behind her, she was aware of Dobbs going quiet at the engineering console, of Silas drifting into the doorway with his bad arm held stiff against his side. "Don't know what you're talking about," she said. Voss smiled, small and tired, like a man doing paperwork he'd done a hundred times before. "Captain, I'm not here to argue what it is. I'm here to collect what it's numbered as. Save us both the philosophy." "That an official designation? 'What it's numbered as'?" "It's the only one I've got clearance to give you." He shifted, wincing slightly, a hand going briefly to his hip before he caught himself and stopped. "Look. I've done this a dozen times on a dozen boats just like yours. It goes easiest when it goes simple. You hand over the specimen, I credit your account, everybody flies on with a story to tell in a bar someday. You don't, and it gets a good deal less simple for you, and honestly, Captain, not much better for me. I'd rather not have that day." "And if I told you the crate came open on its own three days back and whatever was in it's long gone?" Something in Voss's expression flickered — not surprise, exactly, more like a man recalculating a number he'd been fairly sure of. "Then I'd say that's an expensive story for you, because my instruments say otherwise, and I'd hate for us to disagree about it in a way that costs anybody blood." Mercy cut the feed a few seconds after that, more to have the last word than because there was anything left to say. "He's bluffing about the instruments," Silas said from the doorway. "Maybe. Maybe not." "Either way," Dobbs said, "man knows exactly what he's after and he's not shy about admitting he'll come take it." They regrouped in the galley — all four of them this time, even Ellis pulled off his shift, the ration tin business apparently common knowledge now whether or not anyone said so out loud. Mercy laid the permit scrap on the table, the one she'd been carrying folded in her jacket for two days, and let them all look at it before she spoke. "Xeno-containment," Dobbs read aloud, squinting. "Live specimen. That's what near took Silas's arm off?" "That's what the paperwork says came out of that crate, yeah." "And you sat on this for two days," Silas said. Not a question. "I did." Nobody said anything to that for a moment. It was Ellis, of all of them, who broke the silence — quieter than usual, more certain than she'd heard him yet. "So it's not vermin. It's not some feral thing that got loose by accident. Somebody was moving it on purpose, and somebody lost it, and now this Voss wants it back bad enough to chase us across half a system for it." "Don't romanticize it," Silas said. "Could be a weapon they're recovering same as anything else." "Could be," Ellis said. "Or it could be the last thing this Voss wants is witnesses to whatever they were doing with a live, sapient thing in an unmarked crate." "We don't know it's sapient." "We don't know it isn't." Silas didn't have an answer for that one right away, which was its own kind of answer. Mercy let the argument run a while before she cut it off, because it wasn't really about the creature anymore — it was Dobbs's loyalty and Silas's anger and Ellis's guilt all finding a place to land, and none of it was going to get decided at this table. "We're not handing it over," she said finally. "And we're not fighting a company ship head-on if we can help it. We stall, we run if we can, and if we can't—" She stopped. "If we can't," Silas said, "we fight a company ship head-on." "If we can't," Mercy agreed, "yeah." Dobbs let out a breath that was half laugh, half something else. "Well. Least the coolant pump's fixed. Small mercies." On the bridge an hour later, the sensor board pinged once, twice, then steady — Voss's ship, closing distance, patient as a man with nowhere better to be and all the time in the world to get what he'd been sent for.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Six delivers on most of what the writing plan promised for this beat: Voss arrives as a tired, transactional company man rather than a menacing villain, the crew argument doesn't resolve into clean unity, and Mercy's unilateral decision creates friction rather than solidarity. The chapter is competent and often quite good, but it has some notable strengths and a few weaknesses worth examining carefully. **Strengths:** Voss is the chapter's best achievement. The description of his face as "unremarkable in a way that took effort to achieve" is a genuinely sharp observation. His dialogue is exactly right — bureaucratic, slightly apologetic, deflecting philosophy with procedure. The hip detail (his hand going briefly to his hip before catching himself) is the kind of specific physical tell that earns its place. "I'm not here to argue what it is. I'm here to collect what it's numbered as" is a strong line that does real character work. He's uncomfortable to hate cleanly, as planned. The dialogue throughout is mostly dry and oblique in the right way. The exchange between Ellis and Silas about sapience ("We don't know it's sapient." / "We don't know it isn't." / Silas didn't have an answer for that one right away, which was its own kind of answer) is well-handled — it lets the argument breathe without forcing a resolution, and the narratorial observation earns its place. Mercy cutting the feed "more to have the last word than because there was anything left to say" is a nice character beat — small, specific, true to her profile. **Weaknesses:** The chapter is somewhat mechanical in its execution of the plan. The beats proceed in a fairly predictable order: hail, negotiation, crew argument, decision, closing sensor ping. There's little that genuinely surprises within the chapter itself. The plan called for Voss to be "harder to hate cleanly" — this is achieved, but only just; the chapter doesn't push into the genuinely uncomfortable territory where you might feel some sympathy for his position. The reveal of the permit scrap at the table is handled a bit too cleanly. Mercy laying it on the table and "letting them all look at it" is a slightly stagey gesture — the plan noted she'd been sitting on this for two days, and Silas's "you sat on this for two days" / "I did" exchange is good, but the moment passes too quickly. The friction this should create is acknowledged but not really felt. The final image — "patient as a man with nowhere better to be and all the time in the world to get what he'd been sent for" — is competent but slightly overwritten compared to the chapter's otherwise spare register. It's not purple prose exactly, but it's the one moment where the writing reaches for effect rather than earning it. Dobbs's "Small mercies" line is a bit on-the-nose given the captain's name, though it could be intentional. It reads as slightly too cute. The chapter follows its plan faithfully and the characters are consistent with their profiles. Ellis is appropriately more certain than usual, Silas is cold and practical, Dobbs provides loyalty-over-conviction. The plan's note about "no clean unity" is honored. The chapter does what it needs to do without doing much more than that — it's solid craft rather than exceptional writing. The pacing is appropriate for a chapter that's meant to be a hinge point — not the slow chapter (that was Chapter 3) and not the action chapter (that's Chapter 7). It moves efficiently. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Seven: Boarding Voss's ship didn't fire on them. That was the first wrong note — Mercy had braced for a warning shot across the bow, something to force a surrender, and instead the company vessel simply matched velocity, latched a docking clamp to the *Second Chance*'s aft airlock with a shudder that ran the length of the hull, and started cutting. "He's not here to negotiate anymore," Silas said, already moving, sidearm in his good hand, the other bound tight against his ribs. "Four thousand credits didn't stretch far as a threat," Dobbs muttered, grabbing the old scattergun he kept bolted under the engineering console for exactly this kind of day nobody wanted. Mercy split them fast — Silas to the aft corridor to hold the breach point, Dobbs to engineering to keep the drive alive and cut power to any section they needed to seal, Ellis to the bridge to keep the ship flying if it came to that, herself to the hold, because whatever came through that airlock was coming for the crate, and the crate was where this ended one way or the other. The cutting torch finished its circle on the airlock door with a groan of metal giving way, and four of Voss's people came through in staggered order, not soldiers exactly, more like men who'd been issued weapons and told to be thorough. Silas met the first one in the corridor with a shot that dropped him before he'd cleared the hatch, but the second came in low and fast and the corridor lit up with return fire that chewed a line of scorch marks down the bulkhead a foot from Silas's head. Mercy heard the exchange over the comm from the hold, sharp bursts and Silas's voice, tight and controlled, calling positions. She had her own problem — a fifth crew member of Voss's, come around through the cargo bay's side hatch, catching her half-turned toward the crate. The fight in the hold was ugly and short and not at all like the gunfights she remembered from before she'd stopped being paid to have them. She took a graze along the ribs that she wouldn't fully feel until later, put her attacker down with two shots that used up more of her clip than she'd have liked, and turned back toward the crate just as its lid finally, fully, tore free. What came out of it was not what she'd braced for. Smaller than the noise it made, angular in a way that didn't sit right in the eye, dark-scaled, moving on limbs that bent in more places than limbs should. It didn't look at her. It looked past her, toward the corridor, toward the sound of Ellis's voice crackling distressed over the comm from the bridge — *they're through, they're through, someone's in the corridor* — and it moved that way, fast, low, gone before she could decide whether to raise her weapon. She followed. In the corridor she found chaos: two more of Voss's people down, one of them clearly not by Silas's gun, throat opened in a way no bullet made, the creature already gone again, vanished into the ductwork above with a scrape of claws on metal that faded fast. "It went for them," Ellis said over the comm, voice shaking. "Not me. It went past me, straight at them —" Dobbs's voice cut through, from engineering, tight and strange. "Power's fluctuating on deck two, I got a short in the aft relay, gonna reroute before it takes the whole —" The explosion wasn't large. That was the terrible thing about it, afterward, how small it actually was — a relay junction overloading behind a panel Dobbs had his hands inside when whatever last company soldier remaining fired blind and wild down the engineering corridor and clipped the coolant line Dobbs had spent three days fixing. It went up in a flash of superheated vapor and sparks, not an explosion so much as a violent exhale, and Dobbs was thrown back against the console hard enough that when Mercy reached him — sprinting the length of the ship with her ribs screaming — he was already still. Not blown apart. Not heroic. Just gone, in the unglamorous way a man goes when the wrong wire meets the wrong spark at the wrong moment, his hand still curled loosely around a spanner he'd been using to tighten a fitting that no longer mattered. She didn't have time to feel it yet. That came later. Right then there was still a company soldier somewhere aft, and Silas limping into engineering a moment behind her, and over the ship-wide comm, Voss's voice, thinner now, coming through from his own bridge. "Pull back," he was saying, to his own people, not to her. "Pull back, we're done here—" "Voss." Mercy keyed the channel, her voice rougher than she meant it to be. "You want to keep sending people onto my boat?" A long pause. When Voss spoke again, some of the tired blandness was gone from it, replaced by something more honest and more human — a man doing math on lives instead of quotas. "I've lost four people for a line item, Captain. That's not a trade my superiors will thank me for, but it's one I'm allowed to make." Another pause. "We're disengaging. Consider the file closed on our end." The docking clamp released with a shudder a few minutes later, and Voss's ship peeled away into the dark, and the *Second Chance* drifted on, holed in two places, short one engineer, the thing from the crate gone into the ductwork somewhere and not coming out. Mercy stood in the engineering bay a long while after, looking down at Dobbs, at the spanner still loose in his hand, and didn't say anything at all.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and discipline. The writing is controlled, concrete, and largely free of the vices that plague genre fiction in this space. Let me work through the key elements. The chapter follows its plan faithfully and precisely. Voss's retreat is handled as a cost-benefit calculation rather than a dramatic defeat — "a man doing math on lives instead of quotas" — which is exactly the intention. Dobbs's death is unglamorous, sudden, and unfair: a relay junction, a stray shot, a spanner in a loosening hand. The creature's appearance is deliberately ambiguous — it moves toward Ellis's voice, attacks Voss's people, but the narration refuses to confirm whether this is protection or opportunism. The creature's description ("angular in a way that didn't sit right in the eye, dark-scaled, moving on limbs that bent in more places than limbs should") is appropriately unsettling without being cute or clarifying. All of this matches the plan. Character consistency is strong. Mercy is forward-leaning, unilateral, feeling things she doesn't have time to process. Silas is functional and controlled even wounded. Ellis is scared and vocal. Dobbs dies doing his job, not heroically, which is exactly right for his character. Voss's retreat is the move of a bureaucrat cutting losses, not a villain conceding defeat — his line "I've lost four people for a line item" is the best dialogue in the chapter and rings completely true to his profile. The prose is plain and sensory in the right ways: "the corridor lit up with return fire that chewed a line of scorch marks down the bulkhead," "a violent exhale," "his hand still curled loosely around a spanner." These are earned images, not decorative ones. The description of the fight in the hold as "ugly and short and not at all like the gunfights she remembered from before she'd stopped being paid to have them" is a clean, efficient piece of characterization that does real work. The pacing is well-managed. The chapter moves fast where it needs to and slows at the right moment — Mercy standing over Dobbs at the end, saying nothing, is the correct landing. The restraint there is admirable; a lesser writer would have given her a line. Some minor criticisms: The creature's appearance, while appropriately ambiguous, is slightly rushed — the description is good but the moment passes so quickly that the deliberate ambiguity about its intentions (protecting Ellis or just attacking whoever's closest) doesn't quite land with the weight it deserves. The reader is told Ellis says "it went for them, not me" but we don't experience the moment ourselves, which slightly undercuts the intended interpretive uncertainty. This is a tell-don't-show issue, small but real. The line "She didn't have time to feel it yet. That came later." is the one moment where the prose tips toward stating what it should trust the reader to infer — the chapter has already shown us she doesn't have time, and the editorial comment is unnecessary. Dialogue is sparse and functional, which suits the chapter. Voss's lines are the strongest. Silas's "already moving" efficiency is shown rather than spoken. The comm chatter from Ellis and Dobbs during the fight is appropriately fragmented and panicked. Overall this is a very competent, disciplined chapter that executes a difficult plan — unglamorous death, ambiguous creature, bureaucratic villain retreat — without sentimentality or melodrama. It earns its darkness. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Eight: Cargo Manifest They buried nothing, because there was nothing planetside to bury him in and no ceremony any of them trusted themselves to get through without it turning into something worse. Silas wrapped Dobbs in a spare thermal sheet from the med locker, the same grey stock they used for cargo insulation, and they put him in the empty crate that had started all of it, because it was the only container on the ship that made any kind of sense, and nobody said out loud how wrong that felt. Corvale port authority took the report the way port authorities always did — bored, procedural, more interested in the docking fee than the dead man, and Mercy filled out the incident form with a hand that didn't shake, listing the cause of death as *engineering accident, hull breach, hostile boarding* and leaving out everything true enough to matter. She had done this before, this particular kind of paperwork, the kind that turned a person into a line item so the people left over wouldn't have to explain themselves to anyone with the authority to ask follow-up questions. She didn't examine too closely how easily it came back to her. The ship needed four days of dock work before it'd be fit to fly again — the aft airlock, the scorched corridor, the ruined coolant relay Dobbs had been elbow-deep in when it happened. Silas oversaw most of it one-armed, short-tempered, throwing himself into the labor in a way that read less like grief and more like a man trying to outrun the space where grief would otherwise sit down and get comfortable. "You could talk about it," Ellis told him, on the second day, handing up a spanner Silas hadn't asked for. "Could," Silas agreed, and didn't. They never found the creature. Ellis looked, the first day, quiet sweeps through the ductwork with a torch and an open ration tin held out like an offering, but there was no sign of it — no scat, no scratch marks, nothing but a section of grating near the aft vent bent outward from the inside, wide enough for something to have squeezed through during the chaos of the boarding and slipped out through the same breach Voss's people had cut, into vacuum or into the drifting wreckage of Voss's own damaged shuttle bay, or nowhere at all. There was no way to know if it had lived. There was no way to know if it had ever been anything they could've understood as living, the way they meant the word. "Maybe it's better we don't know," Ellis said, sitting on the edge of the open cargo hatch on the third night, legs hanging out over the docking gantry, looking at the lights of Corvale's lower district smeared orange against the dark. "Maybe," Mercy said. She was cleaning her sidearm again, out of habit more than need, same as always. "You think Dobbs would've—" He stopped. Started again. "You think he'd have understood, if we'd told him the whole reason, before. About the permit. About why you wanted to keep it instead of spacing it." "I don't know," Mercy said honestly. "I never told him the whole reason. Wasn't fair to ask him to trust a decision he didn't have the pieces to weigh." She set the pieces of the gun down, not reassembling it yet. "That's on me, not him." Ellis didn't ask what the whole reason was. She noticed that, filed it away — the first time he hadn't pushed on something he clearly wanted to know, maybe out of respect, maybe because he'd learned, finally, that some doors on this ship didn't open no matter how long you knocked. She went, on the fourth night, alone, to the dockside comms office and filed something she hadn't told either of the others about: a tip, unsigned, routed through three relays to make it hard to trace back, addressed to a rim-worlds advocacy office that dealt in exactly this kind of thing — corporate exogenic trafficking, restricted specimen transport, the kind of small ugly business that mostly went unpunished because nobody with the standing to punish it ever heard about it in time. She attached what she had: the scrap of permit, half-burned, the redacted designation and all. She didn't sign it. She didn't stay to see if anything came of it. She walked back to the ship in the dark with her hands in her pockets and didn't tell Silas or Ellis what she'd done, the same way she hadn't told them plenty else, and told herself that was fine, that some things a person did just for themselves and didn't need witnesses. It wasn't redemption. She knew better than to call it that, even privately. It was one small, specific thing set against a much larger ledger that would never balance, not really, not ever — Adder's Fork sat on one side of that ledger the same as it always had, and a tip filed anonymously from a rim moon didn't so much as dent the total. But it was something she'd chosen to do without being ordered to, without a designation on a manifest telling her what the thing in question was worth, and that had to count for something, even if she couldn't say exactly what. They flew out on the fifth day, short one engineer, the coolant relay held together with a repair Silas swore would hold and Mercy didn't fully trust, the ship's ledger thinner than it had been in months once the dock fees and the med costs came out. Ellis took over the basic engineering watches, badly at first, Dobbs's old cap sitting unclaimed on its hook by the hatch because nobody had decided yet whether moving it counted as disrespect or just tidying up. "Where to?" Ellis asked, from the co-pilot seat, the way he always did. "Wherever's paying," Mercy said, and eased the *Second Chance* out toward the dark, toward the next job, the next cargo, the next crate that would come aboard with no manifest worth trusting and no way of knowing, until it moved, what exactly they'd agreed to carry.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong piece of writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through it carefully. The chapter opens with the burial scene — Dobbs wrapped in thermal sheeting, placed in the crate that started everything. This is a genuinely good image: economical, ironic without being cute about it, and the line "nobody said out loud how wrong that felt" trusts the reader exactly as the plan promised. The paperwork scene that follows is well-handled — Mercy filling out the incident form with practiced ease, the narrator noting she "didn't examine too closely how easily it came back to her." This is efficient, oblique characterization that earns its weight. The Silas grief-as-labor beat is brief and effective. The exchange with Ellis ("You could talk about it." / "Could," Silas agreed, and didn't.) is the best dialogue in the chapter — perfectly calibrated, dry, character-consistent, and doing real emotional work in two words. The creature's fate is handled well: genuinely unresolved, no catharsis, the bent grating as the only evidence. The line "There was no way to know if it had ever been anything they could've understood as living, the way they meant the word" is perhaps the chapter's most philosophically interesting moment, and it lands without being overwrought. The conversation between Mercy and Ellis on the cargo hatch is competent but slightly weaker than the rest. Ellis's question about whether Dobbs would have understood is a reasonable beat, but Mercy's response ("That's on me, not him") edges toward the kind of explicit moral accounting the plan said it would avoid. It's not egregious, but it's the chapter's most "stated" moment. The observation that Ellis doesn't push further — "the first time he hadn't pushed on something he clearly wanted to know" — is a nice character note, though the narrator explaining its significance ("filed it away") slightly over-explains. The anonymous tip scene is the chapter's most debated choice. The plan called for "one small, specific, uncommented-on thing that echoes her past... without narration explaining what it means." The chapter does the action but then provides substantial internal narration explaining exactly what it means and what it isn't ("It wasn't redemption. She knew better than to call it that"). This is the plan's most significant deviation. The narration here is not bad prose — "a much larger ledger that would never balance" is a reasonable metaphor — but it violates the plan's own stated principle of not explaining the theme. The passage tells us Adder's Fork sits on the ledger, tells us this doesn't balance it, tells us what she's choosing and why. The plan specifically said to avoid this kind of internal accounting. It's the chapter's main weakness. The ending is good. Dobbs's cap on the hook is a strong, specific, uncommented-on detail — exactly the kind of thing the plan called for. "Nobody had decided yet whether moving it counted as disrespect or just tidying up" is quietly excellent. The final exchange ("Where to?" / "Wherever's paying") and the last sentence's callback to the crate/cargo motif closes the loop without being too neat about it. On prose quality: the writing is consistently clean, concrete, and western-flavored without being performative about it. There's no purple prose. Metaphors are sparse and mostly earn their place. The "ledger" metaphor in the tip scene is the most extended and the most borderline, but it doesn't collapse into pretension. Character consistency is high. Mercy's unilateral action, her inability to fully explain herself, her practical grief — all consistent. Silas's two-word response to Ellis is perfect. Ellis's hanging-legs-over-the-gantry posture and his offering of the ration tin to the creature are exactly right for his profile. The absence of Dobbs is felt without being sentimentalized. The plan called for no funeral montage, no catharsis shot, no ride-into-the-sunset. The chapter mostly delivers this, though the internal narration in the tip scene is the closest it comes to a thematic statement the plan said to avoid. Overall: a very accomplished chapter with one meaningful deviation from its own stated principles (the over-explained tip scene) and one minor one (Mercy's "that's on me" line). The prose is controlled, the characters are consistent, the ending is earned, and the emotional register is appropriately muted and lived-in. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a confident, accomplished piece of space western fiction that demonstrates genuine craft across most of its dimensions. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses systematically. STRENGTHS: The voice is the piece's greatest asset. Mercy's narration has a consistent, lived-in quality — laconic, self-aware, carrying weight without announcing it. The prose style suits the genre: lean, occasionally lyrical but never overwrought. The Firefly-adjacent world is established efficiently through detail rather than exposition (the grain silo turned auction house, the scoured corp stenciling, the "rim-trash type named Corbeau"). Character differentiation is strong. Dobbs, Silas, Ellis, and Mercy each have distinct voices and behavioral patterns that remain consistent throughout. Dobbs's half-comic bluster masking genuine loyalty, Silas's cold competence and wounded pride, Ellis's naive moral instinct, Mercy's command-by-withholding — these are rendered through action and dialogue rather than description. The crew dynamics feel earned. The Adder's Fork backstory is handled with admirable restraint. It's never explained, only gestured at, and the parallel drawn between the redacted specimen designation and the dehumanizing language of military orders is thematically coherent without being heavy-handed. This is the piece's most sophisticated move. The pacing is generally well-managed. The slow build of dread in chapters 1-3, the violence of chapter 4, the moral complication of chapter 5, the corporate threat of chapter 6, the boarding action of chapter 7, and the quiet aftermath of chapter 8 follow a satisfying dramatic arc. Dobbs's death is handled well — unglamorous, sudden, the "violent exhale" description is genuinely good. The refusal to make it heroic is a mature choice. WEAKNESSES: The creature itself is underwritten. For something that drives the entire plot, it remains almost entirely abstract — "wrongness with weight and speed," "angular in a way that didn't sit right in the eye," "dark-scaled." This vagueness is partly intentional (mystery is appropriate) but it tips into evasion. The creature's apparent decision to attack Voss's boarding party rather than the crew is the story's most interesting moment, but it's handled in a single sentence and never examined with the depth it deserves. The potential sapience question Ellis raises is dropped rather than developed. Some metaphors are slightly strained. "Like an old dog getting up off a porch" for the ship's shudder is fine but familiar. "The first thread of something pull tight in her gut" is a cliché. "Flat and final" is used twice in close proximity. The piece occasionally reaches for a poetic register it doesn't quite need, given how well the plain prose is working. The permit reveal — Mercy sitting on it for two days — creates a manufactured tension that slightly strains credibility. A captain who found evidence of a xeno-containment specimen on her ship after one crew member was injured would have more urgent reasons to share that information. The withholding feels slightly contrived to serve the chapter 5 confrontation scene. The resolution is somewhat convenient. Voss backs off after losing four people with minimal resistance from the *Second Chance*, which feels like the story needed an exit and took the easiest one. The anonymous tip at the end is a bit tidy as a moral gesture — the story acknowledges it's not redemption, which is good, but the acknowledgment itself feels slightly self-congratulatory. The dialogue is generally strong but occasionally over-explains. "Could be a weapon they're recovering same as anything else" / "Could be. Or it could be the last thing this Voss wants is witnesses to whatever they were doing with a live, sapient thing in an unmarked crate" — Ellis is doing thematic work here that the story has already done more elegantly through implication. The Adder's Fork backstory, while handled with restraint, is perhaps too restrained — it's gestured at so obliquely that readers may not fully grasp its significance to Mercy's decision-making. The parallel is clear to an attentive reader but the emotional weight it's meant to carry in chapter 5 and chapter 8 requires the reader to do considerable inferential work. Overall, this is a well-above-average piece of genre fiction with genuine literary ambition, mostly realized. The voice is distinctive, the characters are credible, the thematic concerns are handled with more sophistication than the genre typically demands. Its weaknesses are real but minor relative to its accomplishments. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 16 Pacing: 16 World Building: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 77.9/100
+ Possession Horror — Different Eyes (Chapter Avg: 81.5 | Final: 82.4)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Possession Horror Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: I caught her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Possession Horror Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: I caught her staring at me again. She thought I&#x27;d been sleeping. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Core premise:** Narrator wakes repeatedly to find a woman—wife, sister, mother, roommate, daughter—staring at him with an expression that isn't hers. The horror is slow-burn: something has been watching from behind her eyes, and it's aware that he's aware. **Possible relationships for "her":** - Wife of several years — intimate horror, marriage as the site of invasion, betrayal by the most trusted person. - Younger sister — sibling protectiveness, guilt over not noticing sooner, family denial. - Mother, now elderly, in narrator's care — dementia used as red herring/cover for possession, ambiguity between medical and supernatural explanation. - Daughter — most viscerally horrifying but perhaps overused/exploitative; could work but riskier emotionally. - Roommate/college friend — less emotional stakes but allows outsider POV without deep history baggage, more mystery-driven. **Tone options:** - Quiet, literary dread (Shirley Jackson-esque, restrained, focus on domestic details going wrong). - Escalating visceral horror (body horror, violence, exorcism-style climax). - Ambiguous psychological horror where reader isn't sure if narrator is unreliable, paranoid, or if she's actually possessed—leaving some doubt until deliberately resolved or left partially unresolved. **Possession entity ideas:** - Something that entered through an old house, an heirloom mirror, a doll, a Ouija session mentioned in backstory. - Something that isn't demonic in traditional sense—more parasitic, ancient, patient; feeds on being watched/witnessed, which is why staring matters thematically (it needs to be seen to nest, or it studies the narrator to replace him next). - Twist: the entity intends to move into the narrator, not stay in her—the staring is not malice but *hunger*, it's learning his patterns to wear him next. This creates delicious dramatic irony over the chapters as we realize the staring is reconnaissance. - Alternative twist: she's not possessed—*he* is the one who's changed/died and this is her seeing his ghost/possessed corpse, i.e., he is the monster and doesn't know it. Interesting inversion of unreliable narrator. - Alternative: mutual possession—both husband and wife are hosts to entities that recognize each other, staring is entities communicating, humans are passengers. **Structural beats across 8 chapters (rough):** 1. Establish normalcy + the first "caught staring" moment. Plant unease. Establish relationship intimately. 2. Escalation: more staring; small wrongnesses—she uses words she wouldn't, stands oddly still, cold hands, a hum/song she never knew. 3. Investigating: narrator researches/recalls something—an event, house history, a relative who "went strange," an incident weeks ago (illness, accident, seance, thunderstorm) that started it. 4. Confrontation attempt: he asks her directly; she deflects convincingly or claims she doesn't remember staring—gaslighting ambiguity; audience unsure if real. 5. First overt supernatural event—undeniable proof (something physically impossible, a voice not hers, mirror image lagging behind her, or she recites his private thoughts/memories back to him). 6. Desperate measures: seeks help—priest, therapist, old friend/relative who's seen this before; learns the mythology/rule of the entity (why it stares, what happens when the staring stops). 7. Climax: she moves against him, or he attempts ritual/removal, revealing the true nature of the threat (the twist—entity is reconnaissance for possessing HIM, or that he's already partially "gone" and doesn't realize it). 8. Resolution: dark ending—ambiguous or tragic. Perhaps he wakes to find himself staring at someone else now, closing the loop; or she's saved but something remains; or full tragic possession completes, told from newly-hollowed narrator perspective revealing he's now the staring thing. **Possible endings:** - Circular/uroboros ending: last lines mirror opening line but with roles reversed ("She caught me staring at her again. She thinks I've been sleeping.") — strong, thematically satisfying, common in good possession fiction, gives dread of inevitability. - Bleak ending where he "cures" her by transferring the entity into himself, sacrificing self for her, story ends on his hollowing. - Ambiguous ending where reader isn't sure who was possessed the whole time. **Setting ideas:** old farmhouse recently inherited, isolated rural home, a fixer-upper with a history, dead of winter (isolation), storms cutting off phone/power at key moments. Could also be modern apartment but isolation is easier to justify in rural/old house setting. **Secondary characters:** an old neighbor/local historian who knows about the house's past; a sibling of the wife who noticing something wrong from outside perspective (calls, visits); a family doctor dismissing symptoms. **Backstory hook:** They moved into his late grandmother's house six months ago. Grandmother was known locally for "talking to someone in the walls" in her final years, dismissed as dementia. Foreshadowed early, paid off late. # Reflection The strongest, most cohesive version combines: intimate spousal relationship (highest emotional stakes, plausible deniability, claustrophobic domesticity), an inherited old house with a whispered family history (gives grounding mythology without overexplaining), and the uroboros/uncanny-mirror ending where the possession is revealed to be a slow exchange/study process rather than one-off invasion — the entity studies its next host by wearing the current one, which explains WHY staring is the horror engine of the story (thematically ties title action to plot). This also creates a delicious dramatic irony for a careful reader: every "she's staring at me" moment is actually the entity cataloguing him to become him, and the final chapter reveal recontextualizes the entire story. Using the wife works better than the daughter/mother for word economy — it lets me build history through a few well-chosen scenes without needing extensive childhood/family exposition, and marriage carries built-in intimacy/trust to violate. I'll name her (say, "Mara" or "Elena") and give the narrator a name too, minimal but grounding (say "Thomas" or just first-person unnamed—unnamed narrator adds to the uncanny "could be you" quality but might feel distant in intimate marriage scenes; I'll give him a name for grounding, her too, since intimacy is key). I like the ambiguity angle (is she ill/grieving vs. possessed) sustained through the middle chapters via a plausible medical/psychological cover story (grief after miscarriage, or sleep paralysis on his side muddying reliability, or her started on new medication) — this raises tension about who's unreliable, then chapter 5's undeniable proof snaps the ambiguity into confirmed horror, giving good pacing shape (slow dread → confirmed horror → desperate action → tragic/inevitable ending). The grandmother backstory is efficient: gives lore, gives an old house setting justification, gives a local character (maybe an aunt or an old neighbor) who can deliver exposition in chapter 6 without infodump feeling forced, since she witnessed grandmother's decline personally. For the ending, the circular ending (final line echoes opening line, roles reversed) is the most elegant and thematically potent choice — it also lets me end on ambiguity/horror without needing a full "cure" plot which would be hard to earn in remaining word count. I will have the narrator realize partway through the last chapter that the process is a slow becoming — she is not being replaced, she IS the entity's current vessel and it's been "trying on" his mannerisms via her, and by story's end it moves into him, meaning HE is now the one who will begin doing to her (or to their unborn child, or to a next unsuspecting person) what was done to her. This gives horror payoff and closes loop thematically with the title/prompt. I'll avoid making it too gory/violent — restrained literary horror suits the "quiet staring" premise better than gore; body horror moments should be minimal, precise, and unsettling rather than splattery. Focus on wrongness of stillness, voice, small physical details (temperature, breathing, blinking) rather than viscera. I will keep POV strictly first-person past tense from Thomas throughout for intimacy and unreliability potential, except perhaps a brief coda/final lines shift to present tense to mark the horror completing ("She catches me staring now."). Decision made: proceed with the spousal / inherited house / entity-that-studies-to-transfer plot, uroboros ending, restrained tone with escalating undeniable supernatural confirmation at the mid-point, grief/medical ambiguity as red herring in early-mid chapters, aunt/neighbor as lore-deliverer in chapter 6, climax in chapter 7 involves failed ritual/confrontation, chapter 8 is the transfer and circular closing. # Plan **Title:** "The Waking Hour" (working title) **POV/Tense:** First person, past tense, narrator Thomas Reyes, mid-30s. Present tense slip in final lines. **Characters:** - Thomas (narrator) — grounded, a little skeptical, a woodworker/teacher, recently lost a pregnancy with his wife which frames his guilt/exhaustion as unreliable-narrator cover. - Elena (wife) — grieving, sleep-disturbed, increasingly "off"; kind, formerly playful, now flattened. - Grandmother Reyes (deceased, backstory only) — original owner of the house, died calling out to "the one in the glass," locally thought senile. - Dot/Aunt Rosa — an elderly neighbor/relative who tended grandmother in her last years, holds the lore. - The Entity — never named, referred to as "it" / "the watcher"; manifests through stillness, mimicry, the staring. **Setting:** Inherited farmhouse in a rural valley, moved in six months prior, isolated, old mirror/wardrobe in the back bedroom as focal object, winter setting for isolation (storms, early dark). **Chapter breakdown (~1000 words each):** **Chapter 1 — "Watching"** Establish Thomas and Elena's marriage post-miscarriage, moved into grandmother's house. Open exactly on the prompt moment: he wakes, catches Elena staring, she claims she thought he was asleep, laughs it off. Small domestic warmth undercut by a wrongness—her stillness lasted too long, her blink was late. Plant grandmother's house history subtly (an old photo, a locked spare room). End chapter on quiet unease, not overt horror. **Chapter 2 — "Small Hours"** Pattern repeats—several nights of catching her staring, always with the same excuse. Days are normal, but small tells accumulate: she stands at the hallway mirror too long; she's begun humming a tune he doesn't recognize; her appetite is off. He rationalizes via grief/insomnia/therapy talk. Introduce Aunt Rosa via a phone call checking in, mentions grandmother "used to do that too, near the end." Foreshadow the spare room / old wardrobe mirror grandmother kept covered. **Chapter 3 — "Cover Story"** Deeper into the ambiguity: Thomas researches Elena's medication, thinks about calling her doctor, feels guilty for suspecting his grieving wife of something sinister. Flashback/memory of the miscarriage night, guilt strengthens his desire to explain everything rationally. But an unsettling scene: he wakes to find her not in bed, discovers her standing in the spare room in front of the covered mirror, hand on the sheet over it, speaking softly in a voice pitched wrong. She returns to bed as if nothing happened when he calls her name. **Chapter 4 — "The Word She Shouldn't Know"** Escalation: Elena says something in her sleep/staring state that she could not possibly know—a phrase from Thomas's private childhood, or his late grandmother's specific voice/nickname for him. This cracks his rationalizations. He confronts her; she has no memory, is frightened for the first time, cries, seems genuinely herself and scared—raising sympathy and complicating "is it her or the thing" question. They agree to have the room, the mirror, looked at/removed. **Chapter 5 — "Undeniable"** The proof-of-supernatural chapter. While trying to remove/cover/destroy the mirror, something overt happens—her reflection lags behind her movements by a half-second, or the reflection stares directly at Thomas while Elena's actual eyes are closed/elsewhere, or the room temperature drops and the sheet/mirror image shows a second figure standing behind Elena that isn't there in the room. Undeniable confirmation for Thomas (and reader) that something else is present. Elena collapses/faints; Thomas panics, first real terror beat of story. **Chapter 6 — "What Grandmother Knew"** Thomas visits Aunt Rosa for answers. She reveals the family history: grandmother inherited the wardrobe/mirror from her own mother, along with a warning never handed down clearly, something about "it needs to learn you before it can wear you," that it watches the sleeper to study them, that grandmother spent her last years feeding it small hours of staring until "she forgot which of them she was." Rosa reveals a ritual/rule: don't let it finish watching you sleep uninterrupted a full night; break its study before the last night. Thomas realizes with dread how many nights they've already had. **Chapter 7 — "The Last Night"** Thomas tries to intervene—stays awake to break the pattern, tries a folk ritual Rosa gave him (salt, mirror-covering, staying awake beside Elena), but exhaustion/grief/dramatic irony wins: he begins to fall asleep despite his resolve. In his last waking moments he realizes with horror that Elena's "sessions" of stillness were never random—they were nightly increments, and tonight is the final one, the completion night. He fights to stay awake, fails, or partially succeeds but too late—something changes hands in the dark, communicated by imagery rather than exposition: Elena's stillness finally breaks, her eyes closing naturally for the first time in weeks, an exhale of relief in her sleep. Thomas doesn't understand yet why he feels suddenly hollow, cold, aware. **Chapter 8 — "Sleeping"** Morning after, told with quiet wrongness: Elena is warm, present, healed—laughing again, herself, unaware anything happened, believing it was all grief and bad nights now passed. Thomas smiles along, but internally something is different: he finds himself watching her sleep, cataloguing her small movements, unable to stop, feeling a patient curious hunger he doesn't recognize as his own. Final scene mirrors opening: Elena wakes, catches him staring. He says he was just sleeping. Final lines shift to present tense, echo/invert the story's opening line to close the uroboros: "She caught me staring at her again. She thinks I've been sleeping." End on that chilling inversion, no further explanation. **Themes to sustain throughout:** grief and how it hollows the griever, the horror of not recognizing your loved one/yourself, surveillance/being witnessed as violation, inheritance (literal house/mirror, figurative trauma passed down family lines), the domestic made uncanny. **Pacing note:** Chapters 1–4 slow-burn ambiguity (grief vs. haunting); chapter 5 pivot to confirmed supernatural; 6 exposition/lore; 7 climax; 8 quiet devastating coda. Word count target ~1000/chapter, ~8000 total.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a restrained, literary possession horror story titled **"The Waking Hour"** (working title), told in first-person past tense by Thomas Reyes, a man living with his wife Elena in his late grandmother's inherited farmhouse, six months after Elena suffered a miscarriage. The horror will be quiet and cumulative rather than gory: stillness, timing, small wrongnesses in voice and blink and breath, rather than viscera or jump-scare mechanics. I want the reader to sit in genuine ambiguity for the first third of the story — is Elena's strangeness grief, insomnia, medication, or something else? — before snapping that ambiguity into confirmed supernatural horror around the midpoint, then moving into folk-horror-inflected exposition, a failed ritual/climax, and a quiet devastating coda. **Key choices:** - **Voice:** intimate, controlled, slightly guilt-ridden first-person past tense. Thomas rationalizes and second-guesses himself; his grief over the miscarriage is the emotional engine that makes his denial plausible and sympathetic, not just a plot device. - **Structure:** the title action ("caught her staring") is the throughline — it recurs and escalates literally, and thematically the entity's "staring" is revealed to be *reconnaissance*, studying its current host in order to move on to its next one. This reframes every earlier staring scene retroactively once the reader reaches the final chapters. - **Central mythology:** an old covered mirror/wardrobe inherited from the grandmother's line, tied to a family pattern of one member "going strange" near the end and the next generation inheriting the house (and the thing). Aunt Rosa serves as the lore-deliverer in Chapter 6, avoiding infodump by grounding it in personal memory of the grandmother's decline. - **Ending:** a uroboros/circular structure. The final lines invert the opening line and shift briefly into present tense, confirming that the possession has passed from Elena to Thomas — he is now the one staring, hollowed and patient, and the horror will continue, unresolved and cyclical. No cure, no exorcism success — this is a story about inevitability and inheritance, not triumph. - **Things to avoid:** gore, over-explaining the entity's nature/rules (Rosa's exposition will be partial, folkloric, uncertain — not a rulebook), melodrama in dialogue, and resolving the ambiguity too early. I want the horror to live in stillness and implication. - **Pacing:** slow-burn chapters 1–4 (ambiguity), pivot at chapter 5 (confirmation), lore chapter 6, climax chapter 7, coda chapter 8. ~1000 words/chapter, ~8000 total. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — "Watching"** Cold open on the prompt: Thomas wakes to Elena staring at him; she laughs it off, claims she thought he was asleep. Establish their marriage, the grief they're both nursing, the inherited farmhouse setting. Small wrongness: her stillness lasted a beat too long, her blink came late. End on unease, not dread. **Chapter 2 — "Small Hours"** The staring recurs over several nights, always explained away. Daytime Elena seems mostly normal but small tells build: humming an unfamiliar tune, lingering too long at the hallway mirror, appetite off. Thomas rationalizes it as grief/insomnia. Phone call from Aunt Rosa checking in plants the grandmother parallel ("she used to do that too, near the end") and the existence of a covered mirror in the locked spare room. **Chapter 3 — "Cover Story"** Thomas leans harder into rational explanations — medication, therapy, his own guilt over the miscarriage night surfacing in memory. But he wakes to find Elena gone from bed, discovers her in the spare room with her hand on the sheet over the old mirror, murmuring in a voice pitched subtly wrong. She returns to bed as if nothing happened when he calls her name. **Chapter 4 — "The Word She Shouldn't Know"** Escalation: in one of her staring states, Elena murmurs something impossible for her to know — his grandmother's private nickname for him, or a memory from his childhood. This cracks Thomas's denial. He confronts her; she's horrified, has no memory, cries — genuinely frightened, deepening sympathy and the ambiguity of blame. They agree to deal with the mirror/room. **Chapter 5 — "Undeniable"** The pivot chapter. While attempting to cover or remove the mirror, something unmistakably supernatural happens — Elena's reflection moves out of sync with her, or stares directly at Thomas while her real eyes are shut, or a second shape stands behind her reflection that isn't in the room. Elena collapses. First true terror beat; ambiguity is resolved — this is real. **Chapter 6 — "What Grandmother Knew"** Thomas visits Aunt Rosa. She recounts grandmother's decline and family history — the mirror/wardrobe passed down, a half-remembered warning that "it has to learn you before it can wear you," that it watches sleepers to study them, that grandmother eventually "forgot which of them she was." Rosa offers a folk countermeasure: don't let it complete an unbroken night of watching. Thomas realizes with dread how many nights have already passed. **Chapter 7 — "The Last Night"** Thomas tries to intervene — stays awake beside Elena, attempts Rosa's makeshift ritual (salt, covering mirrors, vigilance). Exhaustion and grief undercut his resolve; he begins to fail despite himself. In his final waking moments he realizes the nightly staring sessions were increments toward a completion, and tonight is the last one. He fights sleep, loses (or half-succeeds, too late). Something changes hands in the dark, rendered through image rather than exposition — Elena's stillness finally breaks into natural sleep, an exhale of relief. Thomas feels an unfamiliar hollowness and cold awareness settle in himself. **Chapter 8 — "Sleeping"** Morning after: Elena is warm, present, herself again — grief lifted, believing the bad nights are simply over. Thomas smiles along but notices himself watching her sleep, cataloguing her small movements with a patient curiosity that doesn't feel like his own. Final scene mirrors the opening: she wakes and catches him staring. He tells her he was just sleeping. The prose shifts briefly to present tense for the closing lines, inverting the story's first line to close the loop: *"She caught me staring at her again. She thinks I've been sleeping."*
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Might Approach This Piece A human writer given this exact prompt — "I caught her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping." — would probably start from *voice* and a very specific, narrow image rather than architecture. They might not know the whole plot before writing chapter one; they'd write the opening scene first, feel out who this narrator sounds like, what kind of marriage this is, what small telling detail makes the staring wrong, and let the story's shape emerge from that texture. The prompt itself is intimate and small — a bedroom, a look, a lie ("she thought I'd been sleeping" implies *he wasn't*, which is a small, human, slightly guilty detail already baked into the prompt that a plan-first approach can easily flatten or ignore). A human working in the horror short-story tradition (Jackson, Aickman, early King, Karen Russell-ish domestic uncanny) tends to: - Trust silence and withholding far more than a plan usually allows. They'd cut exposition wherever possible, resist explaining the entity's "rules," and possibly never explain it at all. - Let physical, sensory, idiosyncratic detail carry dread instead of stating "unease" or "dread" as emotions. E.g., not "he felt a wrongness" but the specific: the way she chewed toast in eight small bites when she used to eat it in three. - Allow ugly, petty, or mundane human behavior to coexist with horror — irritation, resentment, dark jokes, denial that isn't purely noble grief but also selfishness, avoidance, low-grade marital friction that predates the haunting. This makes it feel lived-in rather than mechanically thematic. - Resist tidy symmetry. Human short stories about hauntings often have loose threads, images that don't fully pay off, digressions that exist for texture/character rather than plot function. - Undercut the "big reveal" chapter structure — a human writer might bury the confirmation scene in an offhand, almost dissociative paragraph rather than staging it as a Big Horror Set Piece with temperature drops and glowing reveals. - Consider whether the ending *needs* to be the neat mirrored inversion at all, or whether that's actually the *expected*, slightly too-clever ending that a workshop would flag as pat. A human might choose a stranger, less symmetrical ending, or one that's emotionally ambiguous rather than structurally satisfying. # Telltale LLM Approaches That Might Hurt This Piece Generally, LLM-generated horror/literary fiction tends toward: 1. **Over-resolution / bow-tying.** LLMs like closed loops, symmetry, callbacks that neatly land. The uroboros ending I've planned is *exactly* this instinct — elegant, satisfying, thematically "correct" — which is also why it might read as a little pat, a little "of course that's how it ends," robbing the story of true dread (true dread often comes from the *not-quite-symmetrical*, the loose end that won't resolve). 2. **Telling emotions rather than inhabiting them.** LLM prose often narrates the emotional beat directly ("dread coiled in his stomach," "he felt a wrongness he couldn't name") rather than trusting a physical or behavioral detail to do the work. This is a real risk across all 8 chapters as planned — the chapter summaries themselves are full of stated emotional beats ("first true terror beat," "deepening sympathy") that could easily translate into over-explained prose. 3. **Safe, moderate horror.** LLMs tend to hedge — horror that's tasteful, restrained to the point of being toothless, avoiding true strangeness or specificity because specificity risks being "wrong" or off-brand. The plan's insistence on "restrained, no gore" is a good instinct tonally, but there's a risk of restraint tipping into blandness — generic "stillness" and "wrongness" without a truly strange, specific, memorable image that only this story owns. Need one or two images so specific they couldn't be generated for any other possession story. 4. **Exposition delivered efficiently and completely.** The Aunt Rosa chapter as planned risks being a clean lore-dump dressed up as a conversation — efficient exposition is an LLM tell. Humans writing folk horror often leave the lore *partial, contradictory, half-remembered, delivered reluctantly or evasively*, with the human character actively not wanting to explain, or misremembering, or being an unreliable source herself. 5. **Even pacing / uniform chapter shapes.** Planning 8 chapters at ~1000 words each with clearly defined "jobs" (this chapter does X escalation) risks a metronomic feel — each chapter doing its assigned work efficiently, like a five-paragraph essay. Human short stories breathe unevenly: some scenes rush, some linger on something seemingly unimportant (a meal, a memory, an argument about something unrelated) that turns out to matter tonally even if not "plot." 6. **Overwritten "impressive" prose.** LLMs, especially at low-to-mid reasoning effort corrected for "literary" tone, can lean on ornate metaphor stacking, abstract nouns, and rhythm-for-rhythm's-sake sentences that sound writerly but don't cohere to a consistent, specific narrating voice. Need to commit hard to Thomas's specific idiom — is he plain-spoken? self-deprecating? clinical (he's a woodworker per the plan — could use trade-specific, tactile metaphors)? The prose should sound like it's coming from one particular tired man, not "literary horror voice" in general. 7. **Villain/entity clarity creep.** LLMs often want to eventually clarify the monster's motive and rules for reader satisfaction (the "reconnaissance" framing I devised is already doing this — giving the entity a legible, almost logical objective). Real folk horror often keeps the entity's motive genuinely unknowable — even the "explanation" Rosa gives could be presented as folk superstition that may or may not be accurate, rather than confirmed authorial truth. 8. **Sympathetic, redemptive character arcs even in horror.** LLMs like to make sure characters are likable/sympathetic and grief is treated respectfully and validated — this can sand down edges. A human writer might let Thomas be more culpable, more avoidant, even unlikeable in small ways (resentment toward Elena's grief, impatience, a wandering eye, a lie of his own) — giving the "possession" thematic teeth (the entity exploits a real fissure in the marriage, not just misfortune). # Deep Dive Critique of the Current Plan - **The chapter-by-chapter plan is very "well-behaved."** Each chapter has a clean single job (establish/escalate/pivot/explain/climax/coda) with almost no room for a scene that exists purely for character texture, contradiction, or tonal variance. This is efficient but risks feeling like a checklist executed rather than a story that surprised its author. A human draft would likely have, say, chapter 3 spend real time on an argument about something totally mundane (money, his mother, an unfinished woodworking commission) that has nothing to do with the haunting, purely to make the marriage feel real before the haunting eats it. - **Aunt Rosa risks being a device.** As planned she exists purely to deliver lore. A human touch: give her her own irritating or endearing specificity — maybe she doesn't want to talk about it, maybe she's angry at Thomas for something unrelated (not visiting grandmother enough before she died), maybe her account contradicts itself and Thomas has to decide what to believe. Rosa should feel like a person with her own agenda, not an exposition faucet. - **The miscarriage backstory is doing a lot of thematic labor efficiently — maybe too efficiently.** It's the "acceptable," well-worn grief-trauma engine that makes rationalization plausible; it is also a very common LLM (and honestly, general contemporary horror) go-to precisely because it's efficient and instantly sympathetic. Worth asking: is there a more specific, less generic source of guilt/fissure in the marriage that would feel more like *this* couple and less like "sad backstory module #4"? E.g., something smaller and stranger — an affair he almost had, a resentment about the house itself (Elena never wanted to move into his dead grandmother's house), infertility treatment they disagreed about, financial desperation forcing the move. The miscarriage isn't necessarily wrong, but it should be rendered with specific texture (what they were doing when it happened, an object connected to it) rather than functioning as a generic Grief Container. - **The "undeniable proof" scene (Ch 5) as planned is a fairly conventional horror-movie beat** (reflection lagging, second figure in mirror) — functional but generic, exactly the kind of imagery that could be generated for any haunted-mirror story. This is the chapter most in need of a genuinely odd, specific, invented image unique to this story — something a human brainstorming session might land on through free-association rather than "what's the standard horror mirror trick." - **The ending's neatness is the single biggest risk.** The uroboros mirror-line ending is satisfying in a workshop-y, "good craft" way, which is precisely why it might read as algorithmic — it's the *obvious* smart ending once you set up the premise this way, and LLMs gravitate hard toward the obvious-smart-satisfying close. Alternatives to consider: end mid-ambiguity without confirming Thomas has "become" anything, just planting one wrong detail and cutting away; or end on something smaller and stranger than a full inversion — e.g., he simply doesn't wake her up one morning when he could have, a small cruelty that's the first sign, rather than a full symmetrical restaging of the opening scene. - **Rules/mythology (the "it studies you to wear you" logic) is quite tidy and legible** — a human folk-horror approach might resist ever confirming this is *true*; it might remain Rosa's superstition, or grandmother's madness, with the "explanation" never validated by the narrative itself, only the dread validated. Keeping the mechanism fuzzy avoids the "movie exposition scene where the rules of the monster are established" trope. - **Emotional beats are named in the plan** ("deepening sympathy," "first true terror beat") — a reminder to self, when drafting, to find the specific behavior/image that produces that emotion rather than asserting it. - **Uniform restraint could tip into flatness.** "No gore, quiet dread throughout" is a good guiding principle but taken too literally across 8000 words risks monotony — a human writer would probably let at least one moment be almost darkly funny, or embarrassingly mundane, or emotionally warm (real tenderness between Thomas and Elena) to modulate the dread, so the horror has contrast to work against. Horror unrelieved becomes wallpaper. # Brainstorming Ways to Make It More Human - Give Thomas a distinct, idiosyncratic voice tied to his trade (woodworking) — let his metaphors come from grain, joints, warping wood, sanding, varnish smell — rather than generic literary imagery. Let some sentences be blunt, even ugly, not polished. - Introduce at least one scene of real marital friction unrelated to the horror — a stupid argument, a joke that lands wrong, sexual tension/distance — to make the relationship feel specific and lived rather than a Grief Couple archetype. - Make Aunt Rosa cranky, reluctant, contradictory, or funny in a way that resists her being pure lore-delivery — maybe she initially denies anything's wrong, or tells the story wrong twice and Thomas has to piece it together, or she's more interested in complaining about grandmother's will than the supernatural stakes. - Consider swapping the "unbroken night of watching" mechanism (a fairly gamified, rules-based horror device — like something out of a screenplay) for something less mechanical/legible — maybe there's no clear rule at all, just Rosa's fear and half-memory, and Thomas never gets certainty about what will or won't stop it. -
