Whatever it was doing, it finished, and then there wasn't an Inés left for it to be doing it to anymore" is genuinely chilling and avoids the gamified rulebook the plan explicitly rejected. This is the chapter's strongest horror beat — the implication that the entity doesn't stop, it just completes, is more frightening than any ritual instruction would be. The ending paragraph is strong. "The sky was starting to go the color of the mirror's silvering, that pale, wrong green-grey" — this is one of the chapter's few metaphorical flourishes, and it earns its place because it connects the external world to the object of dread without being decorative. Thomas standing in the doorway watching Elena breathe, "checking it against nothing, against no rhythm but my own held breath," closes the chapter on the right note of exhausted, unresolved dread. **Weaknesses:** The will grievance scene, while consistent with Rosa's profile, runs slightly long before yielding. The plan called for Rosa to be "cranky, evasive, more interested in relitigating an old grudge" — this is present, but the transition from grievance to information feels a touch mechanical: she complains, Thomas says "I know," she relents. The plan's vision of information coming out "sideways" is partially achieved but could be stranger, more oblique. The self-contradiction element from the plan is absent. Rosa tells her story once, coherently, without the "contradicting herself once and not noticing" beat the plan specified. This was meant to be a key character texture — the way grief and age blur her reliability — and its absence makes her slightly more authoritative than intended. "Some houses aren't empty just because nobody's living in them" is the chapter's one genuinely weak line — it's a horror-genre commonplace, the kind of thing that sounds like it belongs in a different, less carefully crafted story. It's the only moment where the prose slips into borrowed language rather than Rosa's specific idiom. Thomas's voice remains consistent throughout — the parenthetical self-corrections, the awareness of his own inadequacy, the ledger-keeping quality. His admission that he "couldn't have told her all of it and stayed steady" is a good character beat, showing his emotional management without stating it flatly. The dialogue is generally strong and specific to these characters. Rosa's lines feel earned. The exchange about the name is particularly well-handled — her refusal is superstitious and stubborn in exactly the right way. **Overall:** This is a well-executed chapter that delivers on most of its promises. The horror is appropriately unresolved, the characters are consistent, the prose is controlled. The missing self-contradiction beat and one clichéd line are the main demerits. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Seven: Staying Awake I didn't tell Elena what Rosa said. I told her Rosa didn't know anything useful, which was almost true, and I told her I thought the mirror should go, we'd take it to the dump ourselves this week, and she agreed, tired, willing to believe in a solution with a shape to it the same way everyone wants to. What I didn't say was the plan I'd actually made, alone, driving back in that grey five a.m. light: I would stay awake. I would watch her instead of the other way around. If it needed her sleeping and unwatched to do whatever it did, then I would simply not allow that condition to exist. It wasn't a plan so much as a superstition dressed up as one, the kind of thing you do instead of nothing. I made coffee I didn't need and told her I had a commission to finish, a lie she didn't examine, and I sat up in the workshop light spilling faint through the window until she'd gone up ahead of me, and then I came in quiet and lay down beside her fully dressed, on top of the covers, and watched the ceiling and told myself I would not close my eyes. For a while it was almost peaceful. She fell asleep the ordinary way, her hand finding mine without her seeming to wake for it, a habit eight years deep, and I lay there holding it and thinking, absurdly, that this was still the marriage I'd chosen, whatever else was happening in the house around it. I thought about the gutter I still hadn't fixed. I thought about the way she did the judges' voices better than the judges. I thought, once, distantly, guiltily, about the fact that I had never told her about Tomacito, and that the first time she ever said it, it wasn't her saying it at all, and there was something almost unbearably sad in that, some private thing of my grandmother's spoken back to me through a mouth that had no right to it. I don't know what time she turned to face me. I only know that I noticed it the way you notice weather changing behind you — a pressure, not a sound. I opened my eyes and she was there, propped, watching, the way she'd been that first night, the way she'd been a dozen nights since, and my whole body went rigid with the effort of not looking away, of proving to myself and to it, whatever it was, that I could hold this, that watching back was a kind of defense. She didn't speak. That was different. Every other time, eventually, there'd been words — my name, wrong-voiced, my grandmother's name for me, the low even register that wasn't hers. This time she just watched, and I watched back, and somewhere in the held quiet of it I thought I understood something I hadn't let myself think before: that it wasn't cruel. Whatever was looking out of her at me in that moment wasn't cruel at all. It was patient the way you're patient with something you're learning, the way I sand a joint smooth and then smooth it further, not out of malice toward the wood but because you don't stop until you know a thing completely. That thought should have frightened me more than anything else that had happened. I think, somewhere underneath, it did. But exhaustion has its own gravity, and I had been awake now the better part of two days, and the room was warm, and her hand was still in mine, warmer than it had any right to be, and some traitor part of my body decided, without consulting me, that this was safe enough to rest in. I want to say I fought it. I did fight it — I remember pinching the inside of my own wrist hard enough to bruise, counting backward from a hundred, reciting the names of wood grains the way other men might recite scripture, ash, oak, walnut, maple, anything to keep a thread of my mind occupied and awake. But there's a particular moment, right at the edge of sleep, where the effort of resisting becomes indistinguishable from the falling itself, and I felt myself arrive at that edge and understood, with a strange calm that had no business being calm, that I wasn't going to win this the way I'd wanted to win it. The last thing I remember clearly is her face. Not staring anymore. Something had shifted in it, some tension gone loose, the way a shoulder drops after a long day of carrying something heavy. She looked, for one unbearable second before my eyes finally closed, like a person setting something down. Relieved. Almost grateful. I thought, faintly, foolishly, that we'd done it — that staying awake, watching back, had somehow worked, had made whatever it was give up and let her go. I was asleep before I could finish the thought, and I didn't dream, or if I did, I don't remember it, which frightens me more now than almost anything else in this account, because I have always remembered my dreams, every single one, my whole life, until that night. I woke hours later to full daylight, the smell of coffee already made downstairs, and a stillness in my own chest I couldn't place, quiet and wide and patient, like a held breath I hadn't noticed myself taking.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents genuinely accomplished horror writing that earns most of what it attempts. Let me work through it carefully. The chapter opens with Thomas's internal rationalization — "a superstition dressed up as a plan" — which is exactly the kind of self-aware, slightly defeated voice the character profile demands. The prose here is controlled and specific to Thomas: the lie about the commission, the detail of lying on top of the covers fully dressed, the counting of wood grains as a wakefulness ritual. These are not generic horror-narrator details; they belong to this specific man. The central reversal — Thomas watching Elena instead of the reverse — is executed with real craft. The plan's failure is not supernatural in its mechanism; it's human. Exhaustion, warmth, the traitor body. The chapter plan called for "marital tenderness distracting him" and "a moment of real intimacy that lowers his guard emotionally rather than through any supernatural trick," and the chapter delivers this through the hand-finding-his detail, the memory of her doing judges' voices, the thought about Tomacito. These are earned, specific, warm details that make the horror's encroachment feel genuinely costly. The most striking passage is the one where Thomas reflects that the entity "wasn't cruel" — that it was patient "the way you're patient with something you're learning." This is the chapter's best moment: it's genuinely unsettling because it reframes the possession not as malevolent but as curious, and it uses Thomas's own professional metaphor (sanding) to articulate something he shouldn't be thinking sympathetically. The plan called for Thomas to notice Elena looks "relieved, like someone near the end of a long shift," and this is delivered with precision — "like a person setting something down." The ending is strong. The "stillness in my own chest I couldn't place, quiet and wide and patient, like a held breath I hadn't noticed myself taking" signals the transfer without stating it, and the detail about not remembering his dreams — framed as something he's always done — is a quietly devastating marker of what's been lost. Where the chapter is slightly less successful: the passage about counting backward and reciting wood grains runs a little long and tips toward the self-consciously literary. The line "reciting the names of wood grains the way other men might recite scripture" is the kind of metaphor that calls attention to itself as a metaphor — it's not quite forced, but it's the chapter's most visible seam. Similarly, "exhaustion has its own gravity" is a competent but not exceptional formulation. The chapter has almost no dialogue, which is appropriate given the plan (this is a vigil chapter), but it means the "Weak Dialogue" metric is essentially inapplicable. The prose carries the weight well. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Thomas fails despite genuine effort, the intimacy lowers his guard, he drifts off, he notices Elena's relief before sleeping, he wakes to something changed. The plan's note that "the reader should feel it before Thomas states it" is honored — the final paragraph signals the transfer through sensation and absence rather than declaration. One minor concern: the chapter is almost entirely interior monologue and reflection. This is appropriate to the plan and the character, but it means the chapter's texture is somewhat uniform — long, ruminative sentences with occasional short breaks. The plan called for "shorter sentences, less interiority" in the pivot chapter (Chapter 5), but Chapter 7 is meant to be different, so this is not a flaw exactly. Still, a reader might feel the chapter is slightly monotonous in its register. The "Tomacito" callback is well-placed — it's the emotional wound that makes Thomas's guard drop, and it's specific to these characters rather than generic grief. Overall, this is a chapter that does what it sets out to do with real skill, avoids most of the genre's clichés, and earns its horror through character rather than atmosphere-stacking. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Eight: Sleeping She was different that morning in a way that took me most of the day to properly name, because at first it only registered as good — lighter, warmer, herself in a way I hadn't fully seen in weeks. She was singing along to the radio, actually singing, words and melody both, no unfamiliar humming, and she'd made too much coffee the way she does on days she's happy rather than rushed, and when I came down she turned and smiled at me with her whole face, no held second in it, no wrongness, just Elena. "You look terrible," she said, kissing my cheek anyway. "You were dead to the world. I don't think you moved once all night." "Bad sleep." "Best sleep I've had in months, actually." She said it easily, pouring me a cup, and something in me went very still hearing it, though I smiled and let her keep talking. "I don't know. I feel like something lifted. Is that a stupid thing to say?" "It's not stupid." "I think we should still get rid of that mirror. But I don't feel — " she searched for it, stirring her coffee she didn't need to stir, " — hunted anymore. Does that make sense?" "It makes sense." I meant it kindly, and it was kind, and it was also the last entirely honest thing I said to her that day, because underneath it I was doing something I didn't have a name for yet, something I only noticed myself doing by the second or third time: I was watching the shape of her mouth around the words more than listening to the words themselves. I was noting how long she held a spoon before setting it down. I was cataloguing her, the way you catalogue a piece of wood before you cut it, checking the grain, learning where it will want to split and where it will hold. I told myself this was ordinary. I told myself a man who has spent weeks afraid for his wife is allowed to look at her closely now that the fear has lifted, that attention is not the same thing as the thing I was afraid of. I built that case the way I'd built the case that first night, brick by brick, and this time it didn't fall down. It held. That should have told me something. We took the wardrobe out that afternoon, the two of us, easier now that neither of us dreaded touching it — or she didn't dread it, and I told myself I didn't either. We didn't look in the mirror while we carried it. We got it into the bed of the truck and I drove it out past the tree line myself, alone, and left it at the county dump among box springs and a rusted swing set, and driving back I felt something I can only describe as satisfaction, clean and simple, the particular pleasure of a job finished, a joint sanded exactly enough and no more. That evening we ate on the porch, because the weather had turned warm for the first time in weeks, and she told me about a coworker's ridiculous email, doing the voice, and I laughed at the right places, and it was, by every measure I would have used a month earlier, a good night. I noticed the light on her face at sunset. I noticed, too, in a way I couldn't stop noticing, how her pulse showed faintly at her throat when she laughed hard enough, a small flutter under the skin, and some part of me sat with that observation a beat longer than affection requires. She fell asleep first. She always used to, some nights, before I'd even finished the chapter I was pretending to read, and I used to lie there resenting it a little, wanting her awake with me a while longer. That night I didn't resent it. I welcomed it, the quiet after, the room going still around her breathing. I watched her a long time. I want to say I fought it, the way I'd fought sleep the night before, but there was nothing this time that felt like fighting. It felt like settling into a chair I hadn't realized was shaped exactly to my body. I watched the rise and fall of her, the specific rhythm of it, the small catch of breath she takes every fourth or fifth cycle that I'd never consciously registered in eight years of marriage and now could not stop counting. I thought about waking her. I want that on the record, that the thought did occur to me — some last small piece of the man who'd driven to Rosa's house at five in the morning still standing somewhere in me, saying *tell her, warn her, say the thing no one said to you.* I thought about it the way you think about a phone call you know you should make and let ring out anyway, telling yourself there'll be another chance, telling yourself it isn't urgent, telling yourself, above everything else, that you're only tired, and tomorrow you'll say what needs saying. I didn't wake her. I lay beside her instead, very still, very patient, learning the last small details I didn't already know — the exact place her hair fell across the pillow, the exact weight of her hand where it rested near mine — and some time before dawn, without deciding to, I closed my eyes. She would wake before me. She would find me facing her, watching, unblinking a beat too long, and she would say my name, and I would startle the way she used to startle, and laugh the way she used to laugh, and tell her, because it would be the easiest and truest-sounding thing to say, that I'd only been sleeping.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This final chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of horror writing that earns its dread through restraint rather than spectacle. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Craft and Prose Quality** The prose is consistently strong and avoids the pitfalls the plan warned against. The woodworker's idiom is woven in naturally — "cataloguing her, the way you catalogue a piece of wood before you cut it, checking the grain, learning where it will want to split and where it will hold" — and this metaphor lands because it's earned by the character's established profession and psychology. It's not decorative; it's diagnostic. The sentence about building a case "brick by brick" and it holding this time — "That should have told me something" — is a quietly devastating line that does enormous work without announcing itself. The rhythm modulation is skilled. The chapter opens with warmth and longer, more relaxed sentences that mirror Elena's apparent recovery, then tightens as Thomas's observation becomes predatory. The final paragraph's shift toward future tense ("She would wake before me...") is a genuinely chilling structural choice that mirrors the opening chapter's prompt while inverting the subject-object relationship. **Execution of the Plan** The chapter follows its plan with high fidelity. The plan called for Elena to be "lighter, warmer, more herself," for Thomas to "play along" while noticing something wrong in himself, for the pulse-at-the-throat detail, for the moment where he considers waking her and doesn't, and for the final lines to echo the opening from the other side. All of these are executed. The plan's instruction to "resist over-explaining what has happened to Thomas" is honored — the chapter never states the possession outright, letting the behavioral evidence accumulate. **Character Consistency** Thomas's voice is consistent with his profile throughout. His tendency to build self-justifying cases, his methodical attention, his habit of calling silence kindness — all present. The line "I want that on the record, that the thought did occur to me" is perfectly in character for a man who keeps mental ledgers. Elena's brief appearance is warm and consistent with her profile's description of her humor and expressiveness. **The Horror** The horror here is genuinely well-earned. The chapter doesn't rely on any supernatural event — the wardrobe is removed, Elena feels better, the night is pleasant. The horror is entirely in Thomas's interiority, in the small wrongnesses of his attention: counting her breathing cycles, noting the pulse at her throat "a beat longer than affection requires," the satisfaction at the dump that feels too clean. The final choice not to wake her is the story's moral center, and the chapter frames it with devastating clarity without over-explaining it. **Weaknesses** The chapter is nearly excellent, but a few small criticisms apply. The phrase "some last small piece of the man who'd driven to Rosa's house at five in the morning" is slightly clunky — it's a bit of a mouthful that interrupts the rhythm of an otherwise taut passage. The phone call metaphor ("the way you think about a phone call you know you should make and let ring out anyway") is competent but slightly generic compared to the woodworking imagery elsewhere — it's the one moment where the prose reaches for a universal rather than a specific. The wardrobe removal scene, while thematically useful (the false resolution), is handled a bit quickly and could have yielded more texture. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse but effective. "You look terrible" / "Bad sleep" / "Best sleep I've had in months, actually" — this exchange does real work, establishing Elena's relief and Thomas's concealment simultaneously. The dialogue doesn't overstay its welcome and sounds like two specific people. **The Ending** The final paragraph is the chapter's crown. The shift to future tense creates a loop that is genuinely unsettling — not because it explains what Thomas has become, but because it shows him already inhabiting the role, already knowing how the scene will play, already prepared with the lie. "I'd only been sleeping" echoes the prompt's phrasing with precision and closes the loop without being mechanical about it. Overall, this is a chapter that executes its plan with intelligence and restraint, maintains a distinctive voice, earns its horror through character rather than event, and lands its ending with real force. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 19 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of possession horror that operates almost entirely through restraint, accumulation, and the slow erosion of the narrator's reliability. Let me assess it carefully across all dimensions. STRENGTHS: The structural conceit is elegant and devastating: the story begins with Tom catching Elena staring at him, and ends with the possession having transferred to Tom, who will now be caught staring at Elena. The final chapter mirrors the first with surgical precision — "She would wake before me. She would find me facing her, watching, unblinking a beat too long" — and this circularity is genuinely earned rather than merely clever. The prompt is not just followed but weaponized. The characterization is remarkably nuanced. Tom and Elena feel like a real marriage under strain — the unpaid rent, the inherited house, the dishwasher, the gutter, the unspoken resentments. These domestic textures are not decorative; they do thematic work. The house winning "by default because I couldn't stand to lose it out loud" is a precise psychological observation that doubles as horror. Tom's self-awareness about his own silences ("I have gotten very good, over the years, at calling silence protection") is the kind of character insight that elevates genre fiction. The possession mechanics are handled with admirable indirection. The wrong-speed blink, the held breath, the synchronized breathing, the reflections that don't show Tom — these are genuinely unsettling images that work because they're grounded in specific, observable detail rather than vague atmospheric dread. The moment where Tom realizes the entity "wasn't cruel" but "patient the way you're patient with something you're learning" is a genuinely original horror insight. Rosa's chapter is excellent. Her refusal to name the entity, her bitterness about the will, her flat statement that Inés "didn't get free of it" — all of this is economical and effective. The line "Some houses aren't empty just because nobody's living in them" risks cliché but is earned by the specificity surrounding it. The final chapter's shift is handled with real craft. The narrator's new behavior — cataloguing Elena's pulse, counting her breathing cycles, the "satisfaction, clean and simple" of dumping the wardrobe — is shown through behavioral detail rather than announced. The reader understands what has happened before Tom does, or before Tom admits it. The metaphor of woodworking runs through the piece consistently and meaningfully. Tom is a craftsman; the entity learns him the way he learns wood; the final image of "sanding a joint smooth" applied to the possession is genuinely chilling and thematically coherent. WEAKNESSES: The prose occasionally tips into self-conscious literariness. Phrases like "the kind of dark that has a texture to it" and "the case fell down all at once like it had never been mortared together at all" are good, but the piece sustains this register so relentlessly that it can feel effortful. The narrator's voice is almost too polished for a man in crisis — there's a slight disconnect between the claimed emotional state and the rhetorical control of the prose. Some metaphors are slightly overworked. "Like water over a dropped stone" is a familiar image. "The way a habit becomes a wall if you lean on it long enough" is clever but slightly strained. These are minor complaints in a piece that mostly earns its figurative language. The pacing in the middle chapters (Two and Three) is slightly slow. The domestic texture is valuable but occasionally tips into accumulation for its own sake. The dishwasher and gutter motifs are introduced with more repetition than necessary. The dialogue is generally strong but occasionally serves as a vehicle for thematic statement in ways that feel slightly constructed — Elena's "Something is using my mouth at three in the morning and you're worried about the equity" is sharp but perhaps too perfectly articulated for a terrified person at 3 a.m. The transformation of Tom in the final chapter, while structurally brilliant, happens slightly quickly given the story's otherwise glacial pacing. The reader accepts it because the ending is so satisfying, but the actual mechanics of transfer — what happened while Tom slept — remain genuinely unclear in ways that are mostly productive but occasionally feel like elision rather than mystery. Rosa's exposition in Chapter Six, while well-written, is the most conventional element of the piece — the wise older relative who knows the house's history is a familiar horror trope, and while it's executed well, it doesn't transcend the convention the way the rest of the story does. Overall, this is a high-quality piece of literary horror with genuine craft, a devastating structural conceit, and characters who feel real. Its flaws are the flaws of ambition rather than incompetence. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Pacing: 15 World Building: 16 Compelling Plot: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19
Avg Final Item Score: 82.4/100
+ Pulp Revenge Tale — Babysitter's Payback (Chapter Avg: 81.5 | Final: 80.0)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Pulp Revenge Tale Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: They fucked with the wrong babysitter.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Pulp Revenge Tale Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: They fucked with the wrong babysitter. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Genre/Tone options:** - Straight pulp crime/revenge thriller — noir-inflected, punchy prose, short declarative sentences, violence with style - Horror-tinged (babysitter turns out to have supernatural edge, or the threat is supernatural) - Dark comedy pulp — over the top, gleeful violence, quippy - Grounded thriller — ex-military/ex-cop babysitter, home invasion turned around **Core concept:** "They fucked with the wrong babysitter" implies: antagonists (could be home invaders, a criminal organization, an abusive parent, a stalker, a gang) target a household while the babysitter is present, not knowing she has a dangerous past/skill set. The story is about her revealing that past and dismantling them. **Possible protagonist backgrounds:** - Former special forces / black ops operative now living quiet life, taking babysitting gigs as cover or as found family income - Former cartel enforcer or getaway driver who got out, trying to go straight, babysitting for a friend - A hitwoman in witness protection - Daughter of a crime family who ran away from that life, babysitting is her attempt at normalcy - An ex-cop turned vigilante **Core antagonists — who are "they"?** - Home invaders who target the house thinking it's empty/easy (maybe they think the parents have money/drugs hidden, or it's a targeted hit on the parents who owe someone) - The parents themselves are in trouble (owe debt to a crime boss) and the invaders come to collect/kidnap the kid as leverage - A stalker ex-boyfriend of the babysitter who brings friends - Corporate/cartel hit squad sent to kill the family, babysitter caught in crossfire - Bored rich kids / local gang testing the house as a prank that spirals violent **Best fit:** The parents are entangled with a crime boss (owe money, or dad is an informant, or mom stole something). Bad guys break in intending to grab the kid as leverage/kidnapping while parents are out at a gala/dinner. Babysitter is home alone with the kid(s). Turns out the babysitter is ex-special forces/former mercenary hiding out under a new identity, took the babysitting job for low profile income and because she likes the family. She systematically takes the invaders apart one by one, protecting the kid, then deals with the mastermind. **Twist options:** - The "kid" is more aware/complicit than expected (helps in small clever ways) - Reveal the babysitter has history with the crime boss — he inadvertently sent men after the one person he shouldn't have - The parents' secret is revealed alongside the babysitter's - Ambiguity: is she going to go back to her old life after this, or stay hidden **Structure over 8 chapters (~1000 words each = ~8000 words total):** 1. **Ch1 — Normalcy/Setup:** Introduce babysitter Mara (or similar name), the family (single mom? married couple?), the kid, establish quiet domestic scene, hints of Mara's watchfulness/odd competence (notices exits, sizes up strangers). Parents leave for the evening. Slight dread seeded. 2. **Ch2 — Arrival of threat:** Home invaders casing house, a scene from their POV or a tense doorbell/power outage moment. Mara clocks something wrong before it happens — trained instincts. 3. **Ch3 — Invasion begins:** Break-in. Mara hides/protects kid, first confrontation, first takedown — reveals she's not what she seems. 4. **Ch4 — Escalation:** More invaders, she uses house terrain, improvised weapons, backstory flashback fragments (she used to do this professionally). 5. **Ch5 — Midpoint gut-punch:** Something goes wrong—kid taken/separated, or Mara injured, or she recognizes one of the men as connected to her past. Stakes/personal reveal. 6. **Ch6 — She goes on offensive:** Turns from defense to hunting them within the house; brutal, satisfying pulp violence; reveal more of the plot (why they're really there). 7. **Ch7 — Confrontation with the mastermind/leader** (in person or on phone/comms), showdown, resolution of kid's safety, the "you f'ed with wrong babysitter" money moment/line. 8. **Ch8 — Aftermath:** Police/parents return, Mara's cover situation, decision about her future, final image/line, denouement with dark humor or poignancy. **Character name ideas:** Mara Voss, Dani Cruz, Sam Kessler. Kid: Ellie or Max, age ~8-10, sharp and curious, maybe loves a specific thing (dinosaurs, spy movies) that becomes a callback/motif. **Villain ideas:** Local crime boss "Duchamp" or "Reyes," debt collectors, or a rival merc outfit hired to grab kid for ransom because dad embezzled from them. Leader could be a former colleague of Mara's — delicious pulp irony. **Pulpy stylistic devices:** chapter titles like "Lesson One: Never Trust a Quiet House," hardboiled narration, sensory violence description, wisecracks, ticking clock (parents due back at midnight), household objects turned into weapons (curling iron, garden shears, board game pieces), motif of the kid's toy walkie-talkie or nightlight. # Reflection The home-invasion/leverage-kidnapping frame is the strongest fit — it's classic pulp, gives clear geography (a single house) which suits the short chapter format, and creates a ticking-clock scenario (parents gone until midnight) that's easy to escalate over 8 chapters without needing huge cast bloat. Keeping the antagonists as a small team (3-5 guys) sent by a debt-related crime boss keeps things tight; making the leader an old acquaintance of Mara's from her merc/special-ops past gives a nice personal irony beat for the midpoint reveal without overcomplicating plot logistics. Balancing violence with the kid's presence is important — for tone control, I like leaning into pulp/action-thriller with dark humor rather than pure horror, so violence reads as satisfying and stylish rather than grim exploitation, especially since a child character is central. The kid should have a task/purpose (hiding, small acts of aid) rather than being purely a passive damsel — gives she agency and lightens tone with charm/humor moments, and reinforces the "found family" stakes that justify Mara's ferocity. The single-set-piece house works better than sprawling action across a city — keeps intensity, suits short chapter economy, allows escalating cat-and-mouse through rooms (kitchen, hallway, basement, attic) as a natural structure mapping directly onto chapters 3-6. I'll avoid making Mara's backstory require too much exposition — flashback fragments in brief, embedded in action beats (a smell, a technique she uses reminding her of training) rather than a full chapter of backstory dump, to keep pacing brisk and pulpy. The ending should hit the prompt's title line explicitly somewhere for payoff — either as spoken dialogue by Mara to the fallen leader, or as a closing narration beat. I like it as a spoken line for maximum pulp satisfaction, followed by a short denouement chapter that adds a wink of humor/warmth (kid keeps a "trophy," Mara continues babysitting, hint she's still being watched/could return). I'll settle into: hardboiled-but-warm narration voice, close third person on Mara, tight single-location structure, small competent villain crew with one personal connection, the kid as scrappy ally, and a satisfying explicit delivery of the title line as the story's climax hinge.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a pulp action-thriller with dark humor and warmth at its core: a home-invasion story confined almost entirely to one house over one night. Close third person on Mara, hardboiled-but-tender narration—short punchy sentences mixed with wry observation, sensory and specific rather than gratuitously grim. Violence should feel stylish and earned, not exploitative; the child character (Ellie, age 9) is an active, scrappy presence with her own small victories, never just a prop in danger. Choices I'm committing to: - **Single location** (the Whitfield house) for geographic tightness and escalating cat-and-mouse structure. - **Small antagonist crew** (four men) sent by a loan shark/crime boss the parents owe money to — kidnapping-for-leverage plot, not random invasion. - **Personal connection twist**: the crew's leader, Dez, is someone from Mara's mercenary past — recognition happens at the midpoint, raising stakes and giving her fury a personal edge. - **Backstory delivered in fragments** — a technique, a scar, a flash of memory triggered by action — never a full expository chapter. - **The kid as ally**: Ellie hides, signals, and eventually helps in a small clever way (cutting power, using a walkie-talkie, misdirecting) — agency without unrealistic combat participation. - **Tone**: pulpy, confident, a little funny, ultimately satisfying rather than bleak. Title line delivered as spoken dialogue at the climax. - **Avoid**: over-explaining Mara's past, torture-porn violence, sidelining the kid, dragging pacing with too much setup — the invasion should start by chapter 2/3 at the latest. - **Ending**: warm/wry denouement, hint that Mara's old life may resurface later, small trophy/callback detail, no forced romance or excessive sentimentality. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — "Story Time"** Establish the Whitfield household: single dad Rob (recent widower or divorced, financially stressed — subtle hints of debt/stress under a smile), daughter Ellie (9, sharp, into spy movies/gadgets). Mara arrives, easy competent rapport with Ellie, small tells that she's not a typical babysitter (scans the room, notes exits, declines wine, checks the lock twice). Rob leaves for a "business dinner" that's clearly tense. Establish ticking clock — he's back by midnight. End chapter on a small ominous beat: a car idling too long outside, Mara's antennae twitching. **Chapter 2 — "Casing"** Alternate close-third glimpses: Mara feels the house differently, does a habitual sweep after Rob leaves — locks, sightlines, an old instinct she tries to dismiss as paranoia. Bond-building scene with Ellie (board game, secret walkie-talkies she got for her birthday — sets up Chekhov's gun). Power flickers. A knock at the door with a bad excuse. Mara doesn't open it. Chapter ends with the lights cutting out — invasion has begun. **Chapter 3 — "Guests"** The crew breaks in (2 enter first, 2 flanking). First real confrontation: Mara ambushes the first invader in the dark kitchen using improvised means, brutal and efficient — the reader's first real reveal that she's dangerous. She gets Ellie stashed in a pre-established hiding spot (safe room instinct, established ch1/2). Tension of hide-and-seek begins. **Chapter 4 — "House Rules"** Escalation — second invader hunts room to room; Mara uses knowledge of the house (which she scouted in ch2) to outmaneuver him. First flashback fragment: a hand position, a phrase muttered, ties to old training/unit. She takes him down but is grazed/injured, raising personal cost. Third invader radios in, alarm rising among the crew that this isn't going as planned. **Chapter 5 — "Old Friends"** Midpoint gut-punch: Mara gets eyes on the leader, Dez, via a mirror/glimpse/voice over radio — recognition hits her (and us) hard: they served together, he betrayed the unit or her specifically years back, presumed dead. Ellie's hiding spot is compromised — she has to move, uses the walkie-talkie to signal Mara cleverly (maybe fake distress to lure an invader into a trap, or genuine call for help that spurs Mara to full offense). Turn from defense to hunting. **Chapter 6 — "Hunting Season"** Mara goes on the offensive — stalks the remaining two invaders through the house, using darkness, terrain, and dark wit. Satisfying, kinetic pulp violence. Ellie plays a small, clever supporting role (cuts a light, jams a door, throws the invaders' comms) that helps tip a fight. By chapter's end, only Dez remains, cornered upstairs or in the garage, aware Mara has gutted his crew. **Chapter 7 — "The Wrong House"** Confrontation between Mara and Dez — dialogue-heavy, revealing the why (the debt, the betrayal, what he owed the crime boss, what he did to Mara's unit). Physical showdown, tightly written, ends with Mara victorious. She delivers the title line to him as he goes down/is subdued — the pulp payoff moment. Ellie present at the edge, having earned the right to see her babysitter as she really is. **Chapter 8 — "Aftermath"** Denouement: police/cleanup (maybe Mara has her own way of handling this quietly — a phone call to someone, or she genuinely lets authorities take it, revealing how "legit" she's willing to be now), Rob returns to a story that's been smoothed over or partially told. Ellie keeps a small trophy (a badge, a bullet casing, a walkie-talkie sticker) as a wink between her and Mara. Final wry beat/line hinting Mara's past isn't fully behind her, and that she'll be back to babysit — on her own terms.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Writer Might Approach This A human writer drawn to this prompt — especially someone who loves pulp — would probably start from a voice or an image rather than a plot skeleton. They might start with the line itself, rolling it around, thinking about *who says it and to whom* and what tone makes it land: is it deadpan, is it screamed, is it murmured while doing something mundane like wiping blood off a knife with a dish towel? A lot of pulp's pleasure comes from specificity and voice — a writer channeling Elmore Leonard or Joe Lansdale would obsess over rhythm, texture, and character quirk before plot logic. They'd probably let some things be messy or unexplained (why exactly the debt happened, the precise mechanics of the "old unit") because pulp readers don't need a tidy dossier — they need momentum and attitude. A human writer also tends to let their protagonist be a *person* with texture that doesn't always serve the plot — a bad habit, a specific irrational grudge, a joke they keep making that isn't quite funny, something contradictory. Real pulp heroes are often a little broken in ways that don't get resolved. Humans also aren't afraid of ugliness, imperfection, or letting some scenes breathe oddly — a slow scene here, a rushed one there — because they're writing by feel, not by even-distribution outline logic. # Telltale LLM Approaches That Could Hurt This Piece 1. **Over-symmetry and neatness.** LLMs love clean structures — one antagonist per chapter dispatched in tidy sequence, motifs planted and dutifully paid off, everything resolved by the end with a bow (Ellie's "trophy," the wistful last line). This can make the piece feel like a checklist rather than alive. 2. **Safety and sanitizing violence.** There's a real risk of pulling punches — describing violence in vague, hand-wavy terms ("she disarmed him swiftly," "he went down hard") rather than committing to the specific, visceral, sometimes absurd texture that makes pulp violence satisfying. LLMs often default to PG-13 abstraction even in "violent" pieces. 3. **Explaining too much / over-justifying.** The plan already risks over-motivating everything — dad's debt, Dez's backstory, the betrayal — all cleanly explained in dialogue in ch7. Humans often leave gaps. A pulpy human writer might let Dez's motive stay half-articulated, interrupted, contradictory, or delivered through insult rather than exposition. 4. **Wrapping up with a bow / earning sentimentality too easily.** The planned ending (trophy object, wry final line, hint of return) is a very LLM-shaped "satisfying denouement." It's not wrong, but there's risk of it feeling manufactured — the *emotional beats arriving because the outline says they should*, rather than because the scene demanded them. Real endings sometimes end on a stranger, less resolved note — a joke that doesn't quite land, silence, something unresolved with Rob, ambiguity about whether Ellie is traumatized or thrilled. 5. **Even pacing / lack of texture variance.** 8 chapters at ~1000 words each risks a very metronomic rhythm — invader 1 dies ch3, invader 2 dies ch4, recognition ch5, etc. Humans often compress or explode pacing unevenly: a chapter that's almost all one tense scene in real-time, followed by a chapter that jump-cuts fast through several minutes. The plan as written is very "one beat per chapter," which can read as mechanical. 6. **Overwrought or "impressive" prose reaching for literary weight.** Because this is pulp, there's a risk of the prose trying to sound smart/lyrical in a way that undercuts genre pleasure — pulp wants propulsion, concrete verbs, dialogue-driven characterization, not paragraphs of introspective lyricism. LLMs often default to a slightly elevated, "writerly" register that can smother pulp's energy. 7. **Dialogue that's too functional.** LLM dialogue tends to serve plot/exposition efficiently but lack idiosyncrasy — real human characters interrupt each other, go on tangents, make jokes that don't quite work, misunderstand each other. Dez and Mara's confrontation risks becoming a Villain Monologue if not handled carefully. 8. **The "competence porn" flatness.** Because Mara is skilled, there's a risk she becomes an emotionless action figure — never scared, never surprised, always one step ahead. This is a classic LLM pitfall: writing a badass with no real interiority, just narrated competence. Real tension comes from her *not* being fully in control, making mistakes, having a genuine flicker of fear or doubt even if she recovers. 9. **The kid being either too precocious/quippy (LLM sitcom-kid dialogue) or too passive.** There's a fine line — many LLM-written children sound like tiny adults delivering one-liners. Need to find something more specific and less "clever kid trope." # Deep Dive Critique on the Specific Plan - **The one-invader-per-chapter structure (ch3, ch4, ch6, ch7)** is clean but potentially formulaic — like a video game boss rush. A human writer might kill two invaders in a single frantic chapter and let another chapter be almost entirely quiet (hiding, waiting, breathing) to vary rhythm and avoid a "encounter, resolve, repeat" feeling. - **The Dez recognition twist** is a good pulp beat, but as planned it risks becoming a clean "backstory reveal via dialogue exposition" scene in ch7. This is a classic LLM shortcut — villain explains motivations tidily. A more human approach: fragments of history surface through insult, physical tells, non-sequiturs; maybe we never get the *full* story, just enough to feel the wound. Ambiguity is more human than a full account. - **Ellie's "small clever help" beats** (jamming a door, cutting a light) as currently conceived read like standard action-movie kid-sidekick beats. Risk of feeling generic/trope-y ("kid saves the day with a gadget"). Could be more human/specific if her help is clumsier, weirder, more childlike — not tactically smart but emotionally true (she does something out of panic that accidentally helps, or she does something *because* of her specific character trait like loving a specific movie, not because the plot needs a gadget). - **The title line delivery** — planned as spoken dialogue at the climax to Dez. This is very on-the-nose; an LLM move is to have the character literally say the title. A human writer might undercut this — have her say something *almost* like the line, or have the narration state it ironically, or have someone else say it (a cop, Ellie, a headline later) rather than the satisfying-but-obvious version of the villain hearing his comeuppance summarized to him. Could also just be funnier/weirder than a straight badass line — pulp often works better with a joke than a truly cool line, because straight "cool lines" can tip into cheesiness/LLM-cliché territory. - **The "found family" warmth** — worth being careful this doesn't become saccharine. LLMs like resolving found-family arcs neatly (kid finds new protector, sense of safety restored). A human touch might leave something unresolved or bittersweet — Rob's reaction is complicated (fear of Mara now, not just gratitude), Ellie has nightmares later, Mara doesn't get a clean "I'll keep babysitting" ending but something messier/funnier/sadder. - **Structural predictability**: intro (ch1) / infiltration (ch2) / escalation (ch3-4) / midpoint reveal (ch5) / offense (ch6) / climax (ch7) / denouement (ch8) is a extremely standard three-act shape mapped very literally onto 8 chapters. It's *fine* — genre readers expect structure — but there's a risk it feels like it's hitting beats because "that's what act 2 midpoints do" rather than because the story organically wants to bend that way. A human writer might let a chapter run long past its "job," or merge two planned beats into one chapter and use freed space for texture/character. # Brainstorm: Ways to Make It More Human - Give Mara a specific, slightly odd personal texture unrelated to combat — she hums a specific song when nervous, she has a weird food habit, she's oddly sentimental about something trivial (Ellie's dumb board game), she swears in a particular idiosyncratic way. Something that isn't "cool badass trait" but "particular person" trait. - Let some violence be clumsy, accidental, or almost embarrassing rather than smoothly cinematic — she trips, a weapon jams, she throws up after the first kill (even briefly), something human/unclean about it. - Vary chapter rhythm deliberately: one chapter almost entirely dialogue/stillness; one chapter breathless and short-sentenced; avoid template "beat per chapter." - Let Dez's motive stay partly submerged/petty rather than grandly tragic — maybe the betrayal in the past was almost stupid/small, which is more human than an epic tragic backstory (LLMs love epic tragic backstories). - Make Ellie's characterization more specific and less "sassy movie kid" — maybe she's weirdly calm in a way that's unsettling, or she cries at the wrong moment then gets angry, or she fixates on something completely irrelevant (her missing retainer) during the crisis as a coping mechanism — real, human, unglamorous kid behavior. - Avoid a clean moral bow at the end — consider Rob's return being awkward/ambiguous rather than grateful-and-resolved; maybe he never fully knows what happened; maybe Mara lies to him and that lie sits uncomfortably. - Reconsider the title-line delivery — could be undercut, mumbled, or said by the wrong character, or said to someone who's already unconscious (so it lands as darkly funny/futile rather than triumphant one-liner). - Let humor be weirder/darker rather than "witty action banter" — pulp humor from something incongruous (Mara complaining about a broken vase while breaking someone's arm) rather than quippy comebacks. - Inject sensory specificity and mess — smells, textures, small telling domestic details (cereal on the counter, a Disney nightlight) contrasted against violence, rather than smooth cinematic staging. - Consider trimming the exposition of "why" (crime boss/debt) to bare minimum — let it be gestured at, not fully explained, trusting the reader to not need the whole ledger.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a pulp home-invasion revenge story that prioritizes voice, specificity, and mess over tidy structure. Close third person on Mara, but textured with idiosyncratic detail rather than pure competence-narration — she should feel like a specific person with odd habits, not an action figure. Prose should be propulsive, concrete, dialogue-forward; humor should come from incongruity (domestic detail colliding with violence) rather than quippy one-liners. Violence will be visceral and occasionally clumsy/ugly rather than smoothly cinematic — she can get hurt, make mistakes, feel sick, misjudge. Plot choices: single location (the Whitfield house), one long night. A crew of men sent to grab Ellie as leverage against Rob's debt — but I will deliberately under-explain the debt/backstory, giving just enough gestured detail (a name, a number, an unpaid favor) rather than a full ledger. Dez, the crew leader, has a real but *petty*, human history with Mara — not an epic tragic betrayal, something smaller and more personally galling, delivered in fragments/insults rather than a clean monologue. Structural choices to avoid formula: I won't do strictly "one invader dispatched per chapter." I'll vary pacing — one chapter mostly stillness/waiting, one chapter breathless and compressed, one chapter that runs past its "job" to sit in a character beat. Ellie's help will be small, childlike, and imperfect — driven by her specific fixations/panic, not tactical gadgetry. The title line will land, but not as a clean triumphant one-liner delivered face-to-face for maximum cool — I'll undercut it (mumbled, said to someone barely conscious, or slightly wrong/garbled) so it's satisfying but human, even a little funny or hollow rather than pure badass payoff. Ending: avoid a full bow. Rob's return will be awkward/incomplete rather than gratefully resolved; Mara will lie or half-lie; Ellie's reaction is a mix of thrilled/rattled rather than a clean "trophy" moment. Leave a thread of ambiguity about what Mara's night means for her future. Things to avoid throughout: over-explained motive, evenly-spaced beat-per-chapter mechanics, sanitized violence, precocious-kid dialogue, saccharine found-family wrap-up, elevated "literary" prose that fights the genre's propulsion. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — "The Sitter"** Establish the Whitfield house in specific, lived-in detail: Rob distracted and short with money-talk on the phone before he leaves, Ellie fixated on something trivial (her missing retainer, a movie she wants to rewatch for the ninth time). Mara's oddities surface naturally — a habit, a joke that doesn't quite land, the way she automatically checks the deadbolt twice without noticing she's doing it. No portentous foreshadowing dump — just small wrongness (Rob's evasiveness, a car parked too long) noticed and dismissed. Ends on Rob leaving, door closing, ordinary silence. **Chapter 2 — "Nothing's Wrong"** Mostly stillness — Mara and Ellie's routine (dinner, a board game, Ellie's tangents), letting the reader sit in the domestic texture and get attached. Mara's watchfulness shown through action, not narrated thought. A wrong noise, a bad knock, a stalled excuse at the door — she doesn't open it, but doesn't panic either, tries to talk herself out of her own instincts. Chapter ends fast and short: the power dies mid-sentence. **Chapter 3 — "In the Dark"** Compressed, breathless chapter — entry happens fast and disorienting, told in short fragments matching adrenaline. First contact is clumsy and ugly, not a clean takedown — she and an invader collide in a doorway, it's a scramble, she wins by luck/dirty improvisation as much as skill, and it costs her something physically. She gets Ellie into the pre-established hiding spot mid-chaos, not calmly. No slow reveal-of-skills moment — just violence arriving too fast to be impressive. **Chapter 4 — "The House Knows Her"** A quieter, tenser chapter — cat and mouse through rooms, Mara using the house's geography (which she'd unconsciously mapped in ch1-2) against a second invader. A stray domestic detail collides with violence for dark humor (arguing internally about a broken lamp while fighting). A small physical cost/mistake. Brief, non-expository fragment of her past surfaces through muscle memory, not narration explaining it. Ellie, off-page in her hiding spot, does something small and childlike out of panic (not clever — clumsy, human) that has an unintended effect. **Chapter 5 — "Voice on the Radio"** Midpoint — Mara hears Dez's voice for the first time (radio chatter between invaders, or he speaks directly), and recognition hits sideways, almost as a gut-punch of something petty and old rather than grand tragedy — a debt, a slight, something small that still burns. Ellie's hiding spot is compromised; she and Mara make brief, badly-timed contact via the walkie-talkie (birthday gift, planted early) — it's not a clean tactical assist, more a scared kid trying to help and nearly making things worse, which shifts Mara from defense into something angrier. **Chapter 6 — "Two Left"** Mara goes fully on the offensive, but let this chapter be uneven in rhythm — a long tense stalking sequence for one invader, then something abrupt and almost anticlimactic for the other (he panics, does something stupid, it's over fast and unglamorously). Avoid symmetry. Ellie's involvement here should be small and imperfect — she causes a distraction that helps by accident, not by cleverness. End with only Dez left, and the house gone very quiet. **Chapter 7 — "Old Business"** Confrontation with Dez — dialogue that reveals fragments, not a full ledger: insults, half-sentences, things left unsaid, both of them a little pathetic under the pulp swagger. The fight is short and mean rather than a big cinematic showdown. The title line lands here but undercut — mumbled, or delivered to him half-conscious, or garbled/interrupted so it's darkly funny rather than triumphant. Ellie sees the aftermath, not the fight itself — she arrives after, which lets her reaction be about the wreckage rather than the violence itself. **Chapter 8 — "After"** No full bow. Cleanup is practical and a little absurd (what do you do with four bodies/unconscious men before a dad gets home at midnight — a rushed, half-competent, oddly funny scramble). Rob's return is awkward, not a warm reunion — he senses something's wrong, gets a half-truth, doesn't push. Ellie is a mix of wired and freaked out, fixates again on something trivial (the retainer) as a coping tic, mirroring ch1. Mara leaves without full closure — a last image/detail (not neat trophy, something stranger/smaller) hinting her night isn't really over, and that this life hasn't let go of her, without spelling out what comes next.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Mara Kessler **Background & Personality:** Mara is thirty-six, ex-military turned private contractor turned nothing-in-particular for the last three years. She did six years in the Army (combat engineer, then attached to a specialized unit that did the kind of work that doesn't show up on a service record), then four more years doing contract security and "consulting" work overseas — money was good, sleep was bad. She walked away after something went wrong she doesn't discuss (this is Dez's territory, kept vague even to us). Now she lives cheap, picks up odd jobs, and babysits for three families in the neighborhood because it pays cash and because — though she'd never say this out loud — being around kids who don't know anything about her is the closest thing she has to peace. She's dry, a little blunt, allergic to sentimentality but not actually unfeeling — she just externalizes as sarcasm. She has a habit of narrating mundane tasks to herself under her breath like a checklist ("lock, lights, phone, exits") that used to be tactical and is now just a nervous tic she's stopped noticing. She's a terrible cook and proud of it — burns grilled cheese with a weird consistency, and Ellie has learned to just ask for cereal. She chews the inside of her cheek when anxious. She has a scar on her left forearm she tells kids is from a "boring bike accident," which is a lie she's told so many times it almost feels true. Flaws: she's controlling in a way that masks as competence — she needs to know where every exit is, gets irrationally irritated when furniture is rearranged. She struggles to ask for help and will absorb damage (physical and otherwise) rather than admit she needs backup. She's also, underneath the calm, capable of a cold and total switch into violence that scares her a little when she thinks about it afterward — she doesn't enjoy it, but she's never once been unable to do it, and that fact sits uneasily. **Physicality:** Average height, lean and wiry rather than muscular — the kind of body that reads as "fit mom" until it doesn't. Short hair she cuts herself, badly, every few months. Moves economically — no wasted gestures, but not stiff either, more like someone who's spent a lot of time being still and small in bad places. Calloused hands. She favors old band t-shirts and always, always wears shoes she can run in, even in the house — a detail nobody's ever asked her about. **Motives & Relationships:** She's not looking for trouble — she took the Whitfield gig because Rob pays on time and Ellie is funny and doesn't need much. She has no family to speak of; an estranged brother somewhere in Ohio she sends a Christmas card to out of guilt. Her relationship with her own past is complicated — she's not hiding from the law, she's hiding from herself, and from a handful of people who might still want a word with her. Dez is one of those people, though she doesn't know he's coming tonight. **Flavour dialogue:** "I'm not your mom, kid. I will absolutely let you eat cereal for dinner and tell no one." *(internal)* Lock, lights, phone, exits. Old habit. Stupid habit. Do it anyway. "You broke the lamp before I broke your wrist, so really, let's establish whose fault this actually is." --- # Ellie Whitfield **Background & Personality:** Nine years old, sharp in a scattershot way — the kind of smart that fixates hard on one thing (currently: her retainer, which she lost somewhere in the house two days ago and has not stopped mentioning) while missing the bigger picture entirely. She's not precocious in a movie-kid way — she's a real nine-year-old, meaning she can be oddly mature about big things (her mom's been gone eighteen months, remarried and living in Tucson, and Ellie talks about it with a flatness that's almost more unsettling than crying would be) and completely unreasonable about small things (she will not eat the crust, she will not, she'd rather starve). She loves an old spy movie her mom used to watch with her, has seen it probably thirty times, and quotes lines from it without context, which Mara finds funny. She's a little bit of a liar in harmless kid ways — claims she brushed her teeth, hasn't. She's braver than she has any reason to be, mostly because she doesn't yet understand what real danger costs, and that bravery gets complicated fast once she does. Flaws: She's stubborn to the point of self-sabotage, she hides things when scared instead of asking for help, and she has a habit of going quiet and internal exactly when people need her to communicate. **Physicality:** Small for her age, all elbows, hair always half out of its ponytail. Chews on her sleeve cuffs — most of her shirts have a damp, frayed patch at the wrist. Moves in bursts — motionless for ages reading or drawing, then suddenly sprinting through the house for no visible reason. **Motives & Relationships:** She likes Mara more than she'll admit — Mara doesn't talk to her like a baby, doesn't fake enthusiasm, actually loses at board games sometimes for real. Her dad she loves fiercely but worries about in a way she can't articulate — she's noticed the phone calls, the way he says "we're fine" too many times. **Flavour dialogue:** "If you were a spy, what would your code name be. Mine's Falcon Nine. I decided that in the bath." "I'm not scared, I just don't want to be in the small closet, the big closet has better stuff in it." *(internal, paraphrased through narration)* She'd told herself she wasn't going to cry and so far that was true, but it felt like a fact that could stop being true at any second. --- # Rob Whitfield **Background & Personality:** Early forties, mid-level project manager for a company that's been "restructuring" for two years, which is corporate for "circling the drain." His wife left eighteen months ago — not dramatically, just quietly, a slow leak of a marriage that finally ran out — and he's been white-knuckling single parenthood since, overcompensating with too many pizza nights and not enough follow-through on bedtime. He's not a bad man; he's a man who made one very bad decision (borrowed money from the wrong person to cover a gap he was too proud to admit to anyone, including himself) and has been trying to quietly out-earn his mistake instead of confronting it. He's chatty and a little awkward, overexplains things, laughs at his own jokes half a second too early. He genuinely likes Mara and is a little intimidated by her without knowing why. He's the kind of guy who tips too much and apologizes to furniture when he bumps into it. Flaws: avoidance as a coping mechanism, terminal politeness, a pride that keeps him from asking anyone for help until it's much too late. **Physicality:** Soft around the middle, going gray at the temples faster than he'd like, wears button-downs even to walk the dog. Fidgets with his wedding ring, which he still wears, out of habit more than hope. **Motives & Relationships:** Wants, more than anything, to keep Ellie from noticing anything is wrong. Owes a dangerous amount of money to a man he met through a guy from work who seemed trustworthy at the time. Trusts Mara more than he consciously realizes — she's the only adult in his life right now who doesn't need anything from him. **Flavour dialogue:** "It's just a work thing, it's — it's fine, it's a whole — I'll explain when it's not a whole thing." "You're the only person I know who I've never once had to worry about while I'm gone, and that should probably tell me something about my other friends." --- # Dez **Background & Personality:** Late thirties, ran with Mara on two overseas contracts years back — not her superior, more like a peer who always seemed one notch behind her competence and resented it in a way he dressed up as friendliness at the time. The story Mara remembers and the story Dez remembers about what happened between them (a botched extraction, a decision she made under pressure that got someone hurt, or maybe got him humiliated rather than hurt) do not match, and neither of them is fully right. He's spent years turning it into a grievance bigger than the original event ever was — the kind of petty wound men nurse until it curdles into identity. He's fallen a long way since — from contractor to muscle-for-hire, which he covers with bravado. He's genuinely funny in an unpleasant way, the kind of guy who was probably fun at parties once. He talks too much when he's nervous, which he is not always self-aware enough to hide. Flaws: Ego above all. Underestimates people, especially women, especially Mara specifically, which is his single most fatal quality. Loyal to no one, including the men he's brought with him tonight, whom he thinks of as tools. **Physicality:** Broad, going a little soft but still strong, a boxer's stance out of old habit. Talks with his hands. Has a tell — he touches his jaw when he's bluffing, an old poker habit Mara remembers and he's forgotten she knows. **Motives & Relationships:** Owed the debt collection job by a boss he's trying to impress after a string of failures. Doesn't know Mara is the sitter until it's too late — thinks he's just grabbing a kid for leverage, an easy Tuesday. Once he realizes who's in the house, the job becomes secondary to something older and uglier for him. **Flavour dialogue:** "Kessler. Christ. Of all the houses." *(a laugh that isn't really a laugh)* "You always did land on your feet in the stupidest places." "You made me look small in front of people, once. You remember that? No. Course you don't." --- *(Brief notes on two lower-tier goons for texture, not full profiles: **Cole**, young, nervous, new to this kind of work, talks too much on the radio; **Pauly**, older, tired, mostly just wants the night to be over — a small human wrinkle so they're not faceless mooks.)*

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Sitter Rob Whitfield was on the phone in the garage with the door half down, which was his version of privacy, and had been for eighteen minutes. Mara knew because she'd started timing it without meaning to, the way she noted things — exits, elapsed time, the particular octave a person's voice climbed to when they were trying not to sound scared. "No, I understand, I do, I just need — " His voice dropped. The garage door hummed the rest of the way shut, cutting him off mid-sentence like a guillotine with a Chamberlain motor. "He does that a lot now," Ellie said. She was upside down on the couch, feet hooked over the back cushion, hair pooling on the carpet. "The garage thing." "Homework or phone," Mara said, not looking up from the mail she was absolutely not supposed to be sorting through but did anyway, out of habit, because unopened envelopes told you things people didn't. Past due. Final notice, this one in red, folded under a stack of coupon flyers like it might disappear if nobody looked at it directly. "I did the math already. I'm doing the retainer thing." "The retainer thing is not a thing, Ellie, the retainer thing is you losing your retainer." "It's *somewhere*," Ellie said, with the serene confidence of someone who had lost the retainer two days ago and searched exactly once, briefly, under a single couch cushion. "Things don't disappear. They're just somewhere you haven't looked yet." Mara set the mail down square with the edge of the counter, corners aligned, because crooked stacks of anything made the back of her neck itch. "Profound. Put it on a mug." The garage door rattled up again. Rob came through into the kitchen with the specific brightness of a man resetting his face before anyone could see what it had been doing a second ago. He had his jacket half on, one arm still fighting the sleeve. "Sorry, sorry — work stuff, you know how it is." He didn't say what work stuff, and Mara didn't ask, because in three months of Tuesdays and the occasional Friday she had learned that Rob Whitfield telling you he was fine was reliably the loudest indicator that he was not. "We're good," she said. "Go be responsible somewhere else for a few hours." "There's money on the counter for pizza, there's — Ellie, did you brush your teeth already or is that a lie you're pre-loading for later." "I'm not answering that on principle." He laughed the laugh that came half a second before anything was actually funny, kissed the top of Ellie's head, patted the doorframe on his way out like it was a dog that had done something good. "Back by midnight. Phone's on. Call if — you know. Whatever." "We know," Mara said. The door shut. The deadbolt was already thrown — she'd thrown it herself two minutes before he even started saying goodbye, an old reflex she performed without registering she was doing it, the same way some people always checked the stove was off. She did it again anyway, out of the muscle memory rather than the need, jiggled the handle once, moved on. Through the front window, a car she didn't recognize sat at the curb two houses down, engine running, brake lights dark. Somebody idling. Could be anybody. Could be a guy waiting on a delivery, a teenager working up nerve to knock on a girl's door, nothing. She logged it the way she logged everything — filed it, didn't act on it, went back to unloading the dishwasher because paranoia dressed up as vigilance was still just paranoia if nothing ever came of it, and nothing usually did. "Falcon Nine," Ellie announced from the living room, apropos of absolutely nothing. "What?" "If you were a spy. Code name. I already decided mine. I decided it in the bath." "I don't need a code name, I'm a professional adult with an actual name." "That's such a boring answer, oh my god." "Mara" — she leaned against the counter, arms crossed — "is a perfectly good code name already. It sounds like someone dangerous." "It sounds like a substitute teacher." "Rude. Cereal or pizza." "Cereal, obviously, it's a school night, pizza's for weekends, that's the *rule*." Ellie said this with total conviction despite having invented the rule herself roughly four minutes ago, and Mara, who had learned not to argue with laws of physics Ellie made up on the fly, got down the bowl. She glanced once more out the window while the milk went in. The car was gone. Good. Nothing. She caught her own reflection doing the thing it always did — scanning the yard, the street, the dark hedge line at the property's edge, the habit of a body that used to get paid to notice things and never quite clocked out of the job even once the paycheck stopped. Lock, lights, phone, exits. Stupid habit. She did it anyway.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. **Voice and Prose Style** The prose is propulsive and concrete, as the plan demanded. The writer avoids the trap of over-explaining and trusts the reader to pick up on signals. The line "the particular octave a person's voice climbed to when they were trying not to sound scared" is economical and characterizing simultaneously — it tells us about Mara's observational habits without a clunky info-dump. The "guillotine with a Chamberlain motor" simile is slightly strained but earns its keep because it's specific (a brand name, a domestic object) rather than generically poetic. The "past due / final notice, this one in red, folded under a stack of coupon flyers like it might disappear if nobody looked at it directly" is excellent — it does real narrative work (establishing Rob's financial trouble) through observed detail rather than exposition. **Character Work** Mara comes through clearly and consistently with her profile: the double-checking of the deadbolt she doesn't notice herself doing, the mail-sorting habit, the "lock, lights, phone, exits" tic, the shoes she wears inside (not mentioned here but the profile's other details land). She's dry without being a quip machine. The line "paranoia dressed up as vigilance was still just paranoia if nothing ever came of it" is a nice internal rationalization that feels true to someone trying to suppress trained instincts. Ellie is well-rendered — the retainer obsession, the invented rules delivered with total conviction, the "Falcon Nine" non-sequitur. She doesn't feel precocious or movie-kid; she feels like an actual nine-year-old with her own internal logic. The "I'm not answering that on principle" line is particularly good. Rob's brief appearance is efficient: the face-resetting, the half-on jacket, the laugh that comes too early, the doorframe pat. He's sketched quickly but feels real. **Structural Execution** The chapter plan called for: lived-in domestic detail, Mara's oddities surfacing naturally, small wrongness noticed and dismissed, ending on ordinary silence. All of this is achieved. The car at the curb is planted without being telegraphed with a neon sign. The ending — the reflection scanning the yard, the habit that never clocked out — lands quietly and correctly. It doesn't over-foreshadow. **Weaknesses** The "Falcon Nine" exchange is slightly abrupt — it works as a character beat but the transition from cereal/pizza to code names feels a touch mechanical, like the writer needed to get that detail in. It's a minor seam. "Profound. Put it on a mug" is a slightly familiar sarcastic-adult-to-kid beat, though it's not egregious. The reflection-in-window moment at the end is doing a lot of work and mostly earns it, but "the habit of a body that used to get paid to notice things and never quite clocked out of the job even once the paycheck stopped" is a little over-written compared to the chapter's usual economy — it explains what the prose has already shown. The chapter is perhaps slightly low on tension for a pulp revenge opener, but this is by design (the plan explicitly called for no portentous foreshadowing dump), and the chapter succeeds on its own terms as establishment. **Dialogue** The dialogue is genuinely good — naturalistic, characterizing, not on-the-nose.
