Model Output:
# Chapter 5: Tasting Notes I told them over what was left of the beets, because I've learned you should deliver bad news alongside something a person already likes, so the two feelings get confused with each other. "I ate some of it," I said. "The one from the barn." Des's fork stopped on the way to his mouth. Oli didn't stop at all, which told me something about her I filed away for later. "Ate some of what," Des said, in the tone of a man who already knows and is buying time. "You know what." "You ate the—" He put the fork down. "Mara." "Last night. While you were asleep. I'm not dead. I'm not sick. I checked myself for six hours before I said anything, which is more than either of you would've done for me." "That's not the point." "What's the point, then." He didn't have one, immediately, which is usually how you know you've won an argument with Des, though I've learned not to enjoy it, because winning an argument with Des costs you something later, in quiet. Oli, meanwhile, had gone very still in the specific way she goes still before she says something designed to detonate. "What'd it taste like." "Oli." "I'm asking a real question. If we're doing this I want actual information, not — vibes." I looked at Des. Des looked at the ceiling, which is where he goes when he wants no part of a decision but also doesn't want to be blamed for whichever way it goes. "Bacon," I said. "Sort of. Bacon that got weird about it. Good weird." "Good weird is not a food group," Des said. "No," I agreed, "it's a marketing problem," and Oli laughed, actually laughed, the first time I'd heard that sound out of her in longer than I wanted to count, and something in the room loosened by a degree. We didn't decide anything that night. That's the honest shape of it — nobody stood up and gave a speech about survival and necessity, nobody voted. What happened was smaller and worse: three days later the beets ran out, and the beans, and Des went quiet for a whole evening, doing his arithmetic, and the next morning he went out to the shed himself, alone, and came back forty minutes later with his hands shaking and a piece of meat wrapped in a grocery bag, and set it on the counter without a word, and I understood that the vote, such as it was, had happened somewhere private, inside him, and this was the result. I cooked it the way I'd cooked mine. Boil, trim, sear. I added thyme this time, the last of it, on the theory that if we were doing this we might as well do it properly, which made Des laugh — a real laugh, surprised out of him — and say, "You're seasoning the monster," and I said, "I'm seasoning dinner, there's a difference," and he said, "Is there," and I said, "There has to be, or I quit," and that, more than anything anyone actually decided, was the moment it became a thing we did. We ate at the table. All three of us. I gave tasting notes, because it turns out that's a compulsion I cannot switch off even here, even now — "little smoky on the finish, better than the barn one, I think it's a freshness thing" — and Oli laughed so hard at "finish" that she had to put her fork down, and Des ate steadily, without commentary, the way he eats everything, but he had seconds, and I noticed, and didn't say anything, because some kindnesses are best delivered as silence. It was, unfashionable as this is to admit, a good night. Maybe the best one we'd had since the fences went up. There was a moment where Oli did an impression of me narrating my own eventual death — "notes: bitter, slightly stringy, improves with age" — and Des actually put his head back and laughed at the ceiling, and I felt something in my chest that I hadn't felt in months, that I didn't have a clean name for, that might just have been called relief if relief didn't feel, lately, so much like something else standing very close behind it. It was later, doing the dishes, that I noticed the graze on my forearm — the one from the barn, from the fight, a shallow thing I'd barely registered getting — had closed up. Not healing. Closed. Pink new skin where four days ago there'd been a scab, smooth as if it had simply decided the wound wasn't worth the time anymore. I turned my arm under the kitchen light for longer than I meant to. Then I finished the dishes, and didn't mention it, and went to bed, and told myself it was nothing, the way you tell yourself all sorts of things at one in the morning that you would not say out loud at any other hour of the day.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably accomplished piece of short fiction that executes its plan with precision and genuine craft. Let me work through its qualities systematically. **Voice and Character** Mara's voice is the chapter's greatest achievement. The dry, food-critic register is deployed with real discipline — it never feels like a gimmick because it's always grounded in her specific psychology. "Bacon that got weird about it. Good weird" is exactly the kind of thing a former bistro cook would say, and it lands as both funny and slightly horrifying. The tasting notes at the dinner table ("little smoky on the finish, better than the barn one, I think it's a freshness thing") feel earned rather than performed. The line "I'm seasoning dinner, there's a difference" / "Is there" / "There has to be, or I quit" is the best exchange in the chapter — it does real thematic work while feeling completely natural to these characters. Des's behavior is perfectly calibrated to his profile: the private vote, the shaking hands, the silence, the seconds. The model resists the temptation to give him a speech or a moment of explicit moral reckoning, which is exactly right. His cowardice (going out alone, coming back with the meat, saying nothing) is shown without being named. Oli's reaction — not stopping her fork, the deadpan "what'd it taste like," the impression of Mara narrating her own death — is pitch-perfect for the character as profiled. She's funnier and colder than expected, and the model delivers on that promise. **Structure and Pacing** The chapter's shape is smart. It doesn't follow a predictable scene-reflection-turn rhythm. The time compression ("three days later the beets ran out") is handled with a single paragraph that does a lot of work efficiently. The dinner scene has genuine warmth without sentimentality. The ending — the healed graze, the kitchen light, the going to bed — is exactly the right note: ambiguous, quiet, not dramatized. **The Body Horror Thread** The detail about the healed graze is handled with admirable restraint. "Not healing. Closed." is a good line — the distinction is meaningful and slightly chilling. The model correctly refuses to make anything of it in-scene, which is the plan's instruction and the right artistic choice. "The way you tell yourself all sorts of things at one in the morning that you would not say out loud at any other hour of the day" is a strong closing line that doesn't over-explain. **Weaknesses** The chapter is very good, but not without small issues. "Something in the room loosened by a degree" is slightly vague — it's the kind of atmospheric gesture the plan's ban list was meant to prevent, though it's mild. The phrase "something in my chest that I hadn't felt in months, that I didn't have a clean name for" is a touch close to the "she couldn't explain why" construction the plan explicitly banned, though it's saved somewhat by the specific follow-up about relief. The Oli impression of Mara narrating her own death ("notes: bitter, slightly stringy, improves with age") is very good but also slightly too neat — it's the kind of line that feels written rather than spoken, though it's close enough to Oli's established voice to survive scrutiny. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Des's reaction arc (furious-hungry-ashamed-eats), Oli's deadpan, the first full group meal, the warmth, the body-horror detail dropped without comment. The plan called for "real warmth here — first time they've laughed in the story" and the chapter delivers that without cloying. **Overall** This is confident, controlled writing that earns its tonal complexity. The dark comedy and the genuine warmth coexist without either undermining the other. The characters are consistent with their profiles, the dialogue is sharp and functional, and the horror thread is handled with exactly the right amount of restraint. Minor atmospheric vagueness and one or two slightly-too-neat lines keep it from being a complete masterpiece, but it's genuinely excellent work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6: Regulars By the fourth week it had a rhythm to it, the way anything does if you do it enough times, and that's the part I think would have horrified me most, back before, more than the eating itself — how quickly a rhythm forms around anything, given enough hunger and enough repetition. We built the pen in nine days, working around everything else, using fence panels from the far paddock and chicken wire and a gate Des welded shut himself, humming while he did it, some Merle Haggard song he didn't know he was humming. The idea, unspoken until it wasn't, was that walking two miles for a kill you might not get was worse math than keeping a few close, penned, fed on the kitchen scraps that used to just go in a compost heap nobody tended anymore. Livestock, though none of us used that word out loud for the first week. Des called it "the holding pen." I called it "the pantry," once, as a joke, and nobody laughed, and I didn't say it again, though I kept thinking it. There were three in there most of the time, sometimes four, slow-moving things Des lured down with a trail of half-rotten vegetables and the patience of a man who used to sit in a duck blind every October of his adult life. He'd gotten good at it. Better than he was at the other part, the part that still, four weeks in, fell to me every single time, a rotation of one that neither of us ever discussed reassigning. "Your turn," he'd say, some mornings, not meeting my eyes, and I'd taken to saying, "It's always my turn," not bitterly exactly, more like a tasting note, an observation offered without expectation of it changing anything, and it never did. We'd developed cuts. That's the word I used, in my head, cataloguing it the way I used to catalogue a menu — the shoulder meat, mild, best for stewing; the leaner leg cuts, better seared hot and fast; the cap material, still discarded every time, boiled off separately and buried, because even now, even hungry as we'd been, none of us wanted to find out what that part did. I kept a small spiral notebook, the kind you'd use for grocery lists, and filled two pages with actual recipes, actual measurements, a habit that felt, some days, like the last professional thing I had left, and other days felt like evidence I intended never to show anyone. Oli didn't help with the pen. That was understood without being stated. She fed Gary separately, at the north fence, still, every evening, a routine that had not stopped, had if anything intensified, now that there was an actual pen twenty yards away holding creatures she'd started calling, when she thought no one was listening, "the others," a phrase that drew a line I noticed and didn't examine too closely, because examining it meant admitting there was a line to draw at all. "He's not like them," she said once, catching me watching her walk back from the fence with an empty hand. "He still tilts his head. None of the pen ones do that anymore." "They probably did once." "That's not the same as doing it now." I let that sit, because I didn't have a rebuttal that wouldn't have cost more than it was worth. There were other things I noticed, that season, that I wrote down nowhere, not even the recipe notebook. That I'd started to enjoy the smell of the meat searing more than I enjoyed almost anything else in a day that had once been full of things to enjoy. That I'd had, twice now, a dream I couldn't fully hold onto in the morning, of being underground, threaded through with something, warm, unbothered, connected to a great many other warm unbothered things in a way that felt less like fear than like the plain fact of Tuesday. That the small hairs on my arms had a way of lying suspiciously flat these last weeks, as if there was less air moving over them than there used to be. That once, washing up, I caught a whiff of myself under the arm and thought, distinctly, before I could stop the thought: that's coming along nicely. I told no one. Not Des, whose sole responsibility for weeks had been fixing what was fixable and not-looking at what wasn't. Not Oli, who had a fence-post creature to worry about and did not need one more thing worrying about her. I told the notebook, instead, in a code even I wouldn't have called a code if you'd asked me — small margin notes next to certain recipes. Getting better at this one. Less trimming needed than I thought. Two words, once, next to a cut I'd taken to preparing almost raw, seared thirty seconds a side, which I ate alone in the pantry with the door shut: tastes right. I didn't examine that phrase either. There's only so much examining a person can do, in a day, before the examining itself starts to look like the thing you were afraid of finding.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably accomplished piece of writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses systematically. **Voice and Character:** Mara's voice is the chapter's greatest achievement. The dry, cataloguing intelligence — "a rotation of one that neither of us ever discussed reassigning," "not bitterly exactly, more like a tasting note" — is consistent with her established profile and feels genuinely inhabited rather than performed. The notebook detail, the recipes, the professional habit surviving the apocalypse: these are specific and earned. Des's cowardice is rendered through behavior rather than statement ("not meeting my eyes," "It's always my turn" going unrebutted), which is exactly right. Oli's line about the head-tilt — "That's not the same as doing it now" — is the best dialogue in the chapter, cold and precise in exactly the way her profile demands. **The Body Horror Thread:** The ambiguous transformation details are handled with genuine restraint and craft. "That's coming along nicely" is genuinely unsettling precisely because it's delivered in Mara's food-critic register — the horror is in the idiom, not in any explicit statement. The dream of being underground, "threaded through with something," is evocative without being overwrought. The flat hairs on her arms. These accumulate correctly, never tipping into confirmation or denial. **Structural Execution:** The chapter follows its plan closely — the time-skip, the pen as livestock operation, the domestic routine of hunting, the recipe notebook, Oli's continued separation of Gary from "the others," the body-change accumulation. The plan called for "dark domestic humor" and the chapter delivers it through the rotation-of-one joke and the "pantry" line that nobody laughs at. **Weaknesses:** The opening paragraph is slightly over-explained — "that's the part I think would have horrified me most, back before, more than the eating itself" tells us what to feel rather than letting the rhythm speak for itself. The chapter is almost entirely interiority and narration; the dialogue is sparse (three exchanges), which is appropriate for the chapter's design but means the "vary chapter shape" ambition isn't fully realized here. The Merle Haggard detail is good but the parenthetical "some Merle Haggard song he didn't know he was humming" slightly over-explains what was already clear from the profile. **Prose Quality:** The prose is controlled and specific throughout. "The patience of a man who used to sit in a duck blind every October of his adult life" is excellent — it characterizes Des economically while grounding the world. The metaphors are earned rather than decorative. There's no purple prose to speak of; the writing is lean and purposeful. **Minor Issues:** "There's only so much examining a person can do, in a day, before the examining itself starts to look like the thing you were afraid of finding" — this closing line is slightly too neat, a little too aphoristic, gesturing toward profundity in a way that the rest of the chapter wisely avoids. It's the one moment where the writing reaches for a bow when the plan explicitly called for flatness. **Dialogue:** Sparse but effective. The Oli exchange is the standout. Des's "Your turn" / Mara's "It's always my turn" is efficient. No weak dialogue to speak of, though there's not much to evaluate. Overall, this is a chapter that executes its plan with intelligence and restraint, maintains a distinctive and consistent voice, and handles its horror elements with genuine craft. The ambiguous transformation thread is particularly well-managed. Minor issues with the opening and closing lines, and the chapter leans heavily on interiority, but these are small complaints against a largely excellent piece. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7: Leftovers The winter came in hard the second week of what would have been November, and the pen went from three to two to one inside of nine days — not from us, from cold, from whatever internal weather those things ran on giving out the way anything gives out eventually, leaving Des to drag the stiff bodies to the trimming shed with an expression I didn't like, because it wasn't grief, exactly, and it wasn't relief either, it was closer to a man doing inventory on a business that was about to fail. "We're not going to make it through on one," he said, that morning, at the table, not looking at either of us. "Not with three people eating." "So we go out," I said. "Like before." "In this?" He tipped his head at the window, at the white coming down sideways. "Two miles in that, we don't come back." "Then we're short." "We're short either way." He said it flatly, an engineer's fact, no more weighted than the rest of his sentences that morning, and I should have heard the weight in it anyway, because I'd known him four months by then and I knew what his voice did right before he made a decision he wasn't going to discuss with anyone first. Oli was out at the fence when it happened. I know that because I watched her go, in her coat two sizes too big, carrying a heel of bread we genuinely could not spare, and I watched Des watch her go too, from the kitchen window, with his jaw working on the toothpick that hadn't existed in months, and I understood, standing there, some large slow part of me understanding a half second before the rest of me caught up, what he was about to do, and I said nothing. I want that on the record most of all. I said nothing. By the time Oli came back in, stamping snow off her boots, complaining about the cold in a way that was almost, blessedly, normal, Des was already in the shed. She noticed before I did — she always notices before I do, it's the one advantage of being fourteen and having nothing left to lose slowly, everything's already been lost all at once — her head coming up at some sound from outside, some particular sound, and she was out the door before I could stop her, and I went after her too late, the way you always go after things too late once they're already sounds instead of possibilities. The pen at the north fence was empty. Not empty like escaped. Empty like accounted for. Des was in the shed doorway with his hands red to the wrist, not meeting either of our eyes, and there was a wrapped bundle on the bench behind him that neither Oli nor I needed explained. "We were short," he said. His voice didn't shake. That was somehow the worst part — that he'd found his way, finally, to doing the thing he'd never once been able to do in four months, and the doing of it had cost him nothing visible at all, as if the only thing standing between Des and killing had never been the killing itself, just which one it was. Oli didn't scream. I'd braced for that, some part of me had already rehearsed how I'd hold her, and instead she went very quiet, quieter than I'd ever seen her, and said, in a voice with no heat in it whatsoever, "Which one." Des didn't answer. He didn't need to. "You knew he wasn't like the others," she said. Still to Des. Not raising her voice. "You said it yourself. You said he still tilted his head." "Oli—" "You said it yourself," she said again, the same flat register, and then she turned around and walked back to the house, and didn't slam the door, which somehow landed harder than slamming it would have. I stood in the shed with Des for a long moment, in the cold, the bundle on the bench between us like a held breath. "You should've told me," I said, finally. "Before. Not after." "Would you have stopped me?" I didn't answer that, because the honest answer was I didn't know, and the dishonest answer was the one he wanted, and I owed him neither that morning. "She's not going to forgive you," I said instead. "I know." "You don't sound like you know." "What do you want me to sound like, Mara." For the first time his voice did crack, just slightly, right at the edge. "We were short. I did the math. I've been doing the math every day since the beans ran out, and every day it comes out the same, and I finally did the thing you've been doing since the barn, and now everyone's furious at me for it instead of grateful, and I don't — " He stopped. Started again, quieter. "I don't know what I thought was going to happen." I looked at the bundle on the bench. I thought about a name tag, faded past reading, pinned crooked to a shirt that wasn't a shirt anymore. I thought about tilting heads, and waiting, and the difference between the two, that Oli had tried to explain to me once at a fence line, that I hadn't listened to closely enough. "I'll cook it," I said, because someone had to say something, and that was the only true thing left in the shed that morning.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. The writing plan called for the Gary betrayal to be presented "flatly, almost off-page," discovered after the fact, with Oli going "colder and stranger about it than expected," Des failing to apologize properly, and Mara being complicit by silence. The chapter delivers all of this with precision. The opening paragraph establishes the winter scarcity through a specific, concrete image — Des dragging stiff bodies to the trimming shed with an expression that "wasn't grief, exactly, and it wasn't relief either" — which is a genuinely good piece of characterization. The narrator's analytical distance from her own emotions is consistent with Mara's established coping mechanism. The central structural choice — Mara watching Des watch Oli leave, understanding what's about to happen and saying nothing — is the chapter's moral core, and it's handled with appropriate weight. "I said nothing. I want that on the record most of all." This is exactly the kind of complicity-by-silence the plan called for, and it lands because it's been seeded throughout the story's design. Oli's reaction is the chapter's strongest moment. She doesn't scream, she goes cold, she asks "Which one" in a voice with no heat. This is precisely what the character profile promised — the cold precision that "frightens Mara more than crying would." The detail about Des having said Gary "still tilted his head" is a small, devastating callback that feels earned. Des's breakdown in the shed is the chapter's most complex passage. His voice cracking, his confused resentment ("now everyone's furious at me for it instead of grateful"), his genuine bewilderment — this is the character profile's "ugly cowardly streak" rendered with more sympathy than condemnation, which is the right call. The observation that he finally managed to kill something, but only because it was Gary, is quietly devastating: "as if the only thing standing between Des and killing had never been the killing itself, just which one it was." The ending — "I'll cook it" — is appropriately bleak and consistent with Mara's character: competence as moral alibi, doing the necessary thing as a way of not having to feel it. Some minor criticisms: The phrase "like a held breath" for the bundle on the bench is a slightly worn metaphor. The line "the way you always go after things too late once they're already sounds instead of possibilities" is the chapter's most strained moment — it reaches for profundity and lands somewhere between insight and obscurity. The parenthetical about Oli noticing things first because she's fourteen and "everything's already been lost all at once" is a bit over-explained; the observation would be stronger without the justification. The dialogue is largely excellent — spare, character-specific, doing real work. Des's "What do you want me to sound like, Mara" is particularly good. The chapter avoids the trap of having characters articulate their emotional states too cleanly. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the betrayal is off-page, Oli's reaction is cold rather than explosive, Des's non-apology is rendered accurately, and Mara's complicity is foregrounded. The body-horror thread (Mara's ambiguous changes) is notably absent from this chapter, which is consistent with the plan's instruction to keep it as background accumulation rather than foregrounded revelation. The darkness is well-earned throughout — nothing feels manufactured or manipulative. The chapter's restraint is its greatest strength. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8: Sunday, Maybe Oli came down for dinner. That was the surprise of it — not that she came down, exactly, we all still had to eat and there was nowhere else in the house to do it, but that she sat at her usual chair, in her usual place, and didn't say anything about which chair anyone should or shouldn't be sitting in, the way you'd expect from someone who'd drawn a line in the snow that morning. I'd cooked it plain. No thyme this time. It felt wrong to season it, though I couldn't have told you exactly whose sense of wrong I was honoring — mine, or hers, or some rule that didn't belong to either of us but had shown up in the kitchen anyway and asked to be observed. Des didn't sit down right away. He stood by the stove longer than he needed to, doing something to the pan that didn't need doing, and I understood he was giving Oli the chance to say something first, to set the terms, and I understood too that he was giving himself one last minute where he hadn't yet had to watch her decide. She served herself. That was the thing none of us said out loud afterward, though I know all three of us have turned it over since, alone, in the dark, the way you turn over the one detail from a bad day that refuses to sit still. She took a portion. Not a small one, either. She ate it the way she eats everything now, fast, business-like, no ceremony, and about halfway through she said, to no one in particular, in a voice with no heat left in it at all: "He would've eaten if it was one of us instead. That's not better. I know that's not better. I just keep thinking it." Nobody answered that, because there wasn't an answer that would have made it smaller. Des reached over at one point and put his hand briefly on the table near hers, not touching, just near, the way he does instead of the things he doesn't know how to say, and she didn't pull away from it, which I took, that night, as the whole of the forgiveness we were going to get, doled out in inches, over years probably, if we had years, which none of us had any way of knowing. I gave a tasting note. Old habit, worn smooth by now into something closer to liturgy than commentary. "Notes," I said, to the table, to the room, to whatever was left of the person I used to be who thought this was a funny thing to do. "Bitter. Improves with hunger." Nobody laughed. I hadn't expected them to. I think I said it mostly to hear whether the joke still worked at all, the way you test a knife against your thumb, gently, to see if it's still got an edge, and it didn't, not tonight, and I filed that away too, one more entry in a ledger only I was keeping. Later, doing the dishes alone, because Des had gone up early and Oli had gone to sit at the dark window the way she does some nights now instead of sleeping, I caught the smell of myself again, that same faint mineral warmth under my own arm, the one I'd first noticed weeks ago and never mentioned to either of them, and I stood very still at the sink for a while, hands in water gone cold, deciding, again, not to think too hard about it. I thought instead about the fence, and the name tag, and the particular slowness of something that waits for you because it's learned, somehow, that you come back — and I thought that maybe the worst thing about all of this wasn't the eating, in the end. It was how ordinary it had become to sit at a table and pass a dish around and ask, mildly, could you pass the salt, when the thing on the plate used to have a name someone gave it out of love, out of loneliness, out of the same impulse that had once made a fourteen-year-old drag home every dying stray within five miles, because it was the last good thing left to do, and doing it had cost her the only good thing she had left. Outside, past the dark glass, the snow was still coming down sideways, and somewhere under it, I knew without checking, the pen sat empty, waiting the way pens do, patient, for whatever we decided to put in it next, come spring, if spring came, if we were still three by then, if being three still meant what it used to mean back when the word was simple enough to say without flinching. I finished the dishes. I did not go check on Oli at the window. I did not go check my arm under the good light, the way I'd been doing most nights since the fall, to see whether the skin along my forearm had started, in that spot, to look faintly, impossibly, greener than skin has any business looking. I went to bed instead, and lay in the dark of what used to be Carol's sewing room, listening to the house settle and the wind work at the shutters, and thought, right before sleep took me under: *we'll need more food by March.* And underneath that thought, quieter, unbidden, arriving the way hunger arrives, without asking permission first: *good.*
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through it carefully. **Strengths:** The chapter executes its central emotional task with considerable skill. The detail of Oli serving herself — "not a small one, either" — is exactly the kind of specific, unexpected, devastating choice the plan called for. It's the truest and worst detail, as the plan specified, and it lands without being telegraphed. The writing earns this moment. Oli's line — "He would've eaten if it was one of us instead. That's not better. I know that's not better. I just keep thinking it." — is excellent dialogue. It's cold, precise, and entirely consistent with her character profile's description of how she grieves: not with heat but with strange, uncomfortable logic. This is the "colder and stranger than expected" reaction the plan promised. Des's gesture of putting his hand near but not touching Oli's is perfectly calibrated to his character — expressing love through proximity and maintenance rather than words. This is consistent with his profile ("loves people by fixing their gutters") and executed without over-explanation. The tasting note callback — "Bitter. Improves with hunger." — lands as intended: not funny, not meant to be funny, testing whether the joke still works and finding it doesn't. The plan called for "a joke that doesn't land right at the very end" and this delivers that with self-awareness baked into the narration. The ambiguous body horror ending — the green skin, the final "good" — is handled with appropriate restraint. The plan specified this should remain unconfirmed and unresolved, and it does. The final word "good" is genuinely unsettling because it's ambiguous: is it hunger speaking, or something else that has taken up residence in Mara? **Weaknesses and concerns:** The prose occasionally tips into over-elaboration. The paragraph beginning "I thought instead about the fence, and the name tag..." runs long and becomes somewhat self-conscious in its accumulation of clauses. The plan called for "no big speeches" and this paragraph, while not a speech, has a speechifying quality — it's Mara explaining the theme to herself (and the reader) rather than simply experiencing it. "because it was the last good thing left to do, and doing it had cost her the only good thing she had left" is the kind of neat thematic summation that slightly undercuts the flatness the chapter otherwise achieves. Similarly, the penultimate long paragraph about the empty pen and spring has a slightly purple quality — "patient, for whatever we decided to put in it next, come spring, if spring came, if we were still three by then, if being three still meant what it used to mean back when the word was simple enough to say without flinching." The anaphoric "if" construction is effective but the final clause ("simple enough to say without flinching") is a little too polished, too writerly, for Mara's voice as established. The opening paragraph's "the way you'd expect from someone who'd drawn a line in the snow that morning" is slightly awkward — it references an event we haven't seen (the confrontation presumably happened earlier in the day) and the phrasing "drawn a line in the snow" is a cliché that doesn't quite fit the otherwise fresh language. The chapter is slightly longer than the plan's "quieter, unresolved" tone might suggest. The plan called for "no big speeches" and while there are no speeches per se, the reflective passages accumulate into something that approaches speechifying. **Consistency with plan and profiles:** The chapter follows its plan closely. The "Oli eats too" detail is present and handled correctly. The tasting note turning on herself is present. The body horror ambiguity is maintained. Des's non-apology is rendered through gesture rather than words, consistent with his profile. Mara's cataloguing-instead-of-confronting flaw is present in her decision not to check on Oli, not to examine her arm. The "cozy-horror" register is maintained — there's warmth in the domestic details (dishes, the sewing room, the wind at shutters) alongside genuine darkness. **Overall assessment:** This is a strong, largely successful chapter that executes a difficult tonal task — grief, complicity, dark humor that doesn't land, ambiguous body horror — with considerable skill. Its weaknesses are real but minor: occasional over-elaboration, one or two moments where the prose becomes too consciously literary. The dialogue is sparse but effective. The emotional beats are earned rather than manufactured. The ending is genuinely strange and unsettling in the right way. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of post-apocalyptic horror-adjacent fiction that takes the prompt's darkly comic premise and executes it with genuine literary sophistication. Let me work through the various dimensions carefully. VOICE AND STYLE: The first-person narrator, Mara, is established immediately as a former professional cook, and this identity is used with consistent, disciplined craft throughout. The tasting-notes motif never feels forced because it's rooted in character psychology — it's her way of maintaining professional identity and emotional distance simultaneously. The voice is dry, precise, and genuinely funny without ever undercutting the horror. "Four cans of black beans. One can of cream corn, dented, which I've been saving the way you save a bad card in poker, hoping it improves by proximity to better ones" — this is excellent prose. The humor lands because it's earned through specificity and character logic. CHARACTER: The three characters are genuinely distinct and consistently rendered. Des's inability to kill, his eventual crossing of that line with Gary, and the specific way his shame manifests (doing the thing finally, but doing it wrong, doing it to the one thing that mattered to Oli) — this is sophisticated character work. Oli's arc from window-watcher to Gary's protector to the devastating moment where she serves herself at dinner is handled with real restraint. The author wisely avoids melodrama at every turn. Mara's gradual transformation — the healing wound, the smell, the final "good" — is seeded carefully and lands with genuine dread. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the piece's strongest elements. It's spare, character-specific, and does real work. "He looks like a Gary." "You gave a can of beans a personality yesterday." The exchanges feel lived-in rather than constructed. Des's "I'll gut it. I'll skin it, I'll cook the damn thing three ways from Sunday if it comes to that. I'm not going to be the one to put it down" is a perfect character revelation delivered naturally. PACING: The pacing is excellent. Each chapter advances the situation while deepening character. The escalation from inventory to foraging to eating to the Gary chapter is well-managed. The horror elements are introduced gradually and never overwhelm the domestic texture that makes the piece work. WORLD BUILDING: The Bloomers are economically sketched — enough detail to be vivid (the wrong-standing, the cap growths, the sweet smell) without over-explanation. The farmhouse setting feels real. The world's collapse is implied rather than explained, which is the right choice. THE GARY CHAPTER: Chapter 7 is the emotional and moral center of the piece, and it's handled with real skill. Des's decision is believable — he's been doing the math, he finally found his way to killing, and the specific cruelty is that he chose the one that mattered. Oli's response — the flat voice, the serving herself at dinner, the "he would've eaten if it was one of us" — is devastating and earned. THE TRANSFORMATION: The ending's implication that Mara is becoming something is seeded throughout (the healing wound, the smell, the dreams, the "tastes right" note) and the final "good" lands with genuine horror precisely because it's so quiet. This is well-executed body horror. WEAKNESSES: There are occasional moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard. "patient in the specific horrible way of things that don't understand time is passing at all" is good, but the piece has a few instances where the parenthetical qualifications and the "the way you X" constructions become slightly repetitive as a stylistic tic. The "way you" construction appears perhaps a dozen times and while it's effective, it becomes noticeable. Some of the chapter-ending observations are slightly over-telegraphed — the final paragraphs of several chapters reach for profundity in ways that are mostly earned but occasionally tip into the slightly too-neat. The piece is not amateurish at all. The metaphors generally land. The darkness is well-earned. The lightness (the tasting notes, the jokes) is genuinely light and genuinely funny, which makes the darkness darker. PROMPT FAITHFULNESS: The piece is entirely faithful to the prompt — cozy-horror post-apocalypse survival, the discovery that the spore mutants are delicious, the desperate eating of them. It adds the transformation element which deepens the horror considerably. OVERALL: This is a genuinely excellent piece of short fiction. It has a distinctive voice, consistent characters, earned emotional beats, and a horror that works because it's grounded in the mundane. The main weaknesses are minor stylistic repetitions and occasional over-reaching in chapter endings. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Pacing: 17 World Building: 16 Compelling Plot: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19
